report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared — an 83-year-old living in a family member's home in Klickitat County, needing help with transferring, diagnosed with dementia, covered by Medicare, with monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999 and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — the screening flagged five programs that may be worth a closer look. However, several key pieces of information are still missing (such as current Medicaid enrollment, the full number of daily activities needing help, whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, and any recent hospital stay), so this screening is only a starting point. None of these results are a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency for each program can decide that. The follow-up questions below would help sharpen which of these programs are most worth pursuing with a professional (such as an Area Agency on Aging counselor or HCS office).

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home (not a nursing facility), and has an indicated care need (help with transferring, dementia), which lines up with this benefit's basic requirements — all three of the rules engine's checks passed.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help with walking and bathing) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a doctor or nurse practitioner must certify a need for part-time skilled care and 'homebound' status, and the care must be ordered and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care at home or custodial/personal care alone if that is the only care needed — it must be paired with a skilled care need.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor whether a face-to-face assessment and referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency would be appropriate.
Ask the doctor or agency to explain, in writing, what would and would not be covered before services begin.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 83, lives in a family member's home, needs help with transferring, and has a dementia diagnosis — the kind of support need this program is built around. However, whether they are enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Medicaid, and whether they need help with three or more daily activities on an ongoing basis, was not answered, so this can't be confirmed as a strong match yet.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid option for funding personal care for people who need a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, and it requires meeting Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid financial rules in the community (not institutional rules).
To keep receiving these services at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities.

Covered services include help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, and eating, plus things like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, delivered in settings including a family member's home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP coverage)
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Contact the local Home and Community Services (HCS) office to ask about a CARE functional assessment and Medicaid financial screening.
Check current Apple Health enrollment status, or apply if not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. But this coverage only applies after a specific kind of recent hospital stay, and that information wasn't provided, so it's unclear whether this benefit is currently relevant.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status does not count) and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit, and costs increase over time within a benefit period, with the patient paying all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and when it occurred
nextSteps: If a hospital stay is being considered or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify as a 3-day inpatient stay under Medicare rules.
If no recent qualifying hospital stay applies, ask about alternatives like Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is over 55, and the stated income (between $1,000 and $2,999/month) and assets (between $2,000 and $85,000) fall within this program's higher financial limits. However, whether the person needs a nursing-facility level of care (based on activities of daily living) was not confirmed, and the program currently has a statewide enrollment pause worth knowing about upfront.

whatItCovers.text: This program supports people 55 and older who need a nursing-facility level of care but are not on classic Medicaid, with financial limits well above regular Medicaid, and does not require Medicaid enrollment — people on Medically Needy or Medicare Savings Program coverage may still be considered.
Eligibility requires meeting a nursing-facility level-of-care standard, income at or below $3,976/month, and resources at or below $84,354 (single) as of 1/1/2026.

As of the information gathered, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments happen only once called from the waitlist.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person's care needs meet a nursing-facility level of care (based on how many daily activities require help)
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist process.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds in the meantime, since those don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person appears to need ongoing care or supervision given the dementia diagnosis and help needed with transferring, which matches part of what these respite programs are meant to support. Whether an unpaid family caregiver is providing regular care — a key eligibility factor for these programs — was not stated.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies statewide.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility — plus support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.
These programs generally are for unpaid caregivers of adults who need care, and eligibility and service levels are assessed locally (for example, through a TCARE screening).

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing regular (for example, 40+ hours/week) care to the person
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP respite options and a caregiver screening.
If an unpaid caregiver is providing 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers via wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: Is the 83-year-old currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, under what category (Categorically Needy, Alternative Benefit Plan, Medically Needy, or a Medicare Savings Program)?
How many daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, walking) does the person need help with, and is that help partial or full?
Is there an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care (for example, 40 or more hours a week), and if so, who?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past, and if so, when?
Is the person currently receiving any skilled nursing, therapy, or home health services, or has a doctor recommended any?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a 69-year-old family member in Franklin County, Washington, who lives in a relative's home, needs help with bathing and toileting, is married, has monthly income under $1,000, has countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and has Medicare coverage. Based on those facts, several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look with the right agency, especially Medicare's home health benefit and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA), where the rules engine found a match on every checked factor. Others, like the Medicaid COPES waiver, Apple Health long-term care, and Community First Choice, could also be worth investigating, but some key details — especially the exact countable asset amount and current Medicaid enrollment status — are still unknown and would change how well those programs fit. A few programs (Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit and respite care programs) depend on information (a recent hospital stay, or whether there's an unpaid caregiver) that wasn't provided. None of this is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency can make that call — but it gives a starting point for conversations with HCS, the local Area Agency on Aging, and Medicare.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home (in a family member's home rather than a nursing facility), and needs help with bathing and toileting — needs that fall within what Medicare's home health aide benefit can support when paired with skilled care. All the screening's checked factors passed, and no additional information is currently listed as missing.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and walking, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — not for personal care alone.

To qualify, a person needs a face-to-face assessment and provider certification of homebound status and a need for skilled care, plus a provider order and care plan carried out by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket for the beneficiary; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor would certify a need for skilled nursing or therapy care alongside the personal care needs
Whether the person meets Medicare's 'homebound' definition (difficulty leaving home without help)
nextSteps: Ask the family member's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County from the doctor's office

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is 69 (well above the 55+ threshold), reported monthly income under $1,000 is far below TSOA's $3,976 limit, and countable assets in the $2,000–$85,000 range fit comfortably within TSOA's resource limits ($84,354 single / $156,883 with a community spouse). Needing help with bathing and toileting points toward the kind of nursing-facility-level care need TSOA is designed around, and all the rules engine's checked factors passed with no missing data.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic categorically needy/ABP Medicaid, including those without an unpaid caregiver; it comes with no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to some Medicaid long-term care programs.

As of the most recent update, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide (since December 1, 2025), so new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than processed immediately; people already enrolled keep their services.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org, the HCA 18-008 paper form, or a local Area Agency on Aging to be placed on the statewide waitlist
While waiting, ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is 69, which meets the 65-and-older age pathway for COPES, and reported income under $1,000/month is well within the program's Special Income Level of $2,982/month. Needing help with bathing and toileting points toward the kind of ADL needs COPES looks at, though the full assessment needs more detail. Whether countable assets (reported broadly as $2,000–$85,000) fall within the program's $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple) resource limit is not yet known, and current Medicaid enrollment status is also unknown.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds waiver services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, and transportation, delivered in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To be considered, a person generally must be 65+ (or 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care, have income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), and have countable resources at or below $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple), with higher protections for a spouse still living in the community.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount, to compare against the program's resource limit
Current enrollment status in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
More detail on marital/spousal asset arrangements, since a spouse's resources can be treated differently
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit (or check on) the HCA 18-005 financial application via washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Ask HCS how spousal income and resource rules would apply given the marriage

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with bathing and toileting suggests some personal-care needs that Community First Choice is designed to address, and Medicare coverage was reported. However, CFC generally requires an unmet or partially met need in three or more ADLs to continue receiving services, and only two specific ADLs (bathing, toileting) were mentioned. Current Medicaid enrollment status, which is required for CFC financial eligibility, is also unknown.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (like bathing, toileting, dressing, eating, transferring) and, when ADL help is also needed, certain IADLs such as meal preparation and housework, plus relief care, nurse delegation, and assistive technology, in settings like the home, adult family home, or assisted living.

Financial eligibility generally requires being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid in the community, and continuing CFC requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs at reassessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the family member is currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many ADLs, beyond bathing and toileting, require assistance
nextSteps: Request a CARE assessment through HCS central intake to get a fuller picture of ADL needs
If not already on Apple Health, ask HCS about applying for community Medicaid coverage

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 69 and the reported income under $1,000/month both fit within this program's general age and income guidelines, and needing help with bathing and toileting points toward possible nursing-facility-level care needs, which this program's functional assessment considers. However, whether the countable assets (in the $2,000–$85,000 range) fall within the resource limit, and whether Medicaid is already in place, are both unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is the Medicaid coverage that serves as the financial gateway to nursing facility care and HCS waiver programs like COPES, plus community LTSS more broadly.

Eligibility generally involves meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based criteria, an income test (Special Income Level of $2,982/month, or a Medically Needy pathway if above that), and, for SSI-related coverage, a resource limit of $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple), with spousal protections available.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005, online at washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax/in person) plus a functional CARE assessment arranged through regional HCS intake lines.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount compared to the program's resource limits
Whether Medicaid coverage is already in place
Confirmation of how marital status affects the resource limit calculation
nextSteps: Apply via washingtonconnection.org or submit HCA 18-005 to HCS-LTSS
Call the regional HCS intake line for the area to schedule a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage was reported, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and whether such a recent hospital stay occurred is not known from the information given.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing facility care — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and medical supplies — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
This is described as a short-term skilled and rehabilitative benefit, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
Whether a doctor has determined a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care following such a stay
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage
If custodial (non-skilled) care is the main need, ask about Medicaid or other long-term care options instead

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported need for help with bathing and toileting suggests ongoing care or supervision needs, which is one factor these respite programs consider. However, whether there is currently an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care — and how many hours per week — is not known, and that detail affects which respite options might fit.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, available to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care who cannot afford respite on their own and aren't already receiving respite through another program.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), run through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day, or residential), plus support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with service levels tailored through a caregiver screening; amounts vary by county and there is no published statewide cap.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family member or friend is currently providing regular care, and about how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options for Franklin County

followUpQuestions: What is the exact countable asset amount, more precisely than the $2,000–$85,000 range given?
Is the family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, under what category?
Besides bathing and toileting, are there other daily activities (like eating, dressing, transferring, or mobility) that require help?
Is there an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past?
Does leaving home require help or is it generally not recommended because of a health condition (homebound status)?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what's known so far — an 87-year-old living in Adams County, WA, with a stroke diagnosis, needing help with toileting, married, on Medicare, with income between $1,000-$2,999/month and countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. However, many of the key details needed to know how strongly each program might apply are still missing, especially how many daily activities require help, the exact monthly income, whether there's a family caregiver, and any recent hospital stays. Because so many of these details are unknown, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask an official agency or a family caregiver specialist, rather than a set of firm leads. No agency has reviewed this information, and only the administering agency for each program can determine actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, has an indicated care need (help with toileting related to a stroke), and lives at home rather than in a nursing facility — all matching what this benefit looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical or occupational therapy, and home health aide help (such as help walking, bathing, and grooming) at no cost to the beneficiary for covered services, when ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.

To qualify, the person must be considered 'homebound' — generally having trouble leaving home without help due to illness or injury — and need only part-time or intermittent skilled care rather than around-the-clock help.

nextSteps: Ask the primary doctor whether a face-to-face homebound assessment and home health referral would be appropriate
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 87 (meeting the 65+ age rule) and needs help with toileting due to a stroke, which is one of the activities COPES considers when checking for a nursing-facility level of care. Countable assets under $2,000 fit within the program's resource limit. Whether the income (reported as $1,000-$2,999/month) falls under the program's Special Income Level, and how many total daily activities require help, are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing services, transportation, environmental modifications, and specialized medical equipment, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To use COPES, an assessment must show the person needs nursing-facility-level care, which can include an unmet or partially met need in three or more daily activities such as toileting, bathing, or transferring, or two or more at a higher level of assistance.

COPES is generally available to people 65 and older, and financial rules look at both income (a special limit of $2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and resources ($2,000 for a single person, $3,000 for a couple).

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, or transferring) the person needs help with, beyond toileting
Confirmation of the current living situation for this assessment
The exact monthly income amount, since the reported range spans both sides of a key Medicaid income limit
nextSteps: Contact the local Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, or by mail/fax
Ask about the Acknowledgment of Services form (DSHS 14-225) and any asset transfer history that may need to be documented

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs help with toileting, which is one of the daily activities CFC covers, and has Medicare coverage but current Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment status is not yet known. Whether the total number of daily activities needing help meets the program's threshold is also unclear.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for help with daily activities such as toileting, bathing, dressing, eating, and transferring, plus household tasks like meal preparation and shopping, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care.

To qualify, a person generally must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not institutional rules), and to continue services at reassessment must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Ask HCS about scheduling a CARE assessment to check functional eligibility
Confirm current Medicaid enrollment status with HCA or DSHS

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 87, meeting the age requirement, and has countable assets under $2,000, fitting the program's resource limit. This program is the financial gateway to services like COPES. Whether the income fits within the Special Income Level and how much help is needed with daily activities are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is the Medicaid coverage that acts as the financial gateway to home-and-community waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care.

Financial eligibility considers income against a Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026) and a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person, with additional protections for a spouse still living at home.
The application process includes a financial application plus a required financial interview and an in-person CARE assessment by a Home and Community Services social worker, generally within 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities the person needs help with
Confirmation of current living situation for this assessment
The exact monthly income amount
nextSteps: Call the regional HCS central intake line to schedule a CARE assessment
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application online, by mail, or in person

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A coverage, but whether there has been a recent qualifying hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days is not yet known, and this is required for this benefit to apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term nursing home care — a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days as an admitted inpatient.
Costs in 2026 include $0 per day for days 1–20, $217 per day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient recently
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, confirm with hospital staff whether it is classified as 'inpatient' (not observation) for at least 3 days
Ask hospital discharge planners whether Medicare SNF coverage would apply

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported income and assets appear to fall within TSOA's higher financial limits, and the person is well above the program's age-55 threshold. However, whether the level of help needed (currently just toileting) meets the program's nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement is not yet known, and the program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is meant for people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but have income and resources above regular Medicaid limits, and it can also support unpaid family caregivers.

As of the most recent update, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide since December 1, 2025, and new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving services right away; people already enrolled keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities the person needs help with, to see whether it meets the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement
nextSteps: Contact the Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current statewide waitlist
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting, since TSOA enrollment is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs ongoing help due to a stroke and toileting needs, and is married, which raises the possibility of an unpaid spousal caregiver — but whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care has not been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short breaks for unpaid family caregivers, such as a spouse, through options like Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) or the Family Caregiver Support Program, which can pay for in-home respite, adult day care, or short residential stays.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver (such as a spouse) providing regular care, and how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers or the Family Caregiver Support Program
Clarify whether the spouse or another unpaid caregiver is providing regular care, since this affects which respite option may apply

followUpQuestions: Besides help with toileting, does the person need help with other daily activities, such as bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, or walking?
What is the exact monthly household income (a specific number, rather than the $1,000-$2,999 range)?
Does the stroke diagnosis cause any memory, confusion, or wandering issues that require supervision?
Is there an unpaid family caregiver (such as the spouse) providing regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient recently?
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application been submitted?
Is the current living situation the person's own home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility?

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared — a 71-year-old Wahkiakum County resident living at home with dementia, needing help with transferring, bathing, eating, and dressing, on a low monthly income, with Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) — several Washington programs look worth a closer look with an official caseworker. The strongest matches are Medicaid Community First Choice (personal care already tied to her existing Apple Health coverage) and the Medicare home health benefit (skilled care at home). A few other programs, including the COPES waiver and Apple Health long-term care, may also be worth exploring but depend on her exact countable asset amount and Medicaid coverage category, which weren't specified. Two programs — Tailored Supports for Older Adults and Medicaid Alternative Care — are currently paused for new enrollment statewide (waitlist only since December 1, 2025), and some programs depend on whether she has an unpaid family caregiver, which wasn't stated. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agencies (DSHS/HCS, HCA, or Medicare) can determine that after a full application and assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is already covered by Apple Health (Medicaid) and needs help with four daily activities — transferring, bathing, eating, and dressing — which meets the program's requirement of needing help with three or more ADLs.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and transferring, plus services such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and training on managing personal care providers.

CFC is Washington's main way of providing Medicaid-funded personal care to people who need a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, administered through Home and Community Services.

nextSteps: Because she's already on Apple Health, contact HCS to request a CARE assessment rather than a new financial application
Discuss care setting and provider preferences with an HCS or Area Agency on Aging worker

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is enrolled in Medicare and has documented needs for help with daily activities and dementia-related care, and she lives at home — matching the basic setup for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with things like bathing and walking — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also part of the care plan.
To qualify, a doctor must do a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask her doctor for a face-to-face visit to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Wahkiakum County

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is over 55, already enrolled in Apple Health, lives at home, and has needs consistent with a nursing-facility level of care. Whether she has an unpaid family caregiver — which this program specifically supports — was not reported, and enrollment is currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: MAC provides funded support for the unpaid caregiver of someone who is 55+, already on Apple Health, living at home, and meeting nursing-facility level of care — offered in steps, such as a one-time $250, then up to $500/year, then roughly $928/month, for things like respite care, housekeeping, and adult day care.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver (age 18 or older) who regularly helps with her care
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging / Community Living Connections to ask about the waitlist and screening process

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is 71, lives in her own home, and needs help with transferring, bathing, eating, and dressing along with a dementia diagnosis — the kind of ADL and cognitive-support needs COPES eligibility looks at. Her income under $1,000/month is well within the program's income threshold. Her exact countable assets (reported only as somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000) and current Medicaid category weren't specified, so the resource test can't be checked from this information alone.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home and community-based services on top of personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.
It is Washington's largest program of this type, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and most clients get it alongside Community First Choice personal care.

To be considered, an assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care need — which can be shown through needing help in several daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and transferring, or through cognitive impairment such as dementia that requires supervision plus an ADL need.

informationStillNeeded: Her exact countable asset amount (not just the $2,000–$85,000 range)
Her marital status confirmation for resource-limit purposes
Confirmation of her specific Medicaid coverage category
nextSteps: Submit (or confirm) the HCS financial application (form HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is 71, has ADL needs (transferring, bathing, eating, dressing) and dementia, and reports income under $1,000/month, which fits within this program's income standards. Her exact assets and Medicaid coverage category weren't confirmed, so the full picture isn't clear yet.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial gateway into programs like COPES.

Financial eligibility compares income to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and resources to the SSI standard of $2,000 for a single person, with a home equity limit of $1,130,000.
Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview and an in-person CARE assessment; the standard processing time is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: Her exact countable asset amount
Her marital status confirmation
Confirmation of her specific Medicaid coverage category
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org, or by paper form HCA 18-005, mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office
Call the regional HCS intake number to arrange the CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is enrolled in Medicare, but whether she has had a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay — a requirement for this benefit — was not reported, so it's unclear whether this short-term rehab benefit currently applies to her situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehab care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (observation status doesn't count).
Covered items include the room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, and medications used during the stay; days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance of $217 (2026).

informationStillNeeded: Whether she's had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If there's been a recent hospital stay, ask the hospital discharge team whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay
If no recent hospital stay, ask about other options such as Medicare home health or Medicaid LTSS instead

programs.whyThisMayApply: Her age (71) and reported income/assets appear to fall within TSOA's higher financial limits, and her ADL and dementia-related needs align with the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement. However, she already has Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage, and TSOA specifically excludes people on Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid — her exact coverage category wasn't specified. TSOA enrollment is also currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid; a person on Medically Needy or Medicare Savings Program coverage may still qualify, and its income/resource limits are higher than regular Medicaid's.

As of December 1, 2025, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide — new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving services right away, and there's no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether her current Apple Health coverage is Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan or a different category like Medically Needy or a Medicare Savings Program
nextSteps: Ask HCS or the Area Agency on Aging to clarify her specific Medicaid coverage category
If interested, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) about statewide waitlist placement

programs.whyThisMayApply: Her dementia diagnosis and need for help with several daily activities suggest an ongoing care or supervision need, but the profile doesn't mention whether an unpaid caregiver is currently providing that care — which these programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Respite options include Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP, amount varies by county), which provide short-term relief care delivered in-home, through adult day services, or in a residential care setting.
Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40+ hours per week of care, while FCSP eligibility and service levels vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss whether a caregiver in her life could apply for respite support

followUpQuestions: What is her exact countable asset total (the profile only gave a broad range of $2,000–$85,000)? Programs like COPES and Apple Health LTC use a strict $2,000 single-person resource limit.

Is her current Apple Health coverage Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan, or a different category like Medically Needy or a Medicare Savings Program? This affects TSOA eligibility specifically.
Does she have an unpaid family member or friend regularly helping with her care (and if so, roughly how many hours per week)? This matters for Medicaid Alternative Care and respite programs.
Has she had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently? This matters for Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage.
Has a CARE assessment (by an HCS social worker) already been done to document her level of care need?

Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what's shared so far — a 92-year-old in Franklin County living at home, with a stroke diagnosis, help needed with transferring, income under $1,000/month, assets under $2,000, and current Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage — several Washington programs look worth a closer look. The income and asset figures line up with what Medicaid long-term-care programs typically check, and being on Medicare opens the door to Medicare's home health benefit. That said, a few important pieces are still missing, like how many daily activities need help (beyond transferring), whether a formal care-needs (CARE) assessment has happened, whether there's an unpaid caregiver, and the exact Apple Health coverage category. Two programs (TSOA and MAC) also have statewide enrollment pauses right now. This screening only flags programs that may be worth investigating with the actual agencies — it cannot determine eligibility or guarantee any benefit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home, and has a stroke diagnosis along with a stated need for help transferring — details that line up with the kind of skilled, part-time home care this Medicare benefit is built to cover.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical and occupational therapy, and home health aide help (such as help with walking, bathing, or grooming) when paired with skilled care — at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To use this benefit, a doctor must certify homebound status and order the care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment for home health certification
Ask the doctor for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 92 years old with income under $1,000/month and countable assets under $2,000, this profile fits within the age and financial figures COPES looks at. The stated need for help with transferring and the stroke diagnosis point toward a possible nursing-facility-level care need, which is the piece an official assessment would need to confirm.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra in-home and community services on top of basic personal care — things like adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing — for people whose needs go beyond what basic personal care provides.
Services can be used in a private home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and they are meant to add to — not replace — what Medicare, Medicaid, or other insurance already covers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an official CARE assessment has found a nursing-facility level of care is needed (for example, help with 3 or more daily activities such as bathing, transferring, or toileting)
More detail about the current living situation (own home, adult family home, or assisted living)
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake line to request a CARE assessment
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through Washington Connection, or by mail/fax, if one hasn't already been filed
Ask HCS about the documentation needed for the 5-year look-back on any past asset transfers

programs.whyThisMayApply: Because this person already has Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage and needs help with transferring, Community First Choice — Washington's Medicaid personal-care benefit — may be worth checking into. It isn't yet known whether the number of daily activities needing help meets the 3-ADL level generally used to continue CFC over time.

whatItCovers.text: CFC personal care can include help with transferring, bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and other daily activities, along with supports like personal emergency response systems and training for family caregivers on managing care providers.
It can be used at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living, and it does not replace Medicare, other insurance, or informal help already in place.

informationStillNeeded: Whether help is needed with 3 or more daily activities (ADLs), which is used to determine ongoing CFC eligibility at reassessment
nextSteps: Ask HCS for a CARE functional assessment to establish current ADL needs
Since Apple Health coverage is already in place, ask HCS whether only the functional assessment (not a new financial application) is needed

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial doorway to several of Washington's home-care Medicaid benefits. With income under $1,000/month, assets under $2,000, and age 92, the financial pieces line up with what this program checks, though the functional (care-needs) piece hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care, and it also serves as the gateway to home-based programs like COPES.
Full Apple Health medical benefits continue alongside long-term-care coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a CARE assessment has confirmed a nursing-facility level of care
More detail about the current living situation
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org, or submit the paper HCA 18-005 form by mail or fax
Call the regional HCS intake line covering Franklin County to schedule the required CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit could become relevant if there is ever a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days followed by a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care. It isn't yet known whether a recent qualifying hospital stay has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period, including nursing care, therapy, meals, and medical supplies — but only after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
This benefit is meant for short-term skilled and rehabilitation care, not ongoing custodial or long-term care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay happens, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage
Keep a record of any hospital admission and discharge dates for future reference

programs.whyThisMayApply: TSOA generally isn't available to people already on Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Apple Health coverage, and this family already has Apple Health (Medicaid). The exact coverage category isn't confirmed, and TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, so this program may not be an active option right now.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need a nursing-facility level of care but are not on regular (CN/ABP) Medicaid, along with their unpaid caregivers.
As of December 1, 2025, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a CARE assessment has found a nursing-facility level of care
The exact type of Apple Health/Medicaid coverage currently in place (Categorically Needy/ABP would make TSOA unavailable, while Medically Needy or a Medicare Savings Program might still allow it)
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist
Confirm the specific Apple Health coverage category with HCA to see whether TSOA could still be an option

programs.whyThisMayApply: Since this person is already enrolled in Apple Health Medicaid and lives at home, MAC could be worth exploring as a way to support any unpaid caregiver. However, it isn't known whether there currently is an unpaid caregiver, and MAC enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025.

whatItCovers.text: MAC provides benefits in steps to support an unpaid caregiver — starting with a one-time $250 amount, then up to $500 a year, and eventually an average of about $928 a month (up to $5,568 over six months, current 2025–26 rate) — covering things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals.
As of December 1, 2025, MAC enrollment is paused statewide, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a CARE assessment has found a nursing-facility level of care
Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver (age 18 or older) currently helping with care
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging / Community Living Connections to ask about the MAC waitlist
If there is an unpaid caregiver, ask about being added to the waitlist with basic demographic information

programs.whyThisMayApply: If a family member or friend is providing unpaid care for this 92-year-old, respite programs could offer some relief for that caregiver. The screening doesn't yet know whether there is an unpaid caregiver in the picture.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40 or more hours per week of care, while the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care and other supports, with amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging.
These programs support the unpaid caregiver rather than paying the care receiver directly, and eligibility depends on caregiver hours and need.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver, contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about Lifespan Respite or FCSP
Ask about a TCARE screening/assessment to help determine what level of respite support may fit

followUpQuestions: Besides help with transferring, is help needed with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, or taking medications?
Has a CARE assessment (the official Washington nursing-facility-level-of-care evaluation) already been done, or is one scheduled?
What is the exact type of Apple Health/Medicaid coverage — Categorically Needy, Alternative Benefit Plan, Medically Needy, or a Medicare Savings Program?
Is there a family member, friend, or other unpaid caregiver regularly helping with care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay (3 or more consecutive days) in the recent past?
Is the home a private residence, or is there any plan to move to an adult family home or assisted living facility?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — a 66-year-old on Medicare in Island County who needs help with transferring, toileting, bathing, and mobility after a stroke, lives at home with a spouse, has income between $1,000–$2,999/month, and has countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; it does not determine eligibility for any program. Several key details are still missing (exact monthly income, current Medicaid enrollment status, any recent hospital stay, and whether a family member provides regular unpaid care), so some of the results below are only partial matches until those questions are answered. Please treat every program listed as something to explore with the administering agency, not a confirmed benefit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home (not a nursing facility), and has care needs consistent with a skilled or personal-care need following a stroke, including help with transferring, toileting, bathing, and mobility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, walking, grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — generally at no cost for covered services.

Eligibility requires a need for part-time/intermittent skilled services, homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.
The process starts with a provider's face-to-face assessment and order, followed by selection of a Medicare-certified home health agency that coordinates the care plan.

nextSteps: Ask the treating doctor or nurse practitioner about a face-to-face assessment and homebound-status determination.
Ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Island County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 66, with ADL needs (transferring, toileting, bathing, mobility) suggesting nursing-facility level of care, and income/assets that appear to fall within TSOA's higher financial limits, this program's functional and financial criteria look like a potential match — though current statewide enrollment is paused.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA serves people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 single resource limit as of 2026), and no cost-sharing or estate recovery.

As of the most recent information, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and presumptive-eligibility screenings are suspended, though people already enrolled keep their services.
Applications can be submitted online via washingtonconnection.org, by the HCA 18-008 paper form, or through a local Area Agency on Aging/Community Living Connections.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about joining the TSOA waitlist.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is 66 and needs help with transferring, toileting, bathing, and mobility after a stroke — these are the kinds of ADL needs COPES is designed for — and reported countable assets under $2,000 match the resource limit. However, the exact monthly income wasn't precise enough to compare against the program's income threshold, so this can't be confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver for people 65+ (or 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability) whose CARE assessment finds they need nursing-facility level of care, based on unmet needs in ADLs like eating, toileting, bathing, transferring, and mobility.

It layers extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, usable in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.
Financial eligibility for the institutional pathway generally requires available income at or below $2,982/month (2026 Special Income Level) and countable resources of $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the $2,982/month Special Income Level.
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS office (Region 2 covers Island County) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described needs — help with transferring, toileting, bathing, and mobility — align with CFC's focus on people needing substantial ADL assistance. Whether the applicant is currently enrolled in, or eligible for, community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is unknown, which is a required financial pathway for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC provides personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, transferring, toilet use, and mobility, plus relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, delivered in the home or other community settings.

Eligibility requires a CARE assessment finding nursing-facility level of care and Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid eligibility in the community (or SSI-related CN status); people already on qualifying Apple Health coverage don't need to file a separate financial application.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the applicant is currently enrolled in (or would qualify for) Washington Apple Health Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage.
nextSteps: Check current Medicaid/Apple Health enrollment status with HCA.
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS intake to evaluate functional need.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being 65+ (turning 66) with reported help needed for transferring, toileting, bathing, and mobility fits the functional profile this program screens for, and reported assets under $2,000 match the resource limit. The precise monthly income figure needed to compare against the Special Income Level wasn't available.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to programs like COPES, and requires meeting Apple Health general rules plus a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care.
SSI-related coverage generally requires available income at or below $2,982/month (2026), with a Medically Needy pathway for people whose income is above that.
Resource limits are $2,000 for a single person, with spousal protections for a community spouse.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online) plus a functional CARE assessment; for Island County, the relevant HCS intake line is the Region 2 number.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the $2,982/month Special Income Level.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application online, by mail/fax, or in person.
Call the Region 2 HCS intake line (800-780-7094, since Island County is in that region) to request a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare enrollment supports possible eligibility for this benefit, but whether there has been a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing skilled-care need — a requirement for this coverage — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and medications/supplies used at the facility; costs vary by day within the benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days connected to a current skilled-care need.
nextSteps: Ask the hospital discharge planner or doctor whether any recent stay would qualify as a 3-day inpatient stay.
If a qualifying stay exists, confirm SNF coverage with Medicare before admission.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant's ongoing care needs (help with transferring, toileting, bathing, and mobility after a stroke) fit the kind of care situation respite programs are meant to support, but it isn't yet known whether an unpaid family caregiver (such as the spouse) is providing the regular hands-on care that these programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted respite agencies statewide.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver training, counseling, and support groups, with services tailored through a TCARE screening and varying by local Area Agency on Aging.
These programs generally require an unpaid caregiver providing regular care and generally do not stack with other publicly funded respite programs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family caregiver (such as a spouse) provides regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP eligibility and a TCARE screening.
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, ask about applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher.

followUpQuestions: What is the exact monthly income amount (not just the range), so it can be compared to the $2,982/month Medicaid Special Income Level?
Is the 66-year-old currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), including any Categorically Needy, Alternative Benefit Plan, or SSI-related coverage?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past connected to the current care needs?
Is a spouse or other family member providing regular unpaid care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Would the family like to be placed on the Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) statewide waitlist even though new enrollment is currently paused?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 69-year-old in Skamania County living at home with dementia, needing help with transferring and mobility, income under $1,000/month, assets between $2,000 and $85,000, and Medicare coverage — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. Because a few key details are still unknown (such as whether Medicaid is already in place, exactly how many daily activities need help, whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, and any recent hospital stay), this screening can only point to possibilities, not conclusions. An official agency visit or call is the only way to confirm what actually applies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned you're covered by Medicare and live in your own home, and you need help with mobility and transferring. Medicare's home health benefit is built for people in situations like this who need part-time skilled care or aide help at home.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical and occupational therapy, and home health aide help with walking, bathing, and grooming — as long as skilled nursing or therapy is also being received.
Covered home health services generally cost $0, and the care must be ordered by a provider after a face-to-face assessment, delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Eligibility also requires meeting Medicare's 'homebound' definition — either being advised not to leave home, or needing help/equipment to leave, with leaving home taking considerable effort.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or nurse practitioner about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and skilled care need
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Skamania County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 69 (meeting the 55+ requirement), your monthly income under $1,000 is well within TSOA's $3,976 limit, and your assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall within the $84,354 single-person resource limit. Your mobility and transferring needs also point toward the kind of nursing-facility-level care need TSOA is designed to address.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, including those with no unpaid caregiver, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid amounts.
There is no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to some other Medicaid long-term-care programs, and eligibility is reviewed annually.

As of the most recent update, enrollment is paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and presumptive eligibility screenings are suspended, with no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by mailing form HCA 18-008 to establish your place on the statewide waitlist
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite funding options while waiting

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've shared that you need help with transferring and mobility and have a dementia diagnosis, and your income (under $1,000/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall in ranges that sometimes align with Medicaid-related programs. However, it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid (CN/ABP) or how many total daily activities you need help with — both are required pieces for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help with activities of daily living including transferring, bed mobility, locomotion, and walking, along with related activities like bathing, dressing, and eating.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and financial eligibility generally requires being eligible for regular community Medicaid (CN or ABP), not institutional Medicaid rules.

Other covered services include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, and these can be provided at home or in certain residential care settings.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage
How many total daily activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, transferring) you need help with
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask HCS whether a financial application for community Medicaid is needed alongside the assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, it's not known whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor's order for daily skilled care — this is required for skilled nursing facility coverage and hasn't been mentioned.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient responsibility after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days, followed by a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify for SNF coverage before choosing a facility

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have ongoing care needs related to mobility, transferring, and dementia, which is the kind of situation these respite programs are designed to support. whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, available through contracted agencies, though funding is limited and not guaranteed.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, delivered in-home, at adult day centers, or residential care facilities, plus support groups, counseling, training, and equipment — with amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging.
Both programs are generally intended for unpaid caregivers providing regular care to the person needing help, with eligibility and service levels assessed individually.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care for you
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP eligibility and available funding
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers at wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 70-year-old family member in Skamania County who is married, covered by Medicare, living in a relative's home, needing help with mobility, dressing, toileting and transferring after a stroke, with monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington State and Medicare programs look worth a closer look with the actual agencies. A few programs need a bit more information from you (whether there's Medicaid community coverage already, a recent hospital stay, or an unpaid caregiver) before they can be fully assessed, so some follow-up questions are included below.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 70 (meeting the 65+ age rule), needs help with mobility, dressing, toileting, and transferring — which are core ADLs used to judge nursing-facility-level need — and has income under $1,000/month and assets under $2,000, both of which fall within COPES' financial limits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, nursing services, and specialized medical equipment, for people living in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living.

To be assessed for COPES, a person generally must be 65+ (or have an SSI-criteria disability), be found by a CARE assessment to need nursing-facility level of care, and meet income and resource limits — a single person's resources must be at or below $2,000.
COPES is Washington's largest home-and-community-based waiver, administered by DSHS Home and Community Services, and most clients are enrolled in it together with Community First Choice (CFC).

nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call the regional HCS intake line to request the CARE assessment that determines nursing-facility level of care.
Be ready to sign the DSHS 14-225 Acknowledgment of Services and disclose any transfers or gifts from the last 5 years.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 70, with income under $1,000/month (below the Special Income Level and even the Medically Needy Income Level) and assets under $2,000, your family member's financial picture fits within Apple Health LTC's limits, and the ADL needs described suggest the kind of functional need this program's CARE assessment looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports and is the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility generally requires meeting SSI-related income rules — available income at or below $2,982/month (the Special Income Level) or, if higher, potentially still qualifying through the Medically Needy pathway — along with resource limits of $2,000 for a single person.
Application involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online at washingtonconnection.org), a financial interview, and an in-person CARE assessment; processing is generally within 45 days.

nextSteps: Submit the financial application online at washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax using form HCA 18-005.
Complete the required financial interview with an HCS specialist.
Contact HCS Region 3 intake (covering Southwest Washington, including Skamania County) at 800-786-3799 to request the CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home (a family member's house, not a nursing facility), and has care needs — help with mobility, dressing, toileting, and transferring — that suggest a need for skilled or personal care support, which this benefit is designed to help with when paired with skilled services.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, dressing, and feeding) — but aide care is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.

To qualify, a person needs a health care provider's face-to-face assessment and certification of homebound status, and the care must be ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or nurse practitioner to perform a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and need for skilled care.
Get a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Skamania County.
Confirm with the agency in writing what services Medicare will and won't cover before care starts.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 70 (over the 55+ age threshold), and the reported income (under $1,000/month) and assets (under $2,000) are well within TSOA's higher financial limits, with ADL needs suggesting a nursing-facility level of care. whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, along with their unpaid caregivers, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 single resource limit as of 1/1/2026).

As of December 1, 2025, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and presumptive-eligibility screenings are suspended, with no announced reopening date. Current participants keep their services.

nextSteps: Understand that applying now would only place your family member on the statewide waitlist rather than start services immediately.

Ask the local Area Agency on Aging (via Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds, which don't depend on the paused TSOA budget, while waiting.
Watch for updates on when TSOA enrollment may reopen.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, mobility, medication management) and related IADLs, plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings including the person's own home.

Financially, CFC requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community; people already on qualifying Apple Health coverage don't need a separate financial application, only the functional assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid coverage (CN or ABP Apple Health).
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status.
If not yet enrolled, the financial application process is the same as for Apple Health LTC — this can be pursued alongside the COPES/Apple Health LTC applications.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare, which is one requirement for this benefit, but it isn't yet known whether there was a recent 3-or-more-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — a required trigger for this coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient responsibility after day 100.
