programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 81, live in Adams County, need help with dressing and transferring after a stroke, and have income under $1,000/month — all consistent with the kind of nursing-facility-level need and income range this Medicaid waiver looks at. However, your countable assets fall somewhere in a wide $2,000–$85,000 bracket, so it isn't clear yet whether they fit within this program's resource limit.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that adds services on top of personal care — such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing — for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.
To use COPES, an assessment must find a need at the nursing-facility level of care, which can include an unmet or partially met need in ADLs like transferring or dressing.
Countable resources generally need to be at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with special protections for a spouse who remains in the home.

informationStillNeeded: The exact amount of your countable assets, since the bracket given is too wide to know if it fits within the $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple) resource limit.
Confirmation of marital status for the application.
Whether you are already enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid).
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services to start a financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person.
Call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment to check functional eligibility.
Ask about spousal impoverishment protections given your marital status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with dressing and transferring, which are two of the activities this program looks at, and you have Medicare — but Medicare alone doesn't establish Medicaid eligibility, and it isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid or how many total activities you need help with.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice pays for personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, transferring, and bed mobility, plus related help like meal prep and housework.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must be eligible for community Medicaid (CN or ABP) rather than institutional financial rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
The total number of daily activities you need help with — the profile notes dressing and transferring, but the program looks for three or more ADLs at renewal.
nextSteps: Ask HCS about a CARE assessment to document all ADL needs, not just dressing and transferring.
Check your current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status with the Health Care Authority.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As the financial gateway to Medicaid long-term care services, this program's income and functional criteria seem to match your situation — age 81, stroke-related ADL needs, and income under $1,000/month — but your exact asset amount and current Medicaid status aren't yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Medicaid coverage that acts as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, nursing facility care, PACE, and other community long-term services.

Financial eligibility generally requires income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with protections when a spouse remains at home.
Functional eligibility is based on a CARE assessment showing a need at the nursing-facility level of care.

informationStillNeeded: The exact amount of your countable assets, to see if it's within the $2,000/$3,000 resource limit.
Confirmation of marital status for the application.
Whether you are already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid).
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call the regional HCS intake line for Adams County to schedule a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, but this benefit is for short-term rehabilitation after a hospital stay, not for ongoing help with daily activities like dressing and transferring. Whether there has been a recent qualifying hospital stay is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can pay for short-term skilled nursing facility care — including nursing, therapy, and a semi-private room — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
This benefit is meant for short-term rehabilitation, not ongoing custodial care with daily activities like dressing or transferring.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, which is required before Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay.
If there was no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about Medicare home health or Medicaid long-term care options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for ongoing help with dressing and transferring after a stroke suggests some level of caregiving is happening, but it isn't yet known whether a spouse or another unpaid person is providing that regular care, which these respite programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care.
The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care plus caregiver training, counseling, and support groups; amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver, such as a spouse, is providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about caregiver support options in Adams County.
If a spouse or family member is providing significant care, ask about a TCARE assessment for FCSP respite eligibility.

followUpQuestions: What is your exact countable asset amount? The bracket you gave ($2,000–$85,000) is too wide to compare against specific program limits like the $2,000/$3,000 Medicaid resource limit or the $84,354 TSOA limit.
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, which category (for example, Categorically Needy, Alternative Benefit Plan, or Medically Needy)?
Besides dressing and transferring, do you need help with any other daily activities, such as bathing, eating, toileting, or moving around the house?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently?
Does your spouse or anyone else provide regular unpaid caregiving, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?

Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 69, living in your own home in San Juan County, need help with dressing, transferring, and mobility, are single, have monthly income under $1,000, countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and are covered by both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) — several Washington programs look worth a closer look with a caseworker. Medicaid Community First Choice and the Medicare home health benefit appear to be the strongest fits based on the information given. Others, like the COPES waiver and Apple Health long-term care, could apply but hinge on the exact amount of your countable assets compared to the $2,000 single-person Medicaid resource limit. A few programs (Medicaid Alternative Care and Tailored Supports for Older Adults) depend on whether you have an unpaid family caregiver, and both currently have paused enrollment with a statewide waitlist as of December 1, 2025. The Medicare skilled nursing facility benefit and respite programs need more information (a recent hospital stay, and caregiver details) before it's clear whether they fit your situation. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the administering agency (usually DSHS/HCA or Medicare) can determine that. Please review the follow-up questions below; answering them will make any future screening more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already covered by Apple Health (Medicaid), and you need hands-on help with dressing, transferring, and mobility — needs that fit CFC's focus on people enrolled in community Medicaid who have substantial help needs with daily activities.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, and medication management, plus related IADL help such as meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, community transition help, and training on how to manage your own caregivers.

Services can be provided in your own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, and CFC is a Medicaid State Plan benefit (not limited by waiver slots) administered mainly through Home and Community Services.

nextSteps: Contact your regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment for CFC personal care services
If you aren't already confirmed on Apple Health CN/ABP coverage, ask about completing the related financial application

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 69, enrolled in Medicare, live in your own home, and need help with mobility, dressing, and transferring — the kind of skilled or personal-care needs this Medicare benefit is built to address for people who have trouble leaving home without help.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help (bathing, walking, grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.
Also covers durable medical equipment and supplies for home use, with no cap on the number of visits while you continue to qualify; covered home health services cost $0.
It does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that is the only care needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or another health care provider about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility (including homebound status)
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving San Juan County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 69 and need help with dressing, transferring, and mobility, which are the kinds of ADL needs COPES looks at when checking for nursing-facility level of care. Your monthly income (under $1,000) is well below the COPES income limit, and you're already enrolled in Medicare and Apple Health. However, your countable assets fall somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and COPES generally requires resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, so it isn't clear from what you've shared whether you'd meet that part of the rules.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds waiver services on top of Community First Choice personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, transportation, and help with housing transitions.

Services can be received in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility, and this is Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services.

informationStillNeeded: The exact dollar amount of your countable assets, to compare against the $2,000 single-person resource limit
Confirmation of your marital status for how the resource and income limits are applied
Confirmation of the specific type of Apple Health Medicaid coverage you currently have
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit the LTSS financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, or by mail/fax, so a caseworker can review your exact asset amount
Ask a DSHS/HCS worker directly how your specific assets compare to the $2,000 limit before assuming either way

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 69, with income under $1,000/month and needs around dressing, transferring, and mobility, you may meet the age, income, and functional pieces this program looks at as the financial gateway to services like COPES. But your countable assets are described only as somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and this program's standard resource limit for a single person is $2,000, so it's unclear whether that piece would be met.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, covering nursing facility care and serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (like COPES), PACE, institutional hospice, and other community LTSS.

informationStillNeeded: The exact dollar amount of your countable assets, to compare against the $2,000 single-person resource limit
Confirmation of your marital status for how income and resource rules apply
Confirmation of your current Apple Health Medicaid coverage type
nextSteps: Complete the financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or by phone
Call the Region 2 HCS central intake line (800-780-7094), which covers San Juan County, to request the CARE functional assessment
Ask a caseworker directly how your specific asset amount compares to the $2,000 limit

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which this benefit requires, but this program covers short-term rehabilitation after a hospital stay rather than ongoing at-home help, and it isn't known whether you've had a recent 3-day-or-longer hospital inpatient stay, which is required to qualify.

whatItCovers.text: Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, supplies, ambulance transportation when needed, and dietary counseling — for up to 100 days per benefit period following a qualifying hospital stay.
Days 1–20 have no daily charge; days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026); after day 100 the patient pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days (not observation status)
Whether a doctor has determined you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care following such a stay
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask the hospital discharge staff whether it counts as a qualifying 3-day inpatient stay
Ask your doctor whether ongoing skilled care needs might otherwise be addressed through Medicare home health or Medicaid instead

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55, and your income and asset details you shared are within TSOA's higher limits, and your ADL needs suggest a nursing-facility level of care that TSOA looks for. However, TSOA specifically excludes people already on Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Apple Health coverage, and your profile shows you're already covered by Apple Health Medicaid, so it's unclear this program would apply to you. TSOA is also currently paused for new enrollment statewide.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is described as support for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic (CN/ABP) Medicaid, along with support for their unpaid caregivers; it has no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, with no announced reopening date, though people already enrolled keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your current Apple Health coverage is Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan (which would make you ineligible for TSOA) or Medically Needy/Medicare Savings Program coverage
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to check current waitlist status and ask whether your specific Medicaid coverage type would affect TSOA eligibility
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite options while TSOA enrollment is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 69, already covered by Apple Health Medicaid, live in your own home, and have needs (dressing, transferring, mobility help) that suggest a nursing-facility level of care — these are the pieces MAC's rules matched. This program specifically supports an unpaid family caregiver, though, and it isn't yet known whether you have one. MAC enrollment is also currently paused statewide with a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: MAC provides caregiver support benefits in steps: a one-time $250 amount, then up to $500/year, then a larger ongoing benefit (about $928/month, up to $5,568 over six months for the 2025–26 period), used for things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care/day health, home-delivered meals, caregiver training, specialized medical equipment, and counseling or support groups.

MAC does not cover personal assistance services (those are available under TSOA instead), and there is no separate MAC income/resource test since it attaches to someone already on CN/ABP Apple Health.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (age 18 or older) who helps with your care
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the MAC waitlist and how to identify an eligible unpaid caregiver
If a caregiver is identified, ask about completing a TCARE caregiver screening

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your profile shows ongoing needs for help with dressing, transferring, and mobility, which is the kind of care situation respite programs are designed to support — but these programs generally exist to give relief to an unpaid caregiver, and it isn't yet known whether you have one providing regular care.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options).

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, training, and supplies; amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, with no statewide dollar cap published.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they help
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/your local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about FCSP eligibility for your situation
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington)

followUpQuestions: What is the exact dollar amount of your countable (non-exempt) assets, so it can be compared to program limits such as the $2,000 single-person Medicaid resource limit?
Do you have a family member or friend who provides unpaid care or supervision for you on a regular basis, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, or is one expected soon?
Is your current Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan (CN/ABP), or is it Medically Needy or a Medicare Savings Program?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 78-year-old in Franklin County living at home, needing help with eating, mobility, and transferring after a stroke, divorced, with monthly income under $1,000, countable assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage — several Washington programs and Medicare benefits look worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; none of these programs can be confirmed as something you qualify for until the administering agency reviews a full application and assessment. A few programs need a bit more information from you (such as whether you're already enrolled in community Medicaid, whether you've had a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether you have an unpaid family caregiver) before they can be screened more precisely — see the follow-up questions below.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 78 (meeting the age 65+ rule), you need help with eating, mobility, and transferring — ADLs commonly used to assess nursing-facility level of care — and your income (under $1,000/month) and assets (under $2,000) fall within the limits this program's rules check for.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers extra home-and-community-based services on top of Community First Choice personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

Eligibility involves an age or disability test, a CARE functional assessment finding nursing-facility level of care (including unmet needs in ADLs like eating, transferring, and mobility), income at or below the Special Income Level or Medically Needy Income Level, and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.
It's Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, administered by DSHS Home and Community Services, and most clients are enrolled in both CFC and COPES together.

nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Ask about the required DSHS 14-225 Acknowledgment of Services form and any needed transfer/gift documentation for the 5-year look-back.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 78 with help needed for eating, mobility, and transferring, and income and assets that fall within the program's financial thresholds, this program's financial and functional gateway rules all appear to line up with your situation.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS such as CFC, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

It covers nursing facility care for those meeting nursing-facility level of care, and full Apple Health CN medical benefits accompany LTC coverage; note that estate recovery can apply to LTSS costs paid from age 55 on.

Eligibility depends on meeting general Apple Health requirements, functional nursing-facility level of care via a CARE assessment, and income either at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) or through the Medically Needy pathway (income at or below $994/month).

nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org, or submit the paper HCA 18-005 form by mail or fax.
Call HCS central intake for your region (Region 3 covers Franklin County area — Pierce/Kitsap/Thurston/Clark/SW WA line: 800-786-3799) to schedule a CARE assessment.
Prepare for a financial interview with an HCS financial specialist as part of the process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have reported needs that suggest a skilled or personal care need related to your stroke — all things this benefit's rules look for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, feeding, grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — at $0 for covered home health services.

To qualify, a person generally needs a physician-ordered, certified need for part-time skilled care and must meet Medicare's definition of homebound status, confirmed through a face-to-face assessment.
It does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care that is the only care needed — the services must involve a skilled component.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor for a face-to-face assessment and order for home health services related to your stroke recovery needs.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County from your provider.
Discuss with the agency, before care starts, exactly what Medicare will and won't cover in your case.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 78, with a nursing-facility-level care need reflected in your reported ADL needs, and income and resources within TSOA's higher financial limits, your situation lines up with several of this program's screening rules — however, the program currently has a statewide enrollment pause that limits what's available right now.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, and normally has no cost-sharing or estate recovery.

As of the most recent information available, enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and presumptive-eligibility screenings are suspended, with no announced reopening date; people already enrolled keep their services.

Eligibility otherwise involves a CARE assessment for nursing-facility level of care, gross income at or below $3,976/month, and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person (as of the cited 2026 figures).

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/your local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status for TSOA.
While waiting, ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported needs with eating, mobility, and transferring suggest a substantial ADL need, which is one of the things this program's rules check for. However, it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP), which is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, mobility, medication management) and related IADLs, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings like your own home or an adult family home.

To qualify, someone generally needs a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid in the community (not institutional financial rules), though people already on SSI-related CN Medicaid also qualify.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or able to enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
nextSteps: Check with Washington Apple Health or an HCS office about your current Medicaid enrollment status.
If not yet enrolled, ask about applying for community Medicaid alongside a CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit, but it isn't yet known whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — a required piece of this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical/occupational/speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, generally after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period, with $0 per day for days 1–20 and $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 figures).

This benefit requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor determined you need daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay is upcoming or recent, ask the hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a SNF stay before admission.
If there's no qualifying hospital stay, ask your doctor about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid LTSS programs, or veterans' benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported ongoing care needs (help with eating, mobility, and transferring) suggest you need regular care or supervision, which is one factor these programs consider — but it isn't yet known whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing that regular care, which these respite programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers three main respite channels for unpaid family caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, plus counseling and training, with amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging), and DDA waiver respite (only for care receivers with a developmental disability that generally began before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers require the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver to live in Washington, and that they not already be enrolled in another respite program (with a limited waitlist exception).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week they provide.
nextSteps: If you have a family caregiver, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a Family Caregiver Support Program screening.
Ask your local Area Agency on Aging what respite options and funding levels are currently available in Franklin County.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in (or applying for) Washington Apple Health Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in recent memory, and if so, did a doctor order daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward?
Do you have an unpaid family member or friend who regularly provides you with care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Do you have a diagnosed cognitive impairment (such as memory loss or disorientation) in addition to your physical care needs?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 91-year-old in Stevens County living in a family member's home, needing help with dressing, bathing, mobility, and toileting, with heart disease, Medicare coverage, income under $1,000/month, and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look. A few key pieces of information are still missing (current Medicaid enrollment status, whether there's been a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved), so some of these leads need more detail before you'll know whether they truly fit. None of this is an eligibility decision — that can only come from the agency that runs each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, you're living at home (a family member's home, not a nursing facility), and you have described ongoing needs with mobility, bathing, dressing, and toileting — all of which line up with what this benefit is designed to help with, pending a provider's assessment of skilled or homebound status.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services at home.
Home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while you are also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT.
Covered services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To qualify, a provider must certify a homebound status and the need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment, and care must be ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or nurse practitioner about scheduling the required face-to-face assessment for homebound status and skilled care need.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Stevens County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 91 years old with income under $1,000/month (well below the $3,976 limit) and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 (under the $84,354 single-person limit), plus described needs across multiple ADLs suggesting a nursing-facility level of care, this program's financial and functional criteria appear to line up with your situation. Note, though, that statewide enrollment is currently paused.

whatItCovers.text: Serves people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care, even if they aren't on classic Medicaid — income and resources can be above regular Medicaid limits, and people on Medically Needy or Medicare Savings Program coverage may still qualify.
Income limit is $3,976/month and resource limit is $84,354 for a single person (as of 1/1/2026); functional eligibility requires nursing facility level of care.
There is no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure tied to this program, and eligibility is certified for 12 months.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go onto a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or the paper HCA 18-008 form to establish a place on the statewide waitlist, since intake is currently waitlist-only.
While waiting, ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds, which don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with dressing, bathing, mobility, and toileting — four ADLs — which meets the 'substantial ADL need' threshold this program looks for. whatItCovers.text: Covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, toileting, mobility/transfers, and medication management, plus related IADL support such as meal prep and shopping when ADL help is also needed.
Also includes relief (respite) care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.
Services can be provided in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living, among other settings, and are meant to supplement — not replace — Medicare or other insurance.

Financial eligibility requires being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, and functional eligibility requires a CARE assessment finding nursing-facility level of care with unmet needs in at least three ADLs.

whatItCovers.text: Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services (PT/OT/SLP) in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period, with no cost for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026) for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.
This is short-term skilled/rehab care, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, with no statewide dollar cap — amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.
These programs generally don't stack with each other, and DDA respite applies only when the care receiver has a developmental disability that generally originated before age 18.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — a 78-year-old in Jefferson County, WA, living at home, married, needing help with toileting, with monthly income under $1,000, countable assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage — several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look. A few important pieces of information are still missing, especially the full picture of daily-activity needs beyond toileting, whether the person is currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay. Filling in those gaps will make the next screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, lives at home in Jefferson County rather than in a facility, and has a stated need for help with toileting, which points to a personal or skilled care need — matching the basic criteria this program looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can pay for part-time skilled nursing, physical or occupational therapy, and home health aide help with things like bathing and walking, at no cost for the covered services, as long as skilled care is also part of the plan alongside any aide help.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver run by DSHS that adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, for people who already qualify for nursing-facility level of care but choose to stay in the community.
Covered services can include adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, and help transitioning to a new living situation.

To use COPES, a CARE assessment must show the person needs nursing-facility level of care, meaning significant help with several daily activities like toileting, bathing, or transferring, or a comparable need for nursing supervision.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (beyond toileting) the person needs help with, and how much help, to see whether the nursing-facility level of care standard is met.

Complete or check on a financial application (HCA 18-005) for Medicaid long-term services and supports.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared — an 86-year-old in Clallam County living at home with dementia and needing help with transferring, eating, and toileting, covered by both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid), divorced, with income in the $1,000–$2,999/month range and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. None of this is an eligibility decision; only the administering agency (DSHS/HCA or Medicare) can determine actual eligibility. A few key details are still missing, including the exact monthly income and asset amounts (the ranges given straddle several program cutoffs), whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay. Filling in these details with the family or a caseworker will make the next screening more useful. Two of the programs identified (TSOA and MAC) currently have paused enrollment statewide with waitlists, which is worth knowing before pursuing them.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is already covered by Apple Health (Medicaid) and needs help with three ADLs — transferring, eating, and toileting — which matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue receiving services. Living at home in Clallam County also fits the settings CFC serves.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and medication management, plus related IADL help, relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for caregivers on managing personal care providers.

CFC is a Medicaid state plan benefit (not a capped waiver), available to anyone who is enrolled in or eligible for regular community Medicaid (CN/ABP) and meets a nursing-facility level of care assessment.
People already on Apple Health CN/ABP typically only need the functional (CARE) assessment, not a new financial application.

nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment
Discuss provider options (individual or agency) once the assessment is complete

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare and needs help with transferring, eating, and toileting while living at home in Clallam County — the kind of at-home support this benefit is designed to help with, if a doctor certifies homebound status and a need for skilled care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy) — with no cost to the beneficiary for covered services.

To qualify, a person must need part-time or intermittent skilled care, be certified as homebound by a provider after a face-to-face visit, and receive care ordered by a provider through a Medicare-certified agency.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care at home, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that is the only care needed — aide help is only covered alongside skilled nursing or therapy.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment for homebound status and skilled care needs
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 86 and living at home in Clallam County, this person needs help with transferring, eating, and toileting and has a dementia diagnosis — these are exactly the kinds of needs the waiver's assessment looks for (3+ ADL needs, or cognitive impairment plus an ADL need). However, the income range given ($1,000–$2,999/month) and asset range ($2,000–$85,000) both span across the program's financial cutoffs, so it isn't clear yet which financial pathway would apply.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, nursing services, and help transitioning between living settings.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a need for nursing-facility-level care — for example, an unmet need in 3+ ADLs (which can include eating, toileting, and transferring), or cognitive impairment (like disorientation or memory impairment) plus an unmet ADL need.

Financially, there are two pathways: already being on SSI-related Medicaid (no cost share), or having income at or below $2,982/month (2026) with resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with some higher-income cases still qualifying under a Medically Needy pathway.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
The exact countable asset amount, to compare against the $2,000 resource limit
Confirmation of marital status for any spousal financial rules that might apply
Confirmation of the current Medicaid coverage category
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS office to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit or update the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax
Gather documentation on any asset transfers or annuities from the past 5 years, if applicable

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway to Medicaid long-term care services, and the family's profile — age 86, dementia, needs with transferring/eating/toileting, and existing Apple Health coverage — lines up with the kind of situation this program is built for. As with COPES, though, the exact income and asset figures need to be confirmed against the program's specific thresholds.

whatItCovers.text: This is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care and other community LTSS options.

Income eligibility generally requires income at or below $2,982/month (2026), with a Medically Needy pathway available for people above that amount; there is a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person under the SSI-related rules.

Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online at washingtonconnection.org), an interview with an HCS financial specialist, and an in-person CARE assessment; processing is generally within 45 days.

The exact countable asset amount

Confirmation of current Medicaid enrollment details
nextSteps: Submit or confirm the LTSS financial application through washingtonconnection.org
Complete the required interview with an HCS financial specialist
Schedule the CARE functional assessment

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit — it is meant for short-term skilled recovery after a hospitalization.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA serves people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular (CN/ABP) Medicaid; people already on CN/ABP Apple Health are not eligible for TSOA.

Benefits include caregiver assistance services such as respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals, plus training, specialized equipment, and health/counseling supports — delivered in benefit steps of $250, then up to $500/year, then a monthly amount averaging around $928 (through 6/30/2026).

Other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, Tailored Supports for Older Adults, respite care, and Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage) could also be relevant, but each has one or more key questions that haven't been answered yet — like whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health, how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone provides you unpaid care. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the administering agency can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below would sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, you've indicated a care need (help with bathing), and you live at home rather than in a nursing facility — these are the three things this benefit's screening looked at, and all three line up with what you've shared.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that could relate to an 84-year-old individual in Clallam County who lives at home, needs help with mobility and toileting, has income under $1,000 a month, has assets between $2,000 and $85,000, and is covered by Medicare. Some helpful details are still missing — such as whether Medicaid coverage is in place, exactly how many daily activities need help, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Because several of these answers are unknown, this report leans on suggested follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. None of the statements below are eligibility decisions — only the administering agency for each program can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, you live at home rather than in a facility, and you've described needing help with mobility, all of which line up with the basic requirements for this benefit. A doctor would still need to confirm homebound status and a skilled care need.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can pay for part-time skilled nursing care and therapies delivered in your home, plus home health aide help with things like bathing, walking, and grooming when it's paired with skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy.
To use this benefit, a doctor must certify — after a face-to-face visit — that you are homebound and need skilled care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; for covered durable medical equipment, you'd pay 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask your primary care provider about arranging a face-to-face assessment to check homebound status and skilled care needs.
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 84, your age meets the program's 55-and-older requirement, your monthly income (under $1,000) is well within the program's income limit, and your assets (between $2,000 and $85,000) fall within its resource limit for a single person. Your described needs with mobility and toileting are the kind of needs this program's functional assessment considers.

whatItCovers.text: This program supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular Medicaid, with higher income and asset limits than standard Medicaid — currently $3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources for a single person.

As of the most recent update, Washington has paused new enrollment in this program statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving immediate services, and there is no announced date for reopening.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about joining the current statewide waitlist.
Ask about non-Medicaid respite funding (FCSP) as a possible option while the waitlist is in effect.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with mobility and toileting, which are activities this program is designed to help with, and your monthly income (under $1,000) is in a range that can align with Medicaid financial limits. However, it isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid or how many total daily activities you need help with, both of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program provides personal care help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, and mobility, plus support such as relief care, nurse delegation, and personal emergency response systems.

To qualify, a person generally needs to already have (or be able to get) regular community Medicaid coverage, and an assessment showing an unmet or partly met need in at least three daily activities to continue receiving services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, transferring) you need help with
nextSteps: Contact your local Home and Community Services (HCS) office to ask about Medicaid enrollment and request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask about whether a financial application is needed or whether you may already qualify through existing coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which is a starting requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, and it's not yet known whether that has happened for you.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status does not count).
Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical supplies and equipment used in the facility.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it counts as a qualifying 3-day inpatient stay for skilled nursing facility coverage.
If there's no recent hospital stay, ask about other options like Medicare home health or Medicaid-funded personal care instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described ongoing care needs with mobility and toileting, which is relevant to respite programs meant to support the person providing your care. whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short breaks for unpaid family caregivers through options like Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year, subject to available funding) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which arranges short-term in-home, adult day, or residential facility care along with caregiver counseling and training.

Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing at least 40 hours a week of care, while FCSP eligibility and services vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are assessed through a caregiver screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver, contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite vouchers.
Ask specifically about Clallam County's local respite funding levels and current availability, since amounts and waitlists vary by county.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
Besides mobility and toileting, do you need help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or transferring (getting in/out of bed or a chair)?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more days in a row as an admitted inpatient recently?
Is there a family member or friend who provides unpaid regular care or supervision for you, and if so, about how many hours a week?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs based on the profile shared: a 79-year-old in Walla Walla County living in a family member's home, needing help with transferring, eating, and bathing after a stroke, covered by Medicare, with monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000. Several programs look worth a closer look, but a few key facts are still missing — especially whether the person is already enrolled in (or can enroll in) regular community Medicaid, whether there was a recent 3-day or longer hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly providing care. None of this is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency can decide that. The next best step is usually a conversation with Home and Community Services (HCS) or the local Area Agency on Aging to fill in these gaps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile confirms Medicare coverage, a care need involving help with bathing, eating, and transferring, and living at home rather than in a facility — all of which line up with the basic starting points for this benefit (medicare-home-health#00-overview-eligibility).

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy — not for personal care alone.
To qualify, a doctor or qualifying provider must certify homebound status and order the care after a face-to-face assessment, and the person must need skilled services, not just custodial care.
Durable medical equipment used at home is covered with a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible; DME and Medicare-covered home health services are otherwise $0 out-of-pocket.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor or nurse practitioner about a face-to-face assessment to determine homebound status and skilled-care need.
If a need for skilled care is confirmed, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Walla Walla County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile shows age 79 (well above the 55+ threshold), income under $1,000/month (well under the $3,976 TSOA limit), and care needs — help with transferring, eating, and bathing — that align with the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement (wa-tsoa#01-eligibility). However, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist rather than receiving immediate services (wa-tsoa#00-overview).

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with financial limits well above regular Medicaid, and it also serves people without an unpaid caregiver.

As of the most recent update, new applications only place a person on a statewide first-come, first-served waitlist; full financial and functional assessments happen only once called from the waitlist, and there is no announced reopening date for full enrollment.
People already fully enrolled or approved before December 1, 2025 keep their existing services.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or the paper HCA 18-008 form to establish a place on the waitlist, since the request date determines waitlist order.

While waiting, ask the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes needing help with transferring, eating, and bathing — three ADLs — which matches the substantial-need requirement for ongoing Community First Choice services (wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility). However, this program also requires eligibility for regular community Medicaid (CN or ABP), and it isn't yet known whether that applies here (wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility).

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related IADL help such as meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers relief (respite) care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training.
Services can be provided in a person's own home or in settings like an adult family home or assisted living, but CFC cannot replace Medicare, other insurance, or informal support already in place.
To qualify financially, a person generally must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, not the institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid — CN or ABP coverage.
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about a financial (Medicaid) application and a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS whether current income and assets would support CN/ABP community Medicaid eligibility, since that determination must come from them.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is confirmed, but this benefit specifically requires a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, and it isn't yet known whether that happened (medicare-snf#00-overview-eligibility). Without that stay, this program's short-term rehab coverage likely wouldn't apply to the ongoing daily care needs described.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehab care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not counting observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026), and full patient responsibility after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there was a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was ordered afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay is being planned or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a follow-up SNF stay.
If there was no qualifying hospital stay, consider asking about Medicare home health, Medicaid, or other options instead, as suggested in the program's own guidance.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes living in a family member's home with ongoing needs for help with transferring, eating, and bathing, which suggests a caregiver may be involved, but it isn't yet confirmed whether an unpaid caregiver is regularly providing that care (wa-respite-care#00-overview-eligibility) — this is a requirement for most respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week who cannot afford respite on their own.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies, with amounts varying by county/Area Agency on Aging.

These respite programs generally don't stack with each other — for example, Lifespan vouchers exclude people already receiving respite through another publicly funded program, with a limited waitlist exception.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP eligibility and a TCARE caregiver assessment.
If an unpaid caregiver provides 40+ hours/week, ask about applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher through wapave.org.

followUpQuestions: Is this person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) — either Categorically Needy (CN) or an Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) — or has an application been submitted?
In the last 30 days, was there a hospital admission as an inpatient (not just observation) that lasted 3 days or longer?

None of this is a decision about what you'll receive; only the agency that runs each program can determine actual eligibility. This benefit requires the person to be considered 'homebound' and to need only part-time or intermittent skilled care rather than full-time custodial care alone.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers tiered support: an initial one-time $250, up to $500/year more after a caregiver screening, and — after a full assessment — a larger benefit averaging about $928/month (up to $5,568 over six months, for the 2025–26 period), usable for things like respite, home-delivered meals, personal care, equipment, and caregiver training.

It's Washington's largest program of its kind, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and it wraps around Community First Choice personal care for people whose needs go beyond what CFC alone provides.
To be considered, an assessment must show a need for nursing-facility level care, which includes needing significant help with activities like bathing, toileting, and moving around.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact countable asset amount, to compare against the Medicaid resource limit (generally $2,000 for a single person, $3,000 for a couple)

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at eight Washington programs that may be worth exploring for an 83-year-old in Benton County who has Parkinson's disease, needs help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, is married, and is currently covered by both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid). Several programs — especially Medicaid Community First Choice and the Medicare home health benefit — look like they may be worth a closer look based on the care needs and coverage described. Others, like the Medicaid COPES waiver and Apple Health long-term care, could also be relevant but the rules engine could not confirm some financial details even though a general income and asset range was given, so those still need to be checked with the agency. Two programs — Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) and Medicaid Alternative Care (MAC) — currently have paused enrollment with a waitlist in Washington, so timing matters. Because several details are still unconfirmed (exact income and assets, the specific type of Apple Health coverage, whether there is an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stay), this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a conclusion. None of this is an eligibility determination — only the administering agency (DSHS/HCS or Medicare) can decide eligibility. Answering the follow-up questions below and speaking with the relevant agency or an Area Agency on Aging will give a clearer picture.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help such as bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, and medication management, along with services like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.

It's generally available to people already enrolled in community Medicaid who have an unmet or partially met need in three or more daily activities, which matches the current Apple Health coverage and the reported care needs.

nextSteps: Contact HCS to request a CARE assessment; since Apple Health coverage already exists, an additional financial application may not be needed.
Ask whether CFC could be combined with COPES ('CFC+COPES') if care needs turn out to be more extensive.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a nursing facility, and reports needing help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, which suggests a possible skilled or personal care need. report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared, this 75-year-old resident of San Juan County has several programs worth a closer look, though a few key facts are still missing that would help narrow things down. The VA programs (Aid & Attendance and Housebound) and Medicare's home health benefit look like strong candidates to explore given the veteran status, Medicare coverage, and reported help needed with mobility. Several Medicaid-related programs (COPES, Apple Health LTC, Community First Choice, and TSOA) could also be worth investigating given the very low income and assets, but the rules engine could not confirm functional need (how much help is needed with daily activities) or some other details, so those remain open questions. Respite care and Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit depend on facts not yet provided, like whether there's an unpaid family caregiver or a recent hospital stay. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency for each program can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile notes WWII-era military service, help needed with mobility, and very low income and assets — all of which line up with the underlying VA pension and Aid & Attendance criteria the rules engine marked as passing.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's VA pension for those who need help from another person with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.
The payment is based on the difference between countable income and a Maximum Annual Pension Rate set by the VA, and the money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.

nextSteps: Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680
Gather Social Security number, VA file number, service history, and income/asset details for the pension application (VA Form 21P-527EZ)
Contact a VA regional office or accredited veteran service officer to start the process

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported military service and mobility limitations align with the criteria the rules engine passed for this benefit, though it's worth noting Housebound and Aid & Attendance cannot both be received at the same time — only one applies.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's VA pension for someone substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
It cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.

nextSteps: Discuss with a VA-accredited representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound is the better fit, since only one can be received
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting housebound status

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage was confirmed, mobility help is needed, and the person lives at home rather than in a facility — all factors the rules engine passed for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical and occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, and medical social services delivered at home.
Home health aide help with walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT services.
Covered services cost $0 for the beneficiary, though durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or health care provider for a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving San Juan County

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, serving people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living facilities, layered on top of personal care services.
Covered services can include adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing services, transportation, environmental modifications, and specialized medical equipment and supplies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A enrollment is confirmed, but it isn't yet known whether there has been a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay, which is required for this benefit to apply.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid caregivers, with no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure.
