report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 67, live in Columbia County, WA, in a family member's home, need help with toileting and mobility, have heart disease, are a Vietnam-era veteran, are divorced, have monthly income under $1,000, and have countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000, with Medicare coverage — several programs may be worth a closer look. However, some important details are still missing (such as whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, your exact ADL needs, your military discharge type, any recent hospital stay, and whether someone is providing unpaid caregiving for you). Because these gaps affect several programs, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask an official agency or benefits counselor — not as a determination of what you can receive. Only the administering agency for each program can confirm actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live at home (not in a nursing facility), and you have care needs (help with toileting and mobility) that suggest a possible skilled or personal care need — the three basic conditions this benefit's rule check looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, walking, feeding) — but aide help is only covered while you're also getting skilled nursing or therapy services.

To qualify, a provider must certify you're homebound (leaving home is difficult or not recommended) and order the care, delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency; covered services cost $0, though durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Talk to your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and order home health services.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies in Columbia County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67 (over the program's age-55 threshold), your monthly income (under $1,000) is well within the $3,976 limit, and your assets ($2,000–$85,000) are within the $84,354 single-person resource limit. Your care needs (toileting, mobility) suggest a possible nursing-facility level of care, which this program also requires. Importantly, this program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than started on services right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with financial limits well above regular Medicaid, and it also serves people without an unpaid caregiver; there's no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to some other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

As of the most recent update, enrollment is paused: new applications only place you on a statewide waitlist managed by request date, and full financial/functional assessment happens once you're called from the waitlist; people already enrolled or approved before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

nextSteps: Submit a TSOA application (online via washingtonconnection.org or paper form HCA 18-008) to get a place on the statewide waitlist, since a request date matters even during the pause.
While waiting, ask your Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds, which aren't affected by the TSOA pause.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67 and need help with toileting and mobility, and your income (under $1,000/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) are in ranges that overlap with Medicaid-related financial standards mentioned for this program. However, this program requires being enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid and needing help with at least three ADLs to continue receiving services, and both of those are currently unknown for your situation.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Washington Medicaid State Plan benefit for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, requiring eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan community Medicaid, and (to continue at reassessment) an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.

It covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transfers, mobility) and some IADLs, plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training, in settings like your own home or an adult family home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in or could qualify for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
How many activities of daily living (ADLs) you need help with — the program lists specifics like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and transfers.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to ask about a financial application and a CARE functional assessment.
If not already on Apple Health, ask how to apply for community Medicaid coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're a Vietnam-era veteran, which falls within a recognized wartime period, and you need help with daily activities (toileting, mobility) — one of the A&A clinical criteria. Your reported assets ($2,000–$85,000) are well under the $163,699 net worth limit, and your income (under $1,000/month) is low. The one piece missing is confirmation of your discharge status, which is required for the underlying VA pension.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension amount for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing, layered on top of eligibility for the underlying Veterans Pension (which requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, wartime service, and income/net worth within limits).

It pays as a monthly cash amount — the VA pays the difference between countable income and a set Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR); for a veteran with no dependents, the MAPR with A&A is $29,093/year as of December 1, 2025. The money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.

informationStillNeeded: Your discharge type from military service (the underlying VA pension requires a discharge that was not dishonorable).
nextSteps: Locate your DD-214 or other discharge paperwork to confirm discharge status.
Ask a doctor to complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting your need for help with daily activities.
Consider applying for the underlying VA pension (Form 21P-527EZ) with help from a Veterans Service Officer.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're a Vietnam-era veteran with mobility limitations and heart disease, and your reported assets fall under the VA pension net worth limit. As with Aid & Attendance, your discharge status still needs to be confirmed, and note that a person can receive Aid & Attendance or Housebound, but not both at the same time.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability, and it shares the same underlying pension eligibility rules (discharge status, wartime service, income/net worth limits) as VA Aid & Attendance.

It pays as a monthly cash amount; for a veteran with no dependents, the MAPR with Housebound status is $21,313/year as of December 1, 2025 — lower than the Aid & Attendance rate, and a claimant can only receive one benefit or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Your discharge type from military service (required for the underlying VA pension).
nextSteps: Confirm discharge status using DD-214 or similar documents.
Discuss with a VA-accredited representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound is the better fit, since only one can be received.
Ask a doctor to complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting your home confinement.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a specific type of recent hospital stay, and it's not known whether you've had one.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing facility care — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and medical supplies — but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, with admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient cost after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor determined you need daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If you are hospitalized in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether the stay qualifies you for SNF coverage.
If there's no qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives like Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need ongoing care/supervision given your toileting, mobility, and heart disease needs, and you live in a family member's home, which raises the possibility that person is providing unpaid care. However, it's not yet known whether there's an unpaid caregiver providing regular care for you, which these programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care), the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP, for caregivers of adults 18+ needing care, with services tailored through a caregiver assessment), and DDA waiver respite (only if the care receiver has a developmental disability originating before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year; FCSP respite amounts and services vary by county/Area Agency on Aging with no statewide cap; DDA respite would not apply unless there is a qualifying developmental disability.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the family member you live with (or anyone else) provides you with regular unpaid care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about a caregiver assessment (TCARE) for FCSP respite options.
If a caregiver is providing 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
Besides toileting and mobility, do you need help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management?
What was your discharge status from military service (for example, honorable, general, or dishonorable)?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights as an admitted inpatient recently, and if so, did a doctor say you need ongoing daily skilled care afterward?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at eldercare programs in Washington State based on the profile shared: an 80-year-old living in Lincoln County, needing help with dressing and mobility, with heart disease, Medicare coverage, veteran status (Gulf War era or later), married, monthly income under $1,000, and countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000. Several programs — mostly Medicaid-related long-term care programs, Medicare's home health and skilled nursing benefits, Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA), and respite care options — appear worth a closer look based on age, care needs, income, and Medicare enrollment. However, some important details are still missing or unclear, such as the exact countable asset amount, current Medicaid enrollment status, the specific number of daily activities needing help, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) is involved. None of this is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency (DSHS, HCA, Medicare, or the local Area Agency on Aging) can decide that. This report is meant to help identify which programs are worth investigating further with those agencies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is already in place, and the reported need for help with dressing and mobility points toward a personal or skilled care need. Living at home in Lincoln County (rather than a nursing facility) matches this benefit's setting.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, and home health aide help (such as help with bathing and walking) when also receiving skilled care — at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a doctor must certify homebound status and order the care, which is then delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with a doctor about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Lincoln County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 80, with reported income under $1,000/month and assets in the $2,000–$85,000 range, the figures appear to fit within TSOA's higher income and resource limits. Needing help with dressing and mobility also points toward the nursing-facility-level-of-care functional standard this program uses.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers services for older adults who need a nursing-facility level of care but don't have (or don't want) full Medicaid coverage, with no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure.

It uses higher income and resource limits than Medicaid — $3,976/month income and $84,354 (single) or $156,883 (married) in resources for 2026 — figures the reported income and assets appear to fit within.
Important: as of the latest available information, new TSOA applications are being placed on a statewide waitlist rather than processed for immediate services.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252), keeping in mind new applications currently go on a waitlist.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid respite funds while on the TSOA waitlist.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 80 with help needed for dressing and mobility, and a monthly income reportedly under $1,000 (which is within the program's $2,982/month income limit), COPES may be worth investigating. It is intended for people who need a nursing-facility level of care but wish to stay in their own home, which matches living in a private residence in Lincoln County.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home and community services on top of personal care, such as adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, and specialized medical equipment and supplies.
It is Washington's largest program of this kind, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and services can be delivered in a private home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.
To be considered, an assessment must show a need for nursing-facility level of care, and available income generally needs to be at or below $2,982/month (2026 figure).

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount, to compare against the program's $2,000 (single) resource limit
Details about current marital status for benefit and spousal-protection calculations
Whether Medicaid coverage is currently in place
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services to start the two-part application: the financial form (HCA 18-005) and a CARE functional assessment.
Ask specifically about spousal resource protections given the marital status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with dressing and mobility suggests some level of personal care need, which is the core of what Community First Choice covers. Since this is a Medicaid community-based benefit, it's worth checking alongside COPES for the 80-year-old in Lincoln County.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and moving around, plus supports like personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training.
It's a Medicaid state-plan benefit (not limited by waiver slots) for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, administered through Home and Community Services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there's current enrollment in (or ability to enroll in) community Medicaid coverage
How many daily activities require help, since continuing CFC generally requires needing help with at least three
nextSteps: Contact HCS central intake to request a CARE assessment.
Check current Medicaid enrollment status with HCA or DSHS.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health LTC is the financial gateway to several long-term care programs, and the reported income (under $1,000/month) appears to fall within its income guideline. Needing help with dressing and mobility is also relevant to the functional assessment this program requires.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's the financial gateway that connects to programs like COPES.
Income eligibility uses the Special Income Level, $2,982/month in 2026, which the reported income appears to fall under.
Resource limits are generally $2,000 for a single person, with special rules that can protect some resources for a spouse.
Application can be made online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail or fax, by phone, or in person, followed by a CARE assessment from HCS.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount, to compare against the resource limit
Details about marital status for benefit calculations
Whether Medicaid coverage is currently in place
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online, by mail/fax, or in person.
Call the regional HCS intake line to schedule a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is in place, which is one requirement for this benefit. Whether it currently applies depends on a piece of information not yet available: a recent qualifying hospital stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a short stay in a skilled nursing facility — including room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy — after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period.
It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status doesn't count), with SNF admission generally within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of three or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay has occurred or is planned, ask hospital discharge staff whether it meets Medicare's 3-day inpatient rule.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being married and needing help with dressing and mobility raises the possibility that a spouse or family member is providing unpaid care, which is what respite programs are designed to support — though this hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Programs such as Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) can help pay for short-term relief care, in-home help, adult day services, counseling, and caregiver training.
Lifespan Respite can be applied for online, by phone, or by email; FCSP is accessed through the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options available in Lincoln County.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 85, live in your own home in Jefferson County, WA, need help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, have heart disease, are a peacetime-era veteran, are single, have Medicare, monthly income in the $1,000–$2,999 range, and countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. A few key facts are still missing (your exact monthly income, whether you're already enrolled in Apple Health Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether anyone provides you unpaid care), so some of these can only be explained in general terms until you or a caseworker fill in those gaps. This screening does not decide eligibility for any program — only the administering agency can do that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare and need help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, and you live at home rather than in a nursing facility — all of which match the basic conditions for this benefit. Your heart disease could also be a reason a doctor might order skilled home care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help with things like bathing and walking — but aide help only counts if you're also getting skilled nursing or therapy.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To qualify, you generally need to be homebound (have real difficulty leaving home without help) and have a doctor certify, after a face-to-face visit, that you need part-time skilled care; the care must be ordered and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your primary care provider about whether you meet the 'homebound' standard and could benefit from a referral for skilled home health care.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 85, you meet the age requirement (55+), and your reported income (in the $1,000–$2,999/month range) and assets (under $2,000) both fall well within TSOA's higher income and resource limits. Your need for help with dressing, bathing, and transferring is the kind of need this program's functional assessment considers.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic (CN/ABP) Medicaid, including those with no unpaid caregiver, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person, as of 1/1/2026).
There is no cost-sharing for TSOA participants, and no Medicaid-style estate recovery; eligibility is certified for 12 months at a time.

Important: statewide enrollment in TSOA has been paused since December 1, 2025. New applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional processing happens only when called from the waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about the current TSOA waitlist and how to be placed on it.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds as an alternative while TSOA enrollment is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 85 and need hands-on help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, which are the kinds of daily activities this program's functional assessment looks at, and your countable assets are under $2,000, matching this program's resource limit for a single person. Your income falls in the $1,000–$2,999/month range, which straddles the program's $2,982 threshold, so it isn't yet clear which financial pathway would apply to you.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care — such as adult day health, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, specialized equipment, and skilled nursing — for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.

To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find that you need nursing-facility level of care, which can include an unmet or partially met need in three or more daily activities such as bathing, dressing, transferring, or medication management.

Financially, this program generally requires countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, and either being already on SSI/SSI-related Medicaid, or having income at or below the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (1/1/2026), with a possible spend-down option for people above that amount.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income amount, to see whether it is at or below the program's $2,982/month limit, or whether the spend-down option might apply.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment and start a financial application.
Ask a caseworker to compare your exact income to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) and the Medically Needy option.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for help with dressing, bathing, and transferring lines up with the ADL-based functional test this program uses. It isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in (or would qualify for) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid, which is the financial gateway for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, bed mobility, toileting, and medication management, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems, relief care, and caregiver management training, in your own home or other community settings.

To qualify, a CARE assessment must find you need nursing-facility level of care, and you must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community (or already be on SSI/SSI-related Medicaid).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
nextSteps: Check with the Health Care Authority or DSHS about your current Apple Health Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to document your care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 85 and needing help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, you may meet the functional side of this Medicaid long-term-care coverage, and your assets under $2,000 match the resource limit for a single person. Your income in the $1,000–$2,999/month range straddles the program's income threshold, so more detail is needed to know which pathway would apply.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and nursing facility care, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility generally requires meeting Apple Health rules, functional eligibility through a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, and income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), or qualifying through the Medically Needy pathway if income is higher.
Resource limits are $2,000 for a single person, with a home equity limit of $1,130,000, and a 60-month look-back period for asset transfers.

You can apply online at washingtonconnection.org, by paper form, by phone, or in person, and a CARE functional assessment is arranged through your regional HCS intake line — for the Olympic Peninsula/Jefferson County area, that's Region 3.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income amount, to see whether it falls at or below the $2,982/month Special Income Level or would need the Medically Needy pathway.
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by paper form (HCA 18-005).
Call the Region 3 HCS intake line (800-786-3799) to request a CARE assessment, since Jefferson County falls in that region.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, it isn't known whether you've had a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, which is required to qualify for this particular coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient responsibility after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, with a continuing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If you are hospitalized in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether the stay would qualify you for SNF coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need ongoing help with daily activities like dressing, bathing, and transferring, which fits the 'care receiver needing ongoing supervision' side of these programs. However, it isn't known whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, which is a key eligibility factor for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers three main respite channels for unpaid caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care), the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) through local Area Agencies on Aging, and DDA waiver respite (only if the care receiver has a developmental disability that began before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers require that the caregiver and care receiver both live in Washington, cannot afford respite on their own, and are not already receiving respite through another publicly funded program.

informationStillNeeded: Whether someone provides you with regular unpaid care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: If a family member or friend provides you regular unpaid care, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite voucher eligibility.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 79-year-old Korea-era veteran in Adams County who needs help with eating and bathing, has monthly income under $1,000, countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000, and is covered by Medicare — several programs look worth a closer look. VA Aid & Attendance and the Medicare home health benefit appear to be strong matches given the facts you provided. A few other programs (Community First Choice, VA Housebound, Medicare SNF coverage, TSOA, and respite care) could also be relevant, but the screening is missing some details — like your Medicaid enrollment status, the full count of daily activities you need help with, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stays — that would sharpen the picture. This is an educational screening only; none of the agencies involved have reviewed your situation, and only they can determine actual eligibility. Please treat every item below as something to verify with the program, not a guarantee of any benefit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Korea era, which is a recognized wartime period, your discharge was not dishonorable, you need help with daily activities (eating and bathing), and your countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall within the VA's net worth limit of $163,699 — all specific rule checks passed for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension amount for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

It provides a monthly cash payment on top of the base VA pension; for a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual rate with A&A is $29,093/year (effective December 1, 2025), versus $17,441/year without it.
The payment amount is based on the gap between countable income and the VA's Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR).

nextSteps: Gather VA file number, service history, and income/asset documentation for the pension application (VA Form 21P-527EZ).
Arrange for a medical examiner to complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting your need for help with daily activities.
Contact a Veterans Service Officer or the VA Pension Intake Center to start the application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, you live at home rather than in a nursing facility, and you need help with eating and bathing, which points to a personal or skilled care need — all specific checks for this benefit passed.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time skilled care delivered at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, generally at no cost to the beneficiary for covered services.
Coverage requires homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered by a certified agency.

It covers skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, grooming, feeding) — but aide care is only covered while also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services.
It does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that is the only care needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to determine homebound status and skilled care need.
If eligible, ask your provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 79 (over the 55+ age requirement), your monthly income under $1,000 is well within the $3,976 limit, your assets ($2,000–$85,000) are within the $84,354 single resource limit, and your need for help with eating and bathing points toward a nursing-facility level of care — all specific checks for this program passed. However, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with financial limits above regular Medicaid, and it does not require cost-sharing or carry estate-recovery exposure.

Important: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025. New applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced reopen date; people already enrolled or approved before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: If interested, submit a TSOA application (online via Washington Connection or paper form HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist.
Ask your local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid respite funding (FCSP) that doesn't depend on the paused TSOA program while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 79 and need help with eating and bathing, and your income (under $1,000/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) are in a range that can align with Medicaid financial rules for this program, but the screening doesn't yet know whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid or exactly how many activities of daily living you need help with — CFC generally requires help with 3 or more ADLs to continue at reassessment.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is Washington's Medicaid option for personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, administered through Home and Community Services.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financially must qualify for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan community Medicaid (or already be on SSI-related Apple Health).

It covers help with ADLs (bathing, eating, dressing, toileting, transfers, and more) and IADLs (meal prep, housework, shopping), plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and other supports.
In-home CFC clients who qualify through community CN/ABP Medicaid generally pay no participation toward cost of care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or able to enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many activities of daily living you currently need help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE assessment.
Ask HCS or a Community Living Connections specialist whether your income and assets would fit community Medicaid rules.
If not already on Apple Health, discuss starting a Medicaid application alongside the functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your Korea-era service, discharge status, and net worth appear to fit the underlying VA pension requirements, but the screening doesn't yet know whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability, which is the specific criterion for this benefit. Note that this benefit and VA Aid & Attendance cannot both be received — only one applies.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound is an increased monthly pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and it cannot be paid at the same time as Aid & Attendance.
It adds a monthly cash payment; for a veteran with no dependents the maximum annual rate is $21,313/year (effective December 1, 2025), versus $17,441/year standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance would better fit your situation, since only one can be received.
If pursuing this benefit, arrange for VA Form 21-2680 to document housebound status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, but this benefit specifically requires a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing daily skilled-care need, and the screening doesn't know whether that has happened.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Days 1–20 cost $0, days 21–100 have a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026), and the patient pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been (or will be) a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days with a need for daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as an inpatient stay (not observation) and whether Medicare would cover a follow-up SNF stay.
If no qualifying hospital stay applies, ask about alternatives like Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need ongoing help with daily activities like eating and bathing, which points to an ongoing care need, but the screening doesn't know whether you have an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care — which is central to whether respite programs would apply, since these programs primarily support caregivers rather than care receivers directly.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs in Washington offer short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging), and DDA respite (only if the care receiver has a developmental disability).
Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week of care; FCSP eligibility and service levels are tailored locally and vary by community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and if so, how many hours per week
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver in the picture, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program services in Adams County.
Ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers if the caregiver provides 40+ hours per week of unpaid care.

followUpQuestions: Besides eating and bathing, do you need help with any other daily activities (such as dressing, toileting, walking, or transferring)?
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied?
Do you have an unpaid family member or friend who provides regular care or supervision for you, and if so, how many hours a week?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive nights recently, or is one expected soon?
Do you spend most of your time at home because of a permanent disability (rather than by choice)?

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have care needs (Parkinson's, help with mobility and bathing) that suggest a possible skilled or personal-care need — all of which match what this benefit looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, walking) — but aide help only counts if you're also getting skilled nursing or therapy.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during World War II, which is a recognized wartime period, and you need help with daily activities like bathing — both line up with what this benefit looks for. whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.
For a veteran with no dependents, the Maximum Annual Pension Rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year, compared with $17,441/year without it (figures effective December 1, 2025).

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not counting observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost after day 100 within a benefit period.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 82, live in a family member's home in Skamania County, need help with mobility and toileting, are a Korea-era veteran, are single, have monthly income in the $1,000–$2,999 range, have countable assets under $2,000, and are covered by Medicare — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; it does not determine eligibility for any program. Some important details are still missing (exact income amount for Medicaid comparisons, whether you're already enrolled in Medicaid, how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent 3+ day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly providing your care). Filling in those gaps with the administering agencies will give a clearer picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Korea era, a recognized wartime period, and you need help with mobility and toileting — activities that align with the program's daily-activity-help criterion [va-aid-attendance#00-overview-eligibility]. Your countable assets are well under the VA's net worth limit of $163,699, and your income falls in a range that may work with VA's pension calculation [va-aid-attendance#00-overview-eligibility].

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension amount for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

Underlying pension eligibility requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, service including at least one day during a recognized wartime period (Korean Conflict, 6/27/1950–1/31/1955, is listed), and income/net worth within limits ($163,699 net worth limit effective 12/1/2025).

It pays a monthly cash amount — the difference between countable income and a Maximum Annual Pension Rate — that the household can use at its discretion for care costs; for a veteran with no dependents and A&A, the MAPR is $29,093/year effective December 1, 2025.
Documentation typically includes VA Form 21-2680 completed by a medical examiner, and standard pension paperwork covering service history, income, and assets.

nextSteps: Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to apply for the underlying pension and request the A&A medical examination (VA Form 21-2680).
Gather military service records, income and asset documentation for the application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Korea era, a recognized wartime period, and your countable assets are within the VA's net worth limit [va-housebound#00-overview-eligibility-what-it-covers-how-to-apply-documentation-needed-part-1]. Housebound status specifically requires being substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, which hasn't been separately confirmed here, and this benefit cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — only one applies.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased VA pension amount for veterans substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability, and cannot be received alongside Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.
Underlying pension eligibility (discharge status, wartime service, income and net worth limits) is the same as for Aid & Attendance, with the net worth limit at $163,699 effective December 1, 2025.
It pays a monthly cash amount added to the pension; for a veteran with no dependents, the Maximum Annual Pension Rate with Housebound status is $21,313/year effective December 1, 2025.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability, and whether you'd rather pursue this or Aid & Attendance (only one can be received)
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound status better fits your situation, since only one can be received.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare and need help with mobility and toileting, and you live at home (a family member's home) rather than in a nursing facility, which lines up with this benefit's basic conditions [medicare-home-health#00-overview-eligibility].

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time skilled care at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, generally at no cost to the beneficiary for covered services.
Coverage requires part-time or intermittent skilled care needs, homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and a provider order carried out by a certified agency.

Covered services include skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and part-time home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, grooming) but only alongside skilled nursing or therapy — not as stand-alone personal care.
24-hour care, meal delivery, and general homemaker or custodial care alone are not covered.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to determine if you meet the homebound and skilled-care criteria.
Ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Skamania County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55 and your income and assets appear to fall within TSOA's higher financial limits, and needing help with mobility and toileting points toward the functional need this program targets [wa-tsoa#01-eligibility]. However, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed immediately [wa-tsoa#03-how-to-apply].

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, and involves no cost-sharing or estate recovery.
Financial limits (effective dates noted) include income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person.

As of the most recent verification, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025; new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist, with full processing happening once called from the waitlist.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or a local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist and any interim non-Medicaid respite options.
Submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the waitlist if interested.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 82 (meeting the 65+ age rule) and living in a family member's home while needing help with mobility and toileting, which points toward the kind of daily-living needs this waiver is designed for [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. Your countable assets are under $2,000, matching the single-person resource limit [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. Whether your income fits within the program's financial rules still needs to be checked.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington waiver that adds services on top of Medicaid personal care for people who need nursing-facility level of care but choose to stay in the community, including in a family member's home.

It can include adult day care/health, wellness training, help with moving to a safer living situation, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, without duplicating Medicare or other insurance benefits.

To be financially eligible under the institutional pathway, income generally must be at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.
Eligibility also requires an assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, based on needs in activities like toileting, bathing, transfer, and mobility.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment and start a financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office.
Ask HCS how your specific monthly income compares to the Special Income Level and Medically Needy Income Level.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with mobility and toileting, which are examples of the activities of daily living this program is meant to support [wa-medicaid-cfc#01-what-it-covers-how-to-apply-documentation-needed]. However, it isn't yet known whether you are enrolled in a qualifying community Medicaid plan, or whether your total number of ADL needs meets the program's threshold, so this remains uncertain.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice provides Medicaid-funded personal care for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, including help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and medication management, plus related help like meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training, and can be provided in a family member's home.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid coverage
How many daily activities in total you need help with
nextSteps: Ask HCS or your local Area Agency on Aging whether you're enrolled in a qualifying Apple Health plan.
Request a CARE assessment to document the full extent of your daily living needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 82, with help needed for mobility and toileting, and countable assets under $2,000, you may fit the age, functional, and resource factors this program looks at [wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1, wa-apple-health-ltc#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. Whether your income fits the program's rules is still unclear.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and is the financial gateway to waivers like COPES, PACE, and other community LTSS.
Financial eligibility for the SSI-related pathway generally requires income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month, 1/1/2026), or the Medically Needy pathway if income is above that.
Resource limits are $2,000 for a single person, with a home equity cap of $1,130,000 (1/1/2026).

Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview, and a separate in-person CARE assessment of functional need; the standard processing time is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income amount, to compare against income limits
nextSteps: Submit a financial application through washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office and request the CARE assessment through HCS central intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A coverage, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but this coverage is for short-term rehabilitation following a hospital stay, and it's not yet known whether you've had a qualifying recent inpatient hospital stay [medicare-snf#00-overview-eligibility].

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility after a qualifying hospital stay, up to 100 days per benefit period — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a follow-up SNF stay before admission.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need ongoing help with mobility and toileting, which fits the general idea of needing care or supervision that these programs are meant to support [wa-respite-care#00-overview-eligibility]. It isn't yet known whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly providing your care, which most of these programs require.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for developmental disabilities).

Lifespan Respite vouchers are up to $1,000 per household per year and require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week of care; FCSP has no statewide cap and varies by Area Agency on Aging, tailored through a caregiver assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) is providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP eligibility and available respite funds.
If a family caregiver is providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers as well.

followUpQuestions: What is your exact monthly income (not just the $1,000–$2,999 range), so it can be compared to Medicaid income limits like the $2,982 Special Income Level?
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), either a community (CN/ABP) plan or another category?
Besides mobility and toileting, do you need help with other daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, or medication management? How many in total?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (as an admitted inpatient) recently, or are you expecting one?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid care for you, and if so, about how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared, this screening found several Washington and federal programs worth exploring further with the right agency. VA Aid & Attendance and the Medicare home health benefit look like strong starting points because the profile's details (Vietnam-era veteran status, Medicare coverage, and help needed with dressing, transferring, and toileting) line up closely with what those programs look for. A few other programs — Medicaid Community First Choice, VA Housebound, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA), and respite care programs — may also be worth a conversation, but the screening is missing some key pieces of information (such as current Medicaid enrollment, any recent hospital stay, and whether a family member is providing regular unpaid care) needed to know how well they fit. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency (DSHS, the VA, or Medicare) can determine actual eligibility. Please treat this as a starting point for questions to ask, not a result.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes a Vietnam-era veteran (a recognized wartime period) with a discharge that was not dishonorable, who needs help with dressing, transferring, and toileting — matching the VA's clinical criterion of needing another person's help with daily activities. The stated assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall well within the VA's net worth limit of $163,699.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

As of December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance for a veteran with no dependents is $29,093/year, compared to $17,441/year without it — the VA pays the difference between countable income and this rate.
This is a cash payment the household can use at its discretion for care costs, not a direct service program.

nextSteps: Gather documentation such as Social Security number, VA file number, service history, and income/asset details for the pension application
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 and submit VA Form 21P-527EZ, which includes the Aid & Attendance request

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is covered by Medicare and needs help with dressing, transferring, and toileting while living in a family member's home, which matches this benefit's focus on people with a skilled or personal care need who are not in a nursing facility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing, physical/occupational therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, feeding) — aide help is covered only alongside skilled nursing or therapy services.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment costs 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a doctor must certify homebound status and a need for skilled care, and the care must be ordered and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment to check homebound status and need for skilled care
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is over 55, and the ADL needs described (dressing, transferring, toileting) suggest a level of care similar to what TSOA requires; the stated income ($1,000–$2,999/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall under TSOA's limits of $3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources for a single person.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, and it also serves unpaid family caregivers in some cases.
There is no cost-sharing and no estate recovery for TSOA services, and eligibility is certified for 12 months at a time.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go onto a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced date for reopening.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or apply through washingtonconnection.org to be placed on the TSOA waitlist
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting, since those don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes needing help with dressing, transferring, and toileting — three ADLs, which matches this program's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living. The stated monthly income ($1,000–$2,999) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) are in a range that could align with Medicaid's financial rules, but whether the applicant is enrolled in, or would qualify for, community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is unknown, so this program is only possibly relevant right now.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and medication management, plus supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.
To keep receiving these services at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, which matches the three ADL needs noted in the profile.
Financial eligibility requires being eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage, rather than the financial rules used for institutional care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the applicant is enrolled in, or would qualify for, Washington Apple Health Community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage
nextSteps: Contact Washington Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask DSHS about applying for Apple Health Community Medicaid if not already enrolled

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran with a qualifying discharge and assets within the VA's net worth limit, several Housebound eligibility factors appear to line up. However, whether the veteran is substantially confined to home by a permanent disability is not yet known from the profile, and it's worth noting that Housebound and Aid & Attendance cannot both be paid at the same time — only one would apply.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound adds a monthly cash payment to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
As of December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status for a veteran with no dependents is $21,313/year, compared to $17,441/year standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the veteran is substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits the veteran's situation, since only one benefit can be paid at a time
If pursuing Housebound, have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting housebound status

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is covered by Medicare, which is a starting requirement for this benefit, but the screening does not yet know whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — the specific trigger for this coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
In 2026, the cost is $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and the patient pays all costs from day 101 onward.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, and whether a doctor has determined a need for daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage
Ask the doctor whether ongoing skilled nursing or therapy needs would meet Medicare's SNF criteria

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant lives in a family member's home and needs help with several daily activities, suggesting an unpaid caregiver may be involved — but the screening doesn't yet know whether someone is providing unpaid care on a regular basis or how many hours per week, which these programs use to determine fit.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care who can't afford respite on their own.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers free or low-cost short-term care (a few hours to a few days) plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling for unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care; amounts vary by county's Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care to the applicant, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program respite options in Kittitas County
If a family member is providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, under which category (Community Medicaid CN/ABP, Medically Needy, etc.)?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more days in a row recently, or is one expected soon?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Is the applicant substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability, rarely leaving except for medical care?
Would the family like to be placed on the statewide waitlist for TSOA even though new enrollment is currently paused?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at 9 programs based on what you've shared about your 90-year-old family member in Okanogan County — a stroke diagnosis, help needed with bathing, eating, and dressing, veteran status (Gulf War era or later), marriage, Medicare coverage, and income/asset ranges. Several programs look worth a closer look, especially Medicare home health and VA Aid & Attendance, because the profile lines up with what those programs generally cover. However, a number of details the rules engine needs — the exact monthly income and asset figures, whether the household is already enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, the veteran's discharge type, whether there was a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved — are still unknown. Because so many of these specifics are missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a conclusion. None of these programs can be confirmed as something the family qualifies for — only the agency that runs each program can make that determination. The follow-up questions below would help sharpen the picture before talking with an official agency or benefits counselor.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is confirmed, and there's a clear indication of a personal or skilled care need (help with bathing, eating, and dressing following a stroke), living at home rather than in a facility — all pieces this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and durable medical equipment.
Home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, grooming, or feeding) is covered only while skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT is also being received — it is not a stand-alone personal care benefit.
Covered services cost $0; a homebound determination and a face-to-face provider assessment are required before care starts.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or health care provider for a face-to-face assessment and homebound-status determination
Get a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency
Ask the agency what will and will not be covered before care begins

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 90 years old with income in the $1,000–$2,999 range and assets in the $2,000–$85,000 range, the household appears to fall within TSOA's income and resource limits, and the ADL needs (bathing, eating, dressing) point toward the nursing-facility-level-of-care functional standard this program looks at. However, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA serves people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, with income and resource limits set well above regular Medicaid.
There is no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applications go on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed immediately, with no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist, since intake is currently waitlist-only
Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting
Ask HCS about functional (CARE) assessment timing once called from the waitlist

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 90 years old and needing help with bathing, eating, and dressing after a stroke, the age and functional pieces of COPES look like they could line up. But COPES also depends on exact income and asset numbers and whether Medicaid is already in place, and those specifics aren't confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, such as adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.
It can be used in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and it's meant to add on top of — not replace — Medicare or insurance benefits.
COPES is Washington's largest Medicaid waiver for home and community services, run by DSHS Home and Community Services.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
The exact countable asset amount, to compare against the program's asset limit
Marital status details relevant to spousal financial rules
Whether the household is currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application online, by mail, fax, or in person
Gather exact income and asset figures, plus any Medicaid enrollment information, before applying

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with bathing, eating, and dressing suggests a substantial ADL need, which is one piece CFC looks at. Whether this program applies further depends on current or potential Medicaid enrollment, which isn't confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and medication management, plus supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
It's a Medicaid state plan benefit (not a capped waiver), available in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting, and it can combine with COPES for additional wraparound services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the household is enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
nextSteps: Check on current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status
If not enrolled, ask HCS about applying for community Medicaid alongside a CARE assessment
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS intake to evaluate ADL needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being 90 years old with help needed for bathing, eating, and dressing fits the age and functional pieces this program looks at. Apple Health LTC is also the financial gateway to programs like COPES, but the exact income, asset, and Medicaid enrollment details aren't confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to nursing facility care and to HCS waivers such as COPES.
Financial eligibility uses either a Special Income Level pathway or a Medically Needy pathway if income is higher, and there are asset limits with spousal protection rules for married applicants.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount
The exact countable asset amount
Marital status details relevant to spousal financial protections
Whether Medicaid is already in place
nextSteps: Apply using HCA 18-005 through washingtonconnection.org, by mail, fax, or in person
Call regional HCS intake to arrange a CARE assessment (Okanogan County generally falls under the Eastern Washington HCS region)
Gather income, asset, and marital documentation ahead of the financial interview

programs.whyThisMayApply: Veteran status from the Gulf War era or later, combined with needing another person's help with bathing, eating, and dressing, matches the kind of situation this benefit is designed to look at. Net worth also appears within the program's limit based on the asset range given. The main open item is confirming the discharge type.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need another person's help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

As of December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year for a veteran with no dependents, or $34,488/year with one dependent, with additional amounts per extra dependent.

The underlying VA pension also requires a discharge that was not dishonorable and service including at least one day during a recognized wartime period, which includes the Gulf War era (8/2/1990–ongoing).

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation that the discharge was not dishonorable
nextSteps: Locate discharge paperwork (such as a DD-214) to confirm discharge type
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680
Submit VA Form 21P-527EZ to apply for the underlying pension and Aid & Attendance

This benefit also cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly cash payment to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
As of December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status is $21,313/year for a veteran with no dependents, or $26,710/year with one dependent.
A veteran can receive either Housebound or Aid & Attendance, but not both at the same time.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation that the discharge was not dishonorable
Whether the veteran is substantially confined to the home due to a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound fits the situation better, since only one can be received
Gather discharge documentation
If pursuing this benefit, have a medical examiner document housebound status on VA Form 21-2680

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A enrollment is in place, but this benefit specifically requires a recent hospital inpatient stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and that hasn't been confirmed as part of the current situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare SNF coverage pays for a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
For 2026, days 1–20 cost $0, days 21–100 cost $217/day coinsurance, and the person pays all costs after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage before any SNF admission
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits instead

programs.whyThisMayApply: Living in a family member's home while needing help with bathing, eating, and dressing suggests there may be an unpaid family caregiver involved, which is central to these respite programs — but that hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite or adult day services.

The Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in residential care, plus support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.
DDA respite only applies if the care receiver has a developmental disability that generally originated before age 18, which does not appear to match this situation.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program eligibility
If an unpaid caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers
Confirm whether any current respite support is already being received elsewhere, since programs generally don't stack
