report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — you're 70, live in your own home in Yakima County, have Medicare, get by on under $1,000 a month with modest savings, and need help with bathing, toileting, and eating due to heart disease — five programs came up as worth a closer look: Medicaid Community First Choice, the Medicare home health benefit, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA), and respite care programs for caregivers. None of this means you are approved or guaranteed anything — only the agency running each program can decide that. A few pieces of information are still missing (whether you're on community Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone unpaid regularly helps care for you), so some answers below are tentative until you check with the right office. TSOA in particular currently has a statewide enrollment pause with a waitlist, which is important context if you look into it.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This was flagged because you're enrolled in Medicare, you live at home in Yakima County (not a facility), and you have care needs — help with bathing, toileting, and eating — along with a heart disease diagnosis that a provider could evaluate for a skilled-care need.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment for people who are homebound and need skilled care, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with bathing, walking, grooming, and feeding is included, but only while skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy is also part of the care plan — it isn't covered as stand-alone personal care.
A doctor or nurse practitioner must complete a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and the need for skilled care before a Medicare-certified agency can start services.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face homebound assessment to see if skilled home health services would apply to your situation.
If appropriate, ask your doctor for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Yakima County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: TSOA was flagged because you are 70 (meeting the 55+ age requirement), your monthly income under $1,000 is well within the $3,976 TSOA income limit, your countable assets fall within the $84,354 single resource limit, and your need for help with bathing, toileting, and eating aligns with the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on regular Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, with income and resource limits set well above standard Medicaid, and it also supports unpaid family caregivers or serves people who have no caregiver.
Approved participants pay no cost-sharing and have no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to some other Medicaid long-term-care programs; eligibility is reviewed every 12 months.

Important: statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025. New applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional review only happens once someone is called from the waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: If you'd like to pursue this, apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit paper form HCA 18-008 to join the statewide waitlist, understanding services may not start right away.
Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget while you wait.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This showed up because your need for help with bathing, toileting, and eating meets the substantial-ADL-need rule for this program, and your income (under $1,000/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall in a range close to Medicaid financial limits mentioned in the program materials. What's not yet known is whether you're currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, plus related support such as meal prep and housework when personal-care help is also needed.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for family caregivers on managing paid providers.

To use this program, someone generally needs to be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid in the community, along with a nursing-facility-level-of-care finding that includes an unmet need in at least three daily activities to continue at renewal.

People already on SSI-related Medicaid, where standard limits are around $994/month income and $2,000 in resources, may qualify without a separate financial application — only a functional assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid — Apple Health Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Check your current Apple Health/Medicaid status, or ask HCS about applying for community Medicaid if you're not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This came up because you're covered by Medicare, but whether this benefit currently applies depends on a recent 3-night (or longer) inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor-ordered need for daily skilled care — that information wasn't part of your profile.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and the patient pays all costs after day 100.
This benefit is short-term rehabilitation coverage after a hospitalization, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more nights, and whether a doctor determined daily skilled nursing or therapy care is needed afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay afterward.
Keep records of any hospital admission and discharge dates in case this becomes relevant later.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This was flagged because you need ongoing help with several daily activities, but whether respite programs apply depends on whether an unpaid family member or friend is regularly providing your care — that detail wasn't included in your profile.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care for unpaid caregivers who provide 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options).

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, plus caregiver training, counseling, and support groups, with amounts and services varying by county Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family member or friend regularly provides your care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss whether a caregiver in your life could apply for respite support.
If there isn't currently an unpaid caregiver, ask the AAA about other supports that might better fit your situation.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) under a Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan, or could you apply for it?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights in a row recently, and did a doctor say you need daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Does an unpaid family member or friend regularly help care for you, and if so, about how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — a 71-year-old in Whatcom County living in a family member's home, covered by Medicare, with a stroke diagnosis and needing help with eating, low monthly income (under $1,000) and countable assets (under $2,000), and married — several Washington programs and Medicare benefits look worth a closer look. However, some key details that most of these programs use to sort out fit are still unknown, especially how many daily activities need help with beyond eating, whether Medicaid enrollment status, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Because so many program-specific answers are still missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask an agency or advisor — not as a conclusion about what this family will receive. Nothing here determines eligibility; only the administering agency can do that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a nursing facility, has a stroke diagnosis, and needs help with eating — all factors that align with Medicare's home health benefit, which covers part-time skilled care and aide help for people at home with a skilled or personal care need.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide care (bathing, grooming, feeding help) when also receiving skilled care — at no cost for covered services.

Eligibility requires a face-to-face assessment and provider certification of homebound status and a documented need for skilled care, ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation of homebound status (trouble leaving home without help, or leaving isn't advised) and a doctor's certification of a skilled care need
nextSteps: Ask the treating doctor about a face-to-face assessment and a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 71, with monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000, this family's numbers are well within COPES' income and resource rules, and the need for help with eating is one of the activities COPES' functional assessment looks at [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2].

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver for people who qualify for nursing-facility-level care but choose to stay in the community, layering extra services on top of Medicaid personal care (CFC) — including adult day care/day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized equipment, and transportation.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find the person needs nursing-facility-level care — generally meaning unmet or partially met need in 3 or more daily activities (like eating, bathing, toileting, transferring) at certain levels, or 2 or more at higher levels, or daily nursing supervision, or cognitive impairment plus an ADL need.
Income up to $2,982/month and assets up to $2,000 (single) generally fit the financial rules, and services can be delivered in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, walking) need help with, to see if the nursing-facility-level-of-care threshold might be met
Confirmation of the current living situation (private home, family member's home, adult family home, etc.)
nextSteps: Apply for the financial piece online at washingtonconnection.org, or by mailing/faxing form HCA 18-005 to HCS-LTSS
Call the regional Home and Community Services intake line to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with eating is the kind of daily-activity need CFC's personal care benefit is built around, and this family's very low income and assets suggest they may fit within Medicaid's community financial rules, though current Medicaid enrollment isn't yet confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is Washington's main Medicaid personal-care benefit for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community — covering help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and related tasks, plus relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

To continue CFC after the first assessment, a person needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community (or SSI/SSI-related coverage).

informationStillNeeded: Whether the 71-year-old is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid
How many daily activities need help with, since ongoing CFC generally requires help in three or more
nextSteps: If not already on Apple Health, apply for community Medicaid coverage
Request a CARE functional assessment through regional HCS intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 71, income under $1,000/month, and assets under $2,000 fit comfortably within this program's financial standards, and living in a family member's home (rather than a medical institution) fits the community pathway described, though the functional level of care isn't yet confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's the financial gateway to waivers like COPES as well as nursing facility care, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.
Income up to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) and resources up to $2,000 (single) generally fit within the standard SSI-related financial rules for this coverage.

Functional eligibility requires a CARE assessment finding nursing-facility level of care, and the application involves both a financial application and a separate functional assessment, typically processed within 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities need help with, to help determine nursing-facility-level of care
Confirmation of the exact current living situation
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or by mailing/faxing HCA 18-005 to HCS-LTSS
Call the Region 2 HCS intake line for Whatcom County (800-780-7094) to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is in place, and the stroke diagnosis could relate to skilled nursing needs, but this benefit specifically requires a recent 3-or-more-day inpatient hospital stay, which hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and supplies, with no charge for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 figures).

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was ordered afterward
nextSteps: Check recent hospital discharge paperwork for a qualifying inpatient stay
Ask the hospital discharge team whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 71, with income under $1,000/month and assets under $2,000, this family's finances fall well within TSOA's generous limits ($3,976/month income, $84,354 in resources for a single person), and needing help with eating relates to the functional need TSOA looks for — but statewide enrollment is currently paused, and the level-of-care determination isn't yet made.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, along with support for their unpaid caregivers.

Eligibility requires a nursing-facility-level-of-care functional finding, income at or below $3,976/month, and resources at or below $84,354 (single) or $156,883 (married with a community spouse) as of 1/1/2026.

As of the most recent update, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025; new applicants join a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving immediate services, with no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities need help with, to help determine nursing-facility-level of care
nextSteps: If interested, ask about joining the statewide TSOA waitlist through washingtonconnection.org or a local Area Agency on Aging
While waiting, ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: Living in a family member's home while needing help with eating and being married suggests there may be an unpaid family caregiver involved, which is the basis for these respite programs — though it hasn't been confirmed whether someone is providing regular unpaid care.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), covering care from a few hours to a few days in-home, at adult day services, or in a residential care facility.

Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year and require the caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of unpaid care; FCSP amounts and rules vary by county Area Agency on Aging and are assessed through a caregiver specialist (TCARE screening).
FCSP is reached through the local Area Agency on Aging, found via Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is currently an unpaid family caregiver (such as a spouse or the relative whose home they live in) providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about a caregiver assessment for FCSP or Lifespan Respite eligibility

followUpQuestions: Besides help with eating, does the 71-year-old need help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, walking, or transferring — and about how much help (some help vs. a lot of help)?
Is the 71-year-old currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or in a Medicare Savings Program / Medically Needy coverage?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (not just observation) in the recent past, especially related to the stroke?
Is there an unpaid family caregiver — such as a spouse or the relative whose home they live in — providing regular day-to-day care, and roughly how many hours per week?
What is the exact living arrangement — a private home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility — since this can affect which services apply?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 67-year-old veteran in Grays Harbor County living in a family member's home, needing help with mobility, toileting, and dressing after a stroke, covered by Medicare, with modest monthly income and higher countable assets — six programs came up as worth a closer look. One (Medicare's home health benefit) matches well on the details you've given. The others look potentially relevant too, but each has at least one important unknown (such as Medicaid enrollment status, VA discharge type, net worth relative to VA's limit, recent hospital stays, or whether someone is providing unpaid caregiving) that an agency would need to check before saying anything about eligibility. This screening is educational only — no program membership or benefit amount is being promised, and only the administering agency can determine actual eligibility. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you've described needing help with mobility, toileting, and dressing following a stroke (indicating a skilled or personal care need), and you live at home (a family member's home) rather than in a nursing facility — all three of which line up with this benefit's basic eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (help with bathing, dressing, and similar tasks) when also receiving skilled care, delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket; durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

Eligibility requires homebound status (trouble leaving home without help, or leaving is not recommended), a face-to-face provider assessment, and a provider's order for care from a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Talk with a doctor or health care provider about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and order home health services.
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grays Harbor County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with mobility, toileting, and dressing — three ADLs — which lines up with the level of need this program looks for at reassessment. However, whether you're currently enrolled in (or would qualify financially for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is unknown, and that's a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program provides personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, for people assessed at a nursing-facility level of care.

To keep receiving these services at reassessment, a person needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must be financially eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.
Services can be provided in your own home, an adult family home, or other community settings, and are meant to supplement — not replace — Medicare, other insurance, or informal support.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in (or able to enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage
nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) regional intake to ask about a financial application and CARE functional assessment.

Ask HCS whether your income and asset levels would allow you to qualify for community Medicaid coverage, since your income falls in the $1,000–$2,999/month range and your assets are reported at $160,000 or more.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Korea-era veteran who needs help with daily activities like mobility, toileting, and dressing, you may meet two of the core pieces of this benefit — wartime-era service and the clinical need for help with daily activities. But your discharge type and exact net worth relative to the VA's limit are unknown, and both are required for the underlying pension this benefit is added to.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

The underlying pension requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, net worth under $163,699 (effective December 1, 2025), and at least one day of service during a recognized wartime period — the Korean Conflict (6/27/1950–1/31/1955) is one of the listed wartime periods.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year, compared to $17,441 without it, effective December 1, 2025.

informationStillNeeded: Your discharge type from military service
Whether your countable net worth is within the VA's pension limit of $163,699
nextSteps: Gather military discharge paperwork (DD-214 or equivalent) to confirm discharge status.
Contact a Veterans Service Officer (available through county veterans' offices or VFW/American Legion) to review net worth and income against VA pension limits.
Ask a medical provider about completing VA Form 21-2680 to document the need for help with daily activities.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Korea-era veteran, and based on the rules engine noting you may be substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, this benefit could be worth checking — though it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance, so only one would apply. Your discharge type and net worth are unknown, which are needed to confirm the underlying VA pension eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and it cannot be paid together with Aid & Attendance.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status is $21,313/year, compared to $17,441 standard, effective December 1, 2025.
The underlying pension eligibility — discharge status, net worth limit of $163,699, and wartime service requirements — is the same as for Aid & Attendance.

informationStillNeeded: Your discharge type from military service
Whether your countable net worth is within the VA's pension limit of $163,699
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance would be the better fit to pursue, since only one can be received.
Have a medical provider complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting housebound status if this path is chosen.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, which is a starting requirement for this benefit, but this program is specifically for short-term skilled care after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, and whether you've had such a stay is unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, including room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and medical supplies, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
For 2026, days 1–20 cost $0, days 21–100 cost $217/day coinsurance, and days 101+ are the patient's full responsibility; it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage.
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, consider asking about Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits as alternatives, as suggested in this program's own guidance.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need help with mobility, toileting, and dressing, which suggests a family member may be providing regular care — but whether there is an unpaid caregiver, and how many hours a week they provide, is unknown, and that's central to these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for developmental disabilities originating before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite, adult day services, or other options; funding is limited and not guaranteed.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver support groups, counseling, and training, tailored through a caregiver specialist's assessment; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to connect with the local Area Agency on Aging serving Grays Harbor County and ask about FCSP.
If a family caregiver is providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers by phone (253-442-3214) or email (getrespite@wapave.org).

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it recently?
What was your discharge status from military service (for example, honorable, general, dishonorable)?
Do you know your exact countable net worth, and how it compares to the VA's $163,699 pension limit?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (as an admitted inpatient, not just under observation) recently, followed by a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is a family member or friend providing regular, unpaid care or supervision for you, and if so, about how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look for this 92-year-old veteran in Mason County who needs help transferring and is covered by Medicare. The VA Aid & Attendance benefit and Medicare's home health benefit look like strong first steps to explore because the facts you gave line up closely with what those programs require. Several Medicaid-related and other programs (COPES, Community First Choice, Apple Health LTC, TSOA, VA Housebound, Medicare SNF, and respite care) could also apply, but the rules engine could not confirm key details — such as how many daily activities need help, whether the household is enrolled in Medicaid, exact asset amounts, hospital stay history, or whether there's an unpaid caregiver in the home. None of this means eligibility has been decided one way or the other — only the administering agency (DSHS, HCA, or VA) can make that determination. Answering the follow-up questions below will help sharpen which of these programs are worth pursuing first.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse served during the Vietnam era, which is a recognized wartime period, and needs help with transferring — one of the daily activities the VA looks at for Aid & Attendance. The household's income and assets appear to fall within the VA's net worth limit of $163,699, based on the information given.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

Eligibility requires the underlying VA pension (income/net worth limits, wartime service, discharge not dishonorable) plus at least one clinical criterion, such as needing another person's help with daily activities.
The Vietnam era (8/5/1964–5/7/1975) is a recognized wartime period for pension eligibility purposes.

It's a cash payment added to the pension, not a service — the money can be used at the household's discretion for care costs, with the amount based on the difference between countable income and a VA-set maximum annual pension rate.

nextSteps: Complete VA Form 21P-527EZ for the underlying pension
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting the need for aid and attendance
Gather military service records, income, and asset documentation for the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse is covered by Medicare and needs help with transferring, and lives at home rather than in a nursing facility — all of which align with this benefit's basic requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and home health aide care (only alongside skilled care) delivered by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost for covered services.
Eligibility requires a homebound status determination, a face-to-face provider assessment, and a physician's order for care from a Medicare-certified home health agency.
It does not cover 24-hour care at home or personal/custodial care when that's the only care needed — it must be paired with skilled services.

nextSteps: Ask your spouse's doctor about a face-to-face homebound assessment
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Mason County

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse is 92 (meets the 65+ age rule) and your household's monthly income is under $1,000, which is within COPES' Special Income Level of $2,982/month. Needing help with transferring is one of the activities COPES' assessment looks at, but it's not yet clear whether the overall level of need meets the program's 'nursing facility level of care' standard, and the exact asset amount within your $2,000–$85,000 range matters because the resource limit is just $2,000 for a single person (higher with spousal protections).

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver serving people in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, layered on top of personal care services.

It can cover things like adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, and help with moving to a new living situation.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a 'nursing facility level of care' need — generally meaning unmet help needs in several daily activities, needed nursing-level care, or certain memory/orientation issues — and the person's resources generally must be at or below $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple), with higher protections for a spouse still living in the home.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities need help, and how much (this affects the 'level of care' assessment)
Confirmation of current living situation details for the application
The exact countable asset amount, since the limit is much lower than the $2,000–$85,000 range given
Confirmation of marital status details for spousal protection rules
Whether currently enrolled in any Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) program
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment
Consider submitting the HCA 18-005 financial application via washingtonconnection.org
Ask a Home and Community Services caseworker how spousal asset protections might apply

programs.whyThisMayApply: CFC is closely tied to COPES and Apple Health LTC, and the same age and income facts apply. Whether it's worth pursuing depends on unanswered questions about current Medicaid enrollment and how many daily activities (beyond transferring) need help, since continuing CFC requires an unmet need in at least three activities of daily living.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is Washington's main Medicaid personal care benefit for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, covering help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transfers, and similar tasks, plus things like personal emergency response systems and caregiver training.

To qualify, a person generally needs a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment and must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not institutional rules); continuing CFC after reassessment requires an unmet need in three or more daily activities.

informationStillNeeded: Whether currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many daily activities need help (three or more is the ongoing requirement)
nextSteps: Ask HCS about a CARE assessment for CFC eligibility
Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status with HCA

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway to COPES and CFC, and your spouse's age (92) and monthly income (under $1,000) fit within the described limits. As with the waiver programs, the level-of-care and exact asset details still need to be confirmed by an assessment.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care and serves as the financial pathway into home-and-community waivers like COPES, PACE, and other community long-term-care services.

Income up to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) generally qualifies, and there's a Medically Needy pathway for people above that; resources are generally limited to $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple), with home equity and spousal protection rules applying.
Applying involves a financial application through washingtonconnection.org or paper form, plus a functional (CARE) assessment arranged through regional HCS intake.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities need help, to establish nursing-facility level of care
Confirmation of the exact countable asset amount
Confirmation of current Medicaid enrollment status
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application online or by mail
Call regional HCS intake to schedule the CARE functional assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse's Vietnam-era wartime service and apparent net worth fit the underlying VA pension rules, similar to Aid & Attendance. However, it's not yet known whether your spouse is substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, which is the specific test for this benefit — and importantly, a veteran can only receive Housebound or Aid & Attendance, not both.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound is an increased monthly pension payment for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability.

It requires meeting the underlying VA pension rules (same as Aid & Attendance) plus being substantially confined to the home and its immediate premises due to a permanent disability expected to last a lifetime.
It cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a veteran receives one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your spouse is substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA-accredited representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits the situation, since only one applies

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse is enrolled in Medicare, but this benefit only applies after a recent qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and it's not known whether that has happened recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care per benefit period in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
It covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and related supplies, with cost-sharing starting on day 21 of a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a follow-on SNF stay

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse's age (92) and the household's income and asset levels appear to fall within TSOA's higher financial limits. However, the program has paused new enrollment statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist, and it's not yet known whether the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement is met.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA serves people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but have income and resources above regular Medicaid limits, with no cost-sharing and no estate recovery.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist with no announced reopening date, though current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment would find your spouse eligible
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current TSOA waitlist
Ask about non-Medicaid respite funds through the Area Agency on Aging while waiting

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your spouse needs ongoing help, which could make respite programs relevant if there's an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care — but that hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs include Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year, limited by funding) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which offers short-term relief care, counseling, training, and equipment for unpaid caregivers of adults.
Lifespan Respite requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week of care; FCSP eligibility and service levels vary by Area Agency on Aging and are set through a caregiver screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite options

followUpQuestions: How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around) does your spouse need help with, and how much help — some, or a lot?
Is your spouse currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, which program?
What is the exact amount of countable assets (savings, investments, etc.) your household has, not counting the home?
Has your spouse had a hospital stay (admitted as an inpatient, not just observation) in the last 30 days?
Is your spouse substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability, or able to leave with some help?
Is there an unpaid family caregiver (such as a spouse) providing regular care, and about how many hours per week?

It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 75, live in your own home in Adams County, need help with dressing, have Parkinson's, are covered by Medicare, are a WWII-era veteran, and have low monthly income with modest countable assets — several programs may be worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only, not an eligibility decision; only the agency that runs each program can determine whether you actually qualify. A few important pieces of information are still missing (such as whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, how many daily activities you need help with, whether anyone provides you unpaid care, and whether you've had a recent hospital stay), so some of the programs below have real uncertainty until those gaps are filled in. Please treat every item here as something to explore with the administering agency or a benefits counselor, not as a confirmed benefit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the WWII era, your discharge information suggests it wasn't dishonorable, you need help with a daily activity (dressing), and your countable assets fall well under the VA's net worth limit of $163,699 — all factors the screening checked and found consistent with this benefit. Your reported monthly income under $1,000 is also well below the program's annual payment thresholds.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension amount for veterans (or some survivors) who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

To get the underlying pension, you need a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, income and net worth within VA limits (net worth limit $163,699 as of December 1, 2025), and qualifying wartime service — WWII service (12/7/1941–12/31/1946) counts as a recognized wartime period.
It pays a monthly cash amount added to the pension; for a veteran with no dependents, the maximum with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year (versus $17,441 standard), effective December 1, 2025.
The payment amount is based on the difference between your countable income and this maximum rate.

nextSteps: Gather VA Form 21-2680 (to be completed by a medical examiner) documenting your need for help with dressing and other activities.
Prepare pension application materials: Social Security number, VA file number, military service history, income, and asset documentation.
Contact a Veterans Service Officer or the VA directly to start the pension application (Form 21P-527EZ) with the Aid & Attendance request.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live at home rather than in a facility, and you've described a personal care need (help with dressing) related to your Parkinson's diagnosis — the general factors this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time skilled care delivered at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, you generally need a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care, homebound status (trouble leaving home without help, or leaving isn't recommended), a face-to-face provider assessment, and a provider order for care from a Medicare-certified agency.

Covered services can include skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help (bathing, grooming, dressing-related assistance) — but aide care is only covered while you're also getting skilled nursing or therapy services.
Custodial or personal care alone, when it's the only care needed, is not covered.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether you meet the homebound and skilled-care criteria for a home health referral.
Request a face-to-face assessment and, if appropriate, a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've mentioned needing help with dressing and having a low monthly income, which are the kinds of situations Community First Choice (CFC) is meant to help with, but the screening doesn't yet know whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, or how many total activities of daily living (ADLs) you need help with — CFC generally requires an unmet need in at least three ADLs to continue receiving services.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Washington Medicaid State Plan benefit for personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, administered through Home and Community Services.

To qualify, you generally need a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and to continue receiving CFC at reassessment you need an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Financially, CFC requires eligibility for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage), not institutional Medicaid rules.

If eligible, CFC can cover help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus IADLs such as meal prep and housework, along with things like personal emergency response systems and caregiver training.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Washington Apple Health/Medicaid
How many activities of daily living you currently need help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional intake to request a CARE assessment and find out your Medicaid status.
Ask about applying for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) if you aren't already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your WWII-era service, discharge status, and asset level line up with what this benefit checks for, but the screening doesn't yet know whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability — the key clinical requirement for this specific benefit (and note it cannot be combined with Aid & Attendance, which appears to fit your situation better based on the dressing-assistance need).

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability, and it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.
The underlying pension eligibility (discharge status, net worth limit of $163,699, wartime service) is the same as for Aid & Attendance.
If it applies, it adds a monthly cash payment to the pension — for a veteran with no dependents, up to $21,313/year versus $17,441 standard, effective December 1, 2025.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA-affiliated medical examiner or Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound status better fits your situation, since only one can be received at a time.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, but this benefit specifically requires a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and the screening doesn't know whether you've had such a stay recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Qualifying requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, with no cost for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and no coverage after day 100 in the benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If you have had a recent 3+ night hospital admission, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay.
If there's been no recent hospital stay, this program likely isn't relevant right now, but keep it in mind if that changes.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55, and your reported income and asset levels appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits, but the screening doesn't yet know whether you meet the nursing-facility level of care requirement, and — importantly — TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025 with new applicants going onto a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers (it also serves eligible people with no unpaid caregiver).
Financial limits (effective as of 1/1/2026) allow gross income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 for a single person — higher than regular Medicaid limits.
You must be assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care, and cannot already be enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applications currently only place you on a first-come, first-served waitlist, with no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you meet the nursing-facility level of care requirement (based on how many ADLs you need help with)
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current TSOA waitlist and whether applying now makes sense.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds in the meantime, since those don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have an ongoing care need related to Parkinson's and needing help with dressing, which is the kind of situation these respite programs are meant to support caregivers through — but the screening doesn't know whether you have an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) providing you regular care, which is a central eligibility factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs in Washington offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for developmental disabilities originating before age 18).
Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, and can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, subject to available funding.
FCSP serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with services tailored through a caregiver specialist's assessment; amounts and rules vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing you regular care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: If someone provides you regular unpaid care, have them contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers or the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If there is no current unpaid caregiver, this program area likely doesn't apply yet, but keep it in mind if caregiving arrangements change.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
Besides dressing, do you need hands-on help with other daily activities like bathing, eating, toileting, transferring, or moving around the home? About how many in total?
Does anyone — a family member, friend, or neighbor — provide you regular unpaid care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours a week?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights (as an admitted inpatient, not just observation) in the last 30 days?
Do you have trouble leaving your home without help, or has a doctor said you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — an 88-year-old veteran in Mason County living at home with Parkinson's, needing help with toileting, on Medicare, single, with income between $1,000–$2,999/month and countable assets under $2,000 — two programs look like strong candidates to explore right away: VA Aid & Attendance and the Medicare home health benefit, since the information provided lines up with what these programs look for. Several other programs (Medicaid COPES, Medicaid Community First Choice, Apple Health long-term care, VA Housebound, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, TSOA, and respite care) could also be worth a conversation with the right agency, but key pieces of information are still missing — especially how many daily activities need help, the exact living situation, exact income, recent hospital stays, and whether there's an unpaid caregiver. Because so many functional and financial details remain unknown for the Medicaid-related and respite programs, more information is needed before this screening can say much about those — please treat this as a starting point for questions to ask a caseworker, not a determination of eligibility. Only DSHS, the VA, or Medicare can decide what your family member actually qualifies for.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is a veteran from the Gulf War era or later with a discharge that (per the profile) was not dishonorable, needs help with a daily activity (toileting), and has countable assets well under the $163,699 VA net worth limit — all of which align with what this benefit looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.
For a veteran with no dependents, the increased annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093 (effective December 1, 2025), compared to $17,441 without it.

The underlying pension requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, net worth within the $163,699 limit, and at least one day of service during a recognized wartime period, including the Gulf War era (8/2/1990–ongoing).

nextSteps: Complete VA Form 21P-527EZ to apply for the underlying pension
Have a medical provider complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting the need for aid and attendance
Contact a Veterans Service Officer (VSO) for free help preparing the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home (not a nursing facility), and has care needs from Parkinson's and toileting assistance that suggest a skilled or personal care need — all of which match what this benefit looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy, and home health aide help (such as bathing and grooming) at no cost for covered services, when ordered by a health care provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

Home health aide care (help with things like walking, bathing, and feeding) is only covered while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, speech-language pathology, or occupational therapy.
A face-to-face assessment and provider certification of homebound status and need for skilled care are required before services begin.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment and a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency
Confirm homebound status with the doctor, since this is a required condition

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 88 (meeting the 65+ age rule) and has countable assets under $2,000, which matches COPES's resource limit. Parkinson's and needing help with toileting suggest some functional need, but it's not yet clear whether a full assessment would find the nursing-facility level of care COPES requires, or whether the exact monthly income falls within the program's income limits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that adds services on top of basic personal care for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living — including adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation.

To move forward, an assessment must find a need for nursing-facility level of care, and income must be within Medicaid's Special Income Level (currently $2,982/month) or a lower Medically Needy limit; resources must be at or below $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, or transferring) need help, to see whether the required nursing-facility level of care standard is met
Confirmation of the current living situation (own home, adult family home, or assisted living)
The exact monthly income amount, to compare against Medicaid's income limits
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax
Consider asking a benefits counselor or Area Agency on Aging for help with the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with toileting is one of the activities CFC covers, and your family member's age and situation could fit, but it's unclear whether they are already enrolled in community Medicaid or whether they need help with the three or more daily activities CFC requires for ongoing eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice funds personal care help with daily activities such as toileting, bathing, dressing, and medication management, plus services like personal emergency response systems and caregiver training, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and qualify for community Medicaid.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
How many daily activities need help, since continuing CFC requires help with at least three

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 88 with assets under $2,000, your family member meets the age and resource rules for this Medicaid long-term care coverage, which also serves as the financial gateway to waivers like COPES. whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Medicaid coverage that serves as the financial gateway to home-and-community waivers (like COPES) and to nursing facility care, requiring a nursing-facility level of care assessment and income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month), or a lower Medically Needy limit.

Call regional HCS intake to schedule the CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: The veteran's wartime service era and discharge status appear to fit this benefit's basic pension requirements, but it isn't yet known whether they are substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, which is the specific test for Housebound status. This benefit also cannot be paid at the same time as Aid & Attendance, which already looks like a stronger fit.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly payment to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability; it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.
For a veteran with no dependents, the Housebound annual pension rate is $21,313 (effective December 1, 2025), compared to $17,441 standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the veteran is substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a VSO whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound status fits better, since only one can be paid at a time

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare Part A, but this benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and it isn't known whether such a stay has recently occurred.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing facility care — including skilled nursing, therapy, and related services — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.

nextSteps: Keep this benefit in mind mainly for after a hospital stay, not for ongoing daily care needs

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared, several Washington State and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look for this 86-year-old veteran in Stevens County who has heart disease, needs help with mobility, is on Medicare, and has income and assets that appear to fall within some program limits. However, several key details are still unknown (such as exactly how many daily activities require help, exact monthly income, living arrangement details, whether there is an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stay), so this screening is only a starting point, not a determination of eligibility. Only the agency that runs each program can decide who actually qualifies. The follow-up questions below would help sharpen this picture before contacting an agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, is living at home rather than in a nursing facility, and needs help with mobility, which points toward the kind of skilled or personal care support this benefit is designed to help with.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy), at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To use this benefit, a person generally needs a face-to-face assessment and provider certification confirming homebound status and a need for skilled care, plus a written care plan ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Homebound status means leaving home is not recommended or requires help (such as a cane, walker, or wheelchair) due to illness or injury, and normally leaving home takes considerable effort.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and need for skilled care
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies in Stevens County

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is 86 years old, which meets the age requirement for COPES, and has countable assets under $2,000, matching the program's resource limit. The stated income range of $1,000–$2,999/month falls at or below the program's Special Income Level ($2,982/month), though the exact figure isn't known. Needing help with mobility could point toward the kind of ongoing personal care support COPES is designed to help with, but whether the needs meet the program's nursing-facility-level-of-care threshold hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver run by DSHS Home and Community Services that adds services on top of personal care for people who need a nursing-facility level of care but want to stay in the community, in settings like a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To qualify, a person generally must be 65 or older (or have a qualifying disability), have countable assets at or below $2,000 (single), and have income at or below $2,982/month (or qualify through a related pathway).

Covered services can include adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, specialized medical equipment, skilled nursing services, and help with transitioning to a new living situation.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities the person needs help with, since this affects whether the required 'nursing-facility level of care' assessment would be met
The exact monthly income amount, to see how it compares with the program's income limit
More specific details about the current living situation
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person
Gather documentation on income, assets, and any past asset transfers or gifts

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is over 18 and enrolled in Medicare, and mobility needs suggest a possible personal-care need, which is the kind of support Community First Choice (CFC) is built around. However, it isn't yet known whether this person is enrolled in community Medicaid (a requirement for CFC) or how many activities of daily living require help.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is Washington's main Medicaid state-plan option for personal care for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, covering help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, and medication management, plus related supports.

Covered services include personal care, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, delivered at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many daily activities require help, since ongoing CFC eligibility depends on needing help with three or more
nextSteps: Ask HCS or the local Community Services Office whether Medicaid CN/ABP coverage applies
Request a CARE functional assessment through regional HCS central intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 86 years old with countable assets under $2,000, this person's age and resource level line up with two of the general requirements for Apple Health long-term care, which is the financial gateway to programs like COPES. The reported income range of $1,000–$2,999/month may be at or under the program's Special Income Level ($2,982/month), but the exact amount and the level of functional need for nursing-facility-level care are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to nursing facility care as well as HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS.

Financial eligibility generally requires countable assets at or below $2,000 (single) and income at or below the $2,982/month Special Income Level, or qualifying through a Medically Needy pathway if income is higher.
The application process involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a required interview, and a separate in-person CARE assessment by an HCS social worker to establish functional eligibility.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities the person needs help with, to assess nursing-facility-level-of-care status
The exact monthly income amount
More specific details about the current living situation
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application online, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office
Call the regional HCS central intake line (Stevens County is in Eastern Washington — Region 1: 509-568-3767 or 866-323-9409) to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, and a diagnosis of heart disease could, in some circumstances, lead to a hospital stay and a need for skilled nursing facility care afterward. However, it is not known whether there has been a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay, which is required for this benefit to apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
Covered services include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in the facility.
Costs for 2026 include $0 per day for days 1–20 and $217 per day coinsurance for days 21–100, with the patient responsible for all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been (or will be) a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has determined a need for daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay before admission
If there is no qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 86, this person meets TSOA's age-55-or-older requirement, and the reported income ($1,000–$2,999/month) and assets (under $2,000) both appear to fall within TSOA's higher income and resource limits ($3,976/month and $84,354 for a single person). Needing help with mobility could relate to the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement, but this hasn't been confirmed. Importantly, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic (CN/ABP) Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers if there is one, with no client cost-sharing.

Eligibility generally requires nursing-facility level of care, gross income at or below $3,976/month, and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person, and Medicaid CN/ABP coverage disqualifies a person from TSOA.

As of the most recent update, statewide enrollment is paused: new applications only establish a place on a first-come, first-served waitlist, though people already enrolled before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities the person needs help with, to assess whether the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement would be met
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about the current waitlist status and estimated wait
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting, since these don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person needs help with mobility and lives in a family member's home, which could involve an unpaid family caregiver, but whether someone is providing regular unpaid care — and how many hours per week — has not been reported, so this connection is uncertain.

whatItCovers.text: Respite care programs in Washington offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through channels like Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), generally requiring an unpaid caregiver providing regular care.

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP respite (through the local Area Agency on Aging) has no statewide cap and varies by county, covering options like in-home respite, adult day services, counseling, and caregiver training.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing regular unpaid care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about FCSP respite options
If there is an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or 253-442-3214)

followUpQuestions: How many daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, transferring, walking, or toileting) does the person need help with, and at what level of assistance?
What is the exact monthly income amount, rather than just the $1,000–$2,999 range, since some programs have income cutoffs within that range?
Can you describe the current living situation in more detail — for example, is it a private home, a licensed adult family home, or an assisted living facility?
Is there a family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more days recently, or is one expected, along with a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application been submitted?
