report.overallSummary: This screening looked at four programs based on what you shared: you are 75, live in your own home in Jefferson County, need help with transferring, are a Vietnam-era veteran, are divorced, have monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999, have countable assets of $160,000 or more, and are covered by Medicare. Several important pieces of information are still missing — whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone provides you unpaid care. Because so much is still unknown for some programs, this report leans on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the agency that runs each program can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, live at home in Jefferson County rather than in a facility, and have a noted care need (help with transferring). These match the three basic conditions this program looks for, though a full homebound and skilled-care determination still needs to be made by a health care provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language services, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help walking, bathing, or grooming) when it's paired with skilled care.
Covered home health services cost $0, though durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To qualify, a person must be certified as homebound and need part-time or intermittent skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment and an order from a health care provider, delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care alone when that is the only care needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or another qualifying provider about a face-to-face assessment to determine homebound status and skilled-care need.
Ask your provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 75, live in your own home in Jefferson County, and need help with transferring, which is one of the activities of daily living this program covers. However, it isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, or whether you need help with three or more daily activities, both of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid option for personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, covering help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and transferring, plus things like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

To keep receiving these services at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.
Services can be provided in your own home, an adult family home, or several other community settings, and are meant to supplement — not replace — Medicare, other insurance, or informal support.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or able to enroll in) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many daily activities of daily living you need ongoing help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional central intake to ask about a CARE assessment.
Check with DSHS or a benefits counselor about whether you might qualify for community Medicaid, given your reported income and assets, since eligibility can only be confirmed by the agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but it isn't known whether you've had a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — the key trigger for this type of coverage. Nothing in your profile currently points to a recent hospitalization.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and medications during a short-term stay in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying hospital stay.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not counting observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Costs in 2026 include no charge for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask the hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a follow-up stay in a skilled nursing facility before admission.
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, this program likely isn't relevant right now — consider focusing on home-based options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have an ongoing care need (help with transferring), which is one condition for these programs, but it isn't known whether an unpaid family member or friend regularly provides you care — a requirement for the caregiver-support programs described here. Your profile doesn't mention a caregiver.

whatItCovers.text: These programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and, only for people with a developmental disability, DDA waiver respite.

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP respite amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, covering options like in-home respite or adult day services.

These programs generally require the caregiver to be unpaid and providing regular (often 40+ hours/week for Lifespan vouchers) care, and are aimed at supporting the caregiver rather than the person receiving care directly.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) who regularly provides you care or supervision
nextSteps: If someone in your life provides you unpaid care, they could contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If there is no regular unpaid caregiver currently, this type of program is likely not applicable right now.

Besides help with transferring, do you need regular help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or using the toilet?

report.overallSummary: Based on the details shared — an 85-year-old in Island County living in a family member's home, needing help with bathing and toileting, diagnosed with dementia, a Korean-era veteran, single, with monthly income reported as $1,000–$2,999, countable assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage — several programs look worth exploring further with the right agency. VA Aid & Attendance, the Medicare home health benefit, and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) appear to line up closely with the details given, though TSOA currently has a statewide enrollment pause with a waitlist. Washington Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Community First Choice, Apple Health LTC) may also be worth exploring, but the exact monthly income figure and a few other details are still needed to know how closely they fit. This screening only identifies programs to look into — it cannot determine eligibility for any program; only the administering agency can do that. Some follow-up questions are listed below to help sharpen the picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit was flagged because the profile describes a veteran who served during the Korean era (a recognized wartime period), needs another person's help with daily activities like bathing and toileting, and has countable assets well under the VA's $163,699 net worth limit — details that line up with the program's screening rules.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing or dressing, or who otherwise meet a clinical criterion such as being bedridden or in a nursing home.

Underlying pension eligibility requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, service including at least one day during a recognized wartime period such as the Korean Conflict (6/27/1950–1/31/1955), and net worth within the $163,699 limit (effective 12/1/2025).

The payment amount is based on the difference between countable income and a Maximum Annual Pension Rate; for a veteran with no dependents receiving A&A, the MAPR is $29,093/year effective December 1, 2025.

nextSteps: Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to file VA Form 21P-527EZ for the pension along with VA Form 21-2680 for the medical examination.
Gather military service records and discharge paperwork.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit was flagged because the profile notes Medicare coverage, a need for help with bathing and toileting, and living in a family member's home rather than a nursing facility — details that align with this benefit's basic rules.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit covers part-time skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming, delivered by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost to the beneficiary for covered services.

To qualify, a person needs a provider-certified need for part-time or intermittent skilled care and must meet Medicare's definition of homebound — normally unable to leave home without help, with leaving requiring considerable effort.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment and homebound status certification.
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Island County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: TSOA was flagged because the profile describes an 85-year-old (well over the program's age-55 threshold) with needs — help with bathing, toileting, and a dementia diagnosis — that can factor into a nursing-facility level of care finding, and income/assets in the profile ($1,000–$2,999/month, under $2,000 in assets) fall comfortably within TSOA's higher limits. Importantly, though, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income; $84,354 resources for a single person).

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed for immediate services, though people already enrolled keep their services.

nextSteps: Submit an application to establish a place on the statewide waitlist, since enrollment is currently paused.
Contact the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This screening flagged COPES because the profile describes an 85-year-old (meeting the program's 65+ age rule), needing help with bathing and toileting, and a dementia diagnosis — details that can factor into a nursing-facility level of care assessment — plus countable assets under $2,000, matching the resource limit. The exact monthly income (reported only as a $1,000–$2,999 range) isn't yet clear enough to know where it falls compared to the program's income threshold.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver, operated by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds extra home and community-based services on top of personal care — including adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing services, transportation, and home modifications — for people in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be age 65+ (or younger with a qualifying disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care — which can include an unmet ADL need in bathing or toileting, or cognitive impairment such as dementia plus an ADL need — and have countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.
Financially, income generally must be at or below Washington's Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026), though a Medically Needy pathway may apply for income above that.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against Washington's Special Income Level of $2,982/month
nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) to submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) and request a CARE assessment.
Gather income documentation to check against the Special Income Level.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Community First Choice was flagged because the profile mentions needing help with bathing and toileting, which are personal-care ADLs this program covers, along with residence in Washington. Whether current Medicaid enrollment status and the total number of ADLs needing help meet CFC's specific tests isn't yet known from the profile.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Washington Medicaid State Plan benefit that pays for personal care help with activities like bathing, toileting, and dressing, plus related supports such as a personal emergency response system and caregiver management training, for people meeting a nursing-facility level of care.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan)
The total number of daily activities needing help with (bathing and toileting are noted so far; ongoing CFC eligibility looks at 3 or more)
nextSteps: Check current Medicaid enrollment status with the Health Care Authority.
Ask for a CARE assessment to document the full list of ADL needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program surfaced for reasons similar to COPES: the 85-year-old age, help needed with bathing and toileting, and countable assets under $2,000 line up with several of the program's rules. The exact monthly income (given only as a $1,000–$2,999 range) still needs to be checked against the income limit.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage that serves as the financial gateway to home and community waivers like COPES, and to nursing facility and PACE coverage, for people who meet functional and financial rules.

Financial eligibility generally requires income at or below the $2,982/month Special Income Level and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with a Medically Needy pathway available for income above that level.

Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online at washingtonconnection.org) plus a functional CARE assessment requested through the regional HCS intake line; Island County falls under Region 2.

nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or call HCS Region 2 intake (Island County) at 800-780-7094 to request a CARE assessment.
Confirm the exact monthly income against program limits.

Note that a person can receive Housebound or Aid & Attendance, but not both.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly payment to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability; it cannot be received together with Aid & Attendance.
The underlying pension rules — discharge status, wartime service, and the net worth limit — are the same as for Aid & Attendance.

nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance is the better fit, since only one can be received.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is noted in the profile, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, the profile doesn't mention a recent hospital stay, which is required to trigger this specific coverage — so it's unclear whether this applies right now.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
Days 1–20 have no daily charge; days 21–100 carry a $217/day coinsurance (2026); after day 100 the person pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an inpatient
nextSteps: Confirm whether there has been a hospital stay of 3+ consecutive inpatient days.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Respite programs were flagged because the profile describes ongoing care needs from dementia and help with bathing and toileting, and the person lives in a family member's home — which suggests there may be an unpaid caregiver, though the profile doesn't state this directly.

whatItCovers.text: Washington's respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program, which offers planned short-term care plus caregiver training and support groups.

Lifespan Respite requires the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40+ hours/week of care; Family Caregiver Support Program eligibility and service amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are based on a caregiver assessment.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program for Island County.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 88-year-old living at home in Franklin County who needs help with bathing and eating, has dementia, is covered by Medicare, and has income and assets in the ranges given — five programs came up as worth a closer look. Two (Medicare home health and Tailored Supports for Older Adults) look like a good match based on the details you've given, though TSOA currently has a waitlist. Three others (Medicaid Community First Choice, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs) could be relevant too, but we're missing a few pieces of information — like whether you're on community Medicaid, exactly how many daily activities you need help with, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether someone is providing unpaid care for you — that would help sharpen the picture. This screening cannot determine eligibility for any program; only the agency that runs each program can do that. Please treat this as a starting point for conversations with those agencies, not a decision.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare, live at home in Franklin County (not in a nursing facility), and have care needs including help with bathing and eating due to dementia. Medicare's home health benefit is designed for people in exactly this kind of situation — needing part-time skilled or personal care while living at home.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with bathing, eating, and similar tasks — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT is also being provided.
It also covers durable medical equipment and supplies for home use, with no cap on the number of visits while the person continues to qualify.
To qualify, a provider must certify a homebound status and order skilled care after a face-to-face assessment, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; Medicare-covered durable medical equipment costs 20% of the approved amount after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or a nurse practitioner for a face-to-face assessment to see whether homebound status and a skilled care need can be certified
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 88 years old with a monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999 and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000, your financial details appear to fall within TSOA's published limits (income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 for a single person). Needing help with bathing and eating is consistent with the kind of care need TSOA is meant to support. You live in Franklin County, Washington.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers; it also serves people who have no unpaid caregiver.

Eligibility involves a functional assessment for nursing-facility level of care and financial limits well above regular Medicaid — income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person (as of 1/1/2026).
There is no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-services programs, and eligibility is normally certified for 12 months.

Important: statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025. New applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional review happens only once called from the waitlist; people already enrolled before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 to ask about current TSOA waitlist status in Franklin County
Consider submitting a TSOA application (online via Washington Connection or by mailing form HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist
Ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid respite funding options that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget while waiting

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've mentioned needing help with bathing and eating, are 88 years old, live in your own home in Franklin County, and are covered by Medicare. Community First Choice (CFC) is a Medicaid option for people who need a nursing-facility level of care but want to stay in the community. Whether it applies depends on two things we don't yet know: whether you're enrolled in (or can enroll in) community Medicaid, and how many activities of daily living you need help with, since ongoing CFC requires need in at least three.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and personal hygiene, along with related support such as meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and help transitioning between care settings.
To qualify functionally, an assessment must show a nursing-facility level of care, and to continue receiving CFC at reassessment there must be unmet or partly met need in at least three ADLs.
Financially, CFC requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan community Medicaid, not institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or able to enroll in) community Medicaid, known as Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) coverage
How many activities of daily living you need help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask HCS or a Medicaid caseworker whether you may qualify for community Medicaid if you aren't already enrolled

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and we don't currently know whether such a stay has happened or is expected.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.
Days 1-20 have no daily cost under Original Medicare; days 21-100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026), and the person pays all costs after day 100.
A qualifying stay means a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row; time spent under observation status does not count.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been (or is expected to be) a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days followed by a need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it counts as a qualifying inpatient stay for SNF coverage

programs.whyThisMayApply: Because of dementia, you have care needs that involve ongoing supervision. Respite programs are designed to give relief to unpaid caregivers who provide this kind of regular care, but we don't yet know whether you currently have an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) helping you.

whatItCovers.text: Respite care in Washington is available mainly through three channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for people with a developmental disability that began before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, and provide up to $1,000 per household per year, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite or adult day services.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility, plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment; amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, with no statewide cap published.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) providing regular care or supervision

Ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers if an unpaid caregiver is providing 40 or more hours of care per week

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 73, live in your own home in Walla Walla County, have Medicare, a dementia diagnosis, and need help with transferring and dressing, with monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; none of these programs can be confirmed as available to you until the administering agency reviews your full application. programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare and live at home, and you have care needs (help with transferring and dressing) that could indicate a need for skilled or personal care services — the rules engine found all three basic criteria met for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B home health can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and durable medical equipment, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, speech-language pathology, or occupational therapy — it is not available as stand-alone personal care.
To qualify, a health care provider must do a face-to-face assessment, certify a homebound status, and order the care, which must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether you have a skilled care need (such as a health condition requiring nursing or therapy) that would support a home health referral
Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment and homebound-status certification to start the referral process
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Walla Walla County from your provider

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 73 (meeting the 55+ age requirement), your monthly income under $1,000 is within the program's $3,976/month limit, your countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 fall within the $84,354 single resource limit, and your care needs (help with transferring and dressing, dementia) suggest a nursing-facility level of care that this program is designed around. The screening also found you are not currently on CN/ABP Medicaid, which fits this program's requirement.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid, and also serves people with no unpaid caregiver.

Eligibility requires being age 55+, meeting nursing facility level of care rules, and having income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 (single) as of 1/1/2026; people already on CN/ABP Medicaid are not eligible for TSOA.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments only happen when called from the waitlist, though people already enrolled before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by submitting form HCA 18-008 to get on the statewide waitlist and establish your request date
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a Family Caregiver Support Program respite screening while waiting, since it does not depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need hands-on help with transferring and dressing, which are two of the ADLs this program is built around, and your monthly income under $1,000 is in the range this program's community Medicaid financial rules often cover. However, it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid CN/ABP coverage, or whether your total ADL need count is three or more, both of which the program requires to continue services long-term.

whatItCovers.text: This program provides Medicaid-funded personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, covering help with ADLs like dressing, transferring, and bed mobility, as well as IADLs, health-related tasks, relief care, nurse delegation, PERS, and caregiver training.

To continue receiving CFC services at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility generally requires Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is one of the two requirements for this benefit. However, it's not known whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of three or more days along with a doctor's order for daily skilled nursing care, which this benefit also requires — and this benefit is meant for short-term rehabilitation after a hospitalization, not ongoing at-home dementia or ADL care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain ambulance transportation, with no charge for days 1-20 and a daily coinsurance for days 21-100.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — a 72-year-old veteran in Snohomish County living at home, needing help with bathing, dressing and toileting, covered by Medicare, with monthly income in the $1,000–$2,999 range and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. A few programs (VA Aid & Attendance, Medicare home health, PACE, and Tailored Supports for Older Adults) line up well with the facts given, though TSOA currently has a statewide enrollment pause you should know about. Other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, VA Housebound, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs) may also be relevant, but the screening is missing a few pieces of information — like Medicaid enrollment status, whether you are considered 'housebound,' any recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved — that would help narrow things down. This is only an educational screening; none of the agencies involved have made an eligibility determination, and only they can confirm whether you qualify for any program. Please review the follow-up questions below and consider reaching out to the agencies listed in the next-steps for each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a veteran with Gulf War-era or later service, needing help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, and toileting, and assets reported between $2,000 and $85,000 (within the VA's current net worth limit of $163,699), you appear to meet the pattern this benefit is designed for. The rules engine found every checked factor — veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, help needed with daily activities, and net worth — came back as a match.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans who need help with daily activities, and the money can be used for care costs at your discretion; it cannot be received at the same time as the VA Housebound benefit.

For a veteran with no dependents, the Maximum Annual Pension Rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093 per year (effective December 1, 2025), versus $17,441 without it, with the VA paying the difference between countable income and this limit.

nextSteps: Gather VA Form 21-2680 (to be completed by a medical examiner) and file VA Form 21P-527EZ for the underlying pension, along with income, asset, and service history documentation.
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to begin the application and confirm current details.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, live at home rather than in a nursing facility, and have reported needing help with personal care tasks like bathing, dressing, and toileting — all factors the rules engine found matched this benefit's requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care for things like bathing and grooming — but aide care is only covered while skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT is also being provided.

Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary, though durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible; the benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that's the only care needed.
To use this benefit, a health care provider must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and services must be ordered and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether you would be considered 'homebound' under Medicare's definition and whether a skilled care need (like nursing or therapy) could be certified.
If so, ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 72 and living in Snohomish County — one of the four Washington counties with PACE coverage — and needing help with multiple ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting) that suggests a nursing-facility level of care, you match the pattern the rules engine flagged as likely relevant.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, coordinated by an interdisciplinary team, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community; Washington has PACE organizations covering parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.

Snohomish County coverage spans 21 ZIP codes served by Providence PACE (which has an Everett South center), but coverage within the county is not complete, so a specific ZIP code must be checked against the DSHS coverage list or with the organization's intake line.
Enrollment requires state certification of nursing-facility level of care and a financial eligibility determination by HCS; services start the first of the month after both are complete.

nextSteps: Check whether your specific ZIP code in Snohomish County is within a PACE service area, using the DSHS PACE ZIP code list or by calling Providence PACE intake at (206) 320-5325.
Ask HCS about starting the nursing-facility level of care assessment and financial eligibility review.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 72, with monthly income ($1,000–$2,999) below the $3,976 TSOA limit and assets ($2,000–$85,000) below the $84,354 single-person resource limit, and needing help with ADLs suggesting a nursing-facility level of care, you match several TSOA criteria the rules engine checked. Importantly, though, TSOA has been under a statewide enrollment pause since December 1, 2025.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, using income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, with no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure.

As of the current retrieval, enrollment is paused statewide: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional assessments only happen once someone is called from the waitlist; people already enrolled or approved before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or apply through washingtonconnection.org to be placed on the statewide TSOA waitlist and establish your request date.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds in the meantime, since those don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing help with bathing, dressing, and toileting — three ADLs — which matches the substantial-need requirement this program looks for at reassessment. However, it's not yet known whether you are enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP), which is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This is Washington's Medicaid State Plan option for personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, requiring a CARE assessment showing unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue services.

It covers help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, transferring, etc.), some IADLs when ADL help is also needed, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, and can be provided in your own home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or would be eligible for, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) regional intake to ask about your Medicaid enrollment status and to request a CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, and net worth all matched this benefit's criteria, but it's not yet known whether you are 'substantially confined to home' by a permanent disability, which is the specific clinical test for this program. Note that Housebound and Aid & Attendance cannot both be received — only one applies at a time.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and cannot be received alongside Aid & Attendance.
For a veteran with no dependents, the Maximum Annual Pension Rate with Housebound status is $21,313 per year (effective December 1, 2025), versus $17,441 standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are considered substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability.
nextSteps: Discuss with the VA or a Veterans Service Officer whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance would be the better fit for your situation, since you can only receive one.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, but whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — the key requirement for this benefit — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status doesn't count), admission within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

Covered costs include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medications and supplies used in the facility, with $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026), and no coverage beyond day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient, with an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If you've had a recent hospital stay, ask the hospital discharge staff or your doctor whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay.
If there's been no qualifying hospital stay, this program likely doesn't apply right now; ask about Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits as alternatives.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing ongoing help with ADLs, which points to a need for care or supervision, but it isn't yet known whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) is regularly providing that care — which is central to these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers respite care through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP, with amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging — roughly $1,000–$4,362/year in Snohomish County on a sliding fee scale), and DDA waiver respite for people with a developmental disability that began before age 18.

Lifespan Respite vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, while FCSP eligibility and service levels vary locally and are tailored through a caregiver-specialist screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (family member, friend, or similar) is regularly providing your care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: If an unpaid caregiver is involved, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the Snohomish County Area Agency on Aging (Homage Senior Services) to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program respite options.
Ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers through wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington if a caregiver is providing 40+ hours per week of care.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
Do you spend most of your time at home because of a permanent disability, or would you say you rarely leave home without help?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient recently, with a doctor saying you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward?

What is your specific ZIP code within Snohomish County, so we can check whether it falls inside a PACE organization's service area?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a 54-year-old in Pacific County who lives at home, is married, has a dementia diagnosis, needs help with bathing, is covered by Medicare, has countable assets under $2,000, and a monthly household income between $3,000 and $3,999. Several of the Medicaid-related programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) share eligibility rules that depend on information this screening does not yet have — such as whether a formal disability determination applies, how many daily activities require help, and the exact monthly income figure. Because those pieces are still unknown, this report leans on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations for those three programs. The Medicare home health benefit appears more clearly worth a look because Medicare coverage, a personal-care need, and living at home are already confirmed. Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit and Washington's respite care programs may also be worth exploring, but each depends on details (a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver such as a spouse is providing regular care) that have not been answered yet. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency for each program can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, live at home, and need help with bathing — all of which line up with the basic requirements for this benefit (Medicare enrollment, an indicated care need, and living outside a nursing facility). Whether the specific homebound and skilled-care conditions are met would still need to be confirmed with a health care provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B cover part-time or intermittent skilled care at home — including skilled nursing, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and durable medical equipment — at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide care (help with bathing, walking, grooming, etc.) is covered only while skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT is also being received — it is not covered as a stand-alone personal-care benefit.

To qualify, a provider must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment, and care must be ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment of homebound status and skilled-care need.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Pacific County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned a dementia diagnosis and needing help with bathing, live in your own home in Pacific County, are married, and have countable assets under $2,000 — which already meets COPES's resource limit. Your income of $3,000–$3,999/month is close to the program's $2,982 Special Income Level, so it's worth checking exactly where it falls. COPES is designed for people who need nursing-facility-level care but want to stay in the community, and cognitive impairment (like memory issues) combined with an unmet daily-activity need can factor into that determination — but this hasn't been assessed yet, so no conclusion can be drawn from this screening alone.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's largest Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and adds extra services on top of personal care for people who qualify for nursing-facility-level care but choose to stay in the community.

It can cover things like adult day care and adult day health, wellness/behavior support training, help transitioning into a new home (like rental deposits or utility hookups), environmental modifications, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.
Services can be received in a private home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and cannot duplicate what Medicare or other insurance already covers.

The level-of-care assessment can find a qualifying need through cognitive impairment such as memory loss or disorientation when combined with an unmet or partially met need in a daily living activity.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the age or disability criteria the program requires (being 65 or older, or having a qualifying disability determination) applies

Ask DSHS or an Area Agency on Aging about how the dementia diagnosis and bathing-assistance need might factor into a level-of-care assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with bathing and have a dementia diagnosis, and your countable assets are under $2,000. CFC is a Medicaid personal-care option for people who meet a nursing-facility-level-of-care standard, but whether it applies depends on Medicaid enrollment status and the number of daily activities needing help — both currently unknown from your profile.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice is a Medicaid state plan benefit (not capped by waiver slots) that covers personal care help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and getting around, plus related tasks like meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It can also cover relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training.
Services can be provided in your own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, and CFC does not replace Medicare, other insurance, or informal support.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid coverage
How many daily living activities currently need help
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status.
Contact HCS central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
If already on Apple Health, ask what additional information (care needs, diagnoses, medications) is needed for the assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your countable assets are under $2,000, meeting this program's resource limit, and your income of $3,000–$3,999/month is near the $2,982 Special Income Level threshold used for this coverage. Apple Health LTC is the financial gateway to waiver programs like COPES, so whether it applies depends on the same open questions about age/disability status, level-of-care need, and exact income.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care, and it serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers such as COPES, PACE, and other community LTSS programs.

Eligibility involves meeting general Apple Health requirements, a functional (nursing-facility-level-of-care) assessment, and either SSI-related or income-based financial criteria, with a Special Income Level of $2,982/month and a Medically Needy pathway for income above that.
The resource limit is $2,000 for a single person, with home equity up to $1,130,000 allowed and a 60-month look-back on asset transfers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the age or disability criteria (65+, or a qualifying disability determination) applies
How many daily living activities currently need help
More detail about the current living situation
The exact monthly income amount
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online, by mail/fax, or in person.
Call regional HCS central intake to schedule the required financial interview and CARE functional assessment.
Ask about the Medically Needy pathway if income turns out to be above the Special Income Level.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, which meets the Part A enrollment requirement. However, this benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and your profile does not indicate whether such a stay has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.
Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, PT/OT/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days requiring ongoing daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare is likely to cover a following SNF stay.
If no qualifying hospital stay applies, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned a dementia diagnosis and a need for help with bathing, which suggests ongoing care or supervision needs, and you are married — a spouse may be providing unpaid care, though this has not been confirmed. Respite programs are aimed at giving unpaid family caregivers a break, so whether this applies depends on confirming who is providing care and how many hours per week.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options) for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours of care per week, when the caregiver can't afford respite on their own and isn't already receiving respite through another program.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers free or low-cost planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility, plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies — with service levels and funding varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program and a TCARE screening.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs based on what you shared: you are 75, live in Garfield County, WA, in a family member's home, need help with eating, dressing, and toileting, have heart disease, are covered by Medicare, and have income in the $3,000–$3,999/month range with assets between $2,000 and $85,000. A few key facts are still missing (such as whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is helping you), so some of these results are only partial. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the agency that runs each program can determine that. Please review the questions below and talk with a benefits counselor or the listed agencies to fill in the gaps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, live in a family member's home (not a nursing facility), and need help with several personal care tasks — all of which line up with this benefit's basic conditions. A remaining piece to confirm is whether a doctor considers you 'homebound' and has ordered skilled care, which is required for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B pays for part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, feeding, walking) — but aide help only counts if you're also getting skilled nursing or therapy.
To qualify, a provider must certify homebound status and order the care after a face-to-face assessment, and the care must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
Documentation typically includes the face-to-face visit note, a signed plan of care (CMS-485), and in Washington a POLST form if you have one.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether you meet Medicare's 'homebound' definition and whether a home health referral makes sense.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Garfield County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with eating, dressing, and toileting — three ADLs — which matches the substantial-ADL-need rule this program uses for ongoing services. However, whether you are currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is unknown, and that financial category is a required piece of this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings including a private home or adult family home.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must show a nursing-facility level of care, and to continue receiving CFC at reassessment the person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Financially, CFC requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan community Medicaid coverage — not the special institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to start a financial application and request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or a benefits counselor to check your current Medicaid enrollment status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this program covers short-term rehabilitation after a hospital stay, and it's unknown whether you've had a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay recently — without that, this benefit would not apply to your current at-home care needs.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, and related supplies in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row and when a doctor determines daily skilled care is needed.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period: no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance of $217 for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100 (2026 figures).
Documentation generally includes proof of the 3-day inpatient stay, a doctor's order for daily skilled care, and in Washington a PASRR Level I screening at admission.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has ordered daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay (not observation status).
If no recent hospital stay applies, consider discussing other options like the Medicare home health benefit or Medicaid personal care instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are over 55 and your described needs (help with eating, dressing, toileting) suggest a level of care this program looks at, and your resources appear to be within its limits. Your exact monthly income wasn't confirmed against the program's $3,976/month threshold, and importantly, this program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed for immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits higher than regular Medicaid (as of 1/1/2026: income at or below $3,976/month, resources at or below $84,354 for a single person).
There is no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure for this program, and eligibility is reassessed annually.

As of the most recent update, enrollment is paused statewide; new applications are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and presumptive-eligibility screenings are suspended, with people already enrolled before December 1, 2025 keeping their services.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income, to compare against the program's $3,976/month limit.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or apply through washingtonconnection.org to be placed on the waitlist and get your request date recorded.
Ask your local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds while the TSOA waitlist is in effect.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need ongoing help with several daily activities, which fits the general idea behind respite programs — support for whoever is providing your care. However, it's unknown whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, which is a key requirement for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers up to $1,000 per household per year for an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, etc.).

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) plus support groups, counseling, training, and supplies for caregivers of adults 18+, with amounts and rules varying by county's Area Agency on Aging.
Lifespan applications are largely self-reported with no income verification or attachments required, based on yes/no certifications on the application form.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program services in Garfield County.
If there is an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week, consider having them look into a Lifespan Respite Washington voucher application.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 64-year-old in Clallam County who lives at home, has dementia, needs help with transferring, eating, and dressing, is married, has Medicare, and has income and assets in the ranges you gave — several Washington programs may be worth a closer look. Medicare home health and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) match well on the details you've provided, though TSOA currently has a statewide enrollment pause with a waitlist. The Medicaid-based programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) could also be relevant given the daily-activity and dementia-related needs described, but several key facts — especially whether the disability/age criteria are met, the exact income and asset amounts, and current Medicaid enrollment — are still unknown, so it isn't yet clear whether those programs' basic requirements are met. Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage and respite care programs depend on facts (a recent hospital stay, and whether there's an unpaid caregiver) that haven't been answered yet. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the administering agency can determine that. Gathering the missing details below and speaking with the agencies directly are the best next steps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare and needs help with several daily activities (transferring, eating, dressing) and has dementia, which points toward a possible skilled or personal-care need. They live at home, not in a nursing facility, which matches this benefit's setting.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only alongside skilled nursing or therapy), plus durable medical equipment, for people who qualify.

To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended) and need part-time or intermittent skilled care, certified through a face-to-face assessment and ordered by a health care provider.
Covered home health services cost $0; for covered durable medical equipment, the person generally pays 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and skilled-care need.
If a need is confirmed, ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is over age 55, and the described income and asset ranges fall within TSOA's higher financial limits ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 single/$156,883 married resource limits). The described daily-activity needs and dementia diagnosis align with the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement this program screens for, and being married may also mean a caregiver could be considered as part of a caregiver/receiver pair.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers (it can also serve people with no unpaid caregiver); income limits and resource limits are set well above regular Medicaid amounts — $3,976/month income and $84,354 (single) or $156,883 (married with a community spouse) in resources as of 1/1/2026.
There is no cost-sharing for TSOA and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to some other Medicaid long-term-care programs; eligibility is certified for 12 months at a time.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants currently go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced date for the pause to end.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by submitting the HCA 18-008 form to get on the statewide waitlist and establish a request date.
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite options while waiting, since TSOA's regular budget is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes needing help with transferring, eating, and dressing, along with a dementia diagnosis — this fits the kind of daily-activity and cognitive-impairment picture COPES considers when checking for a nursing-facility level of care need. whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care — for example adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, and transportation — for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a need at the nursing-facility level of care — which can include unmet help needs in several daily activities (like eating, transferring, or bathing) or cognitive impairment such as memory problems combined with an unmet daily-activity need.

Financially, the program looks at whether income is at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and whether countable resources are at or below $2,000 for a single person (with higher protections for a spouse still living in the community).

Ask HCS directly how the disability/age rule and financial limits would apply in this specific situation.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and medication management, along with related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and training for family members who manage care.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and physical/occupational/speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — an 85-year-old in Grays Harbor County with dementia who needs help with eating and toileting, is married, has Medicare and Medicaid (Apple Health), and has income and assets in the ranges given — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. Some, like the Medicare home health benefit, match well with the information provided. Others, like the Medicaid waivers, TSOA, MAC, and respite programs, could be a good fit but depend on a few details that haven't been confirmed yet — like exact income and asset figures, whether a family member provides unpaid care, and how many daily activities need help. This screening cannot determine eligibility for any program; only the agency that runs each program can do that. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening more useful, and speaking with the Home and Community Services (HCS) office or the local Area Agency on Aging is a good next step either way.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Community First Choice was flagged because the family already has Medicaid (Apple Health) coverage, which is the main financial gateway for this program, and the 85-year-old needs help with eating and toileting — activities CFC is designed to support. Confirming the total number of daily activities needing help (three or more) would clarify ongoing fit.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with daily activities like eating, toileting, bathing, dressing, and transferring, plus some household tasks, and can include relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for managing caregivers.

Eligibility requires being enrolled in community (non-institutional) Medicaid and a CARE assessment finding a nursing-facility level of care; continuing CFC after a review requires an unmet or partially met need in three or more daily activities.

nextSteps: Request a CARE assessment through the regional HCS intake line

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program looks relevant because the person already has Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and needs help with personal care tasks like eating and toileting — all consistent with what Medicare home health supports, once a doctor confirms homebound status and orders the care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health covers part-time skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (like feeding and bathing) when paired with skilled care, generally at no cost to the person for covered services.
To qualify, a doctor must do a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status, and order the care from a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Durable medical equipment used at home is covered at 80%, with the person responsible for 20% after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment and a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency

programs.whyThisMayApply: Tailored Supports for Older Adults was flagged because the person is over 55 and the reported income ($1,000–$2,999/month) and assets ($85,000–$160,000) fall within TSOA's higher limits for a married applicant. However, TSOA generally excludes people already on regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, and the family reports having Medicaid (Apple Health) coverage, so it isn't clear this program would apply. TSOA's income and resource limits are higher than regular Medicaid — up to $3,976/month in income and, for a married applicant with a community spouse, up to $156,883 in resources (as of 1/1/2026).
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, with no announced reopening date.

whatItCovers.text: MAC offers caregiver assistance benefits in steps — a one-time $250 amount, up to $500/year, and up to roughly $928/month (capped at $5,568 over six months for 7/1/2025–6/30/2026) — covering things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, training, equipment, and counseling for the caregiver.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of regular Medicaid personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, transportation, and skilled nursing, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at seven Washington programs that might help with the 84-year-old's care needs in Skagit County — help with dressing, mobility, and toileting, along with a dementia diagnosis. The family already has Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid), which are the doorways into several of these programs. Because the countable assets ($85,000–$160,000) and exact monthly income aren't fully worked out against each program's specific rules, and because a few details (like whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, or a recent hospital stay) are still unknown, this report should be treated as a starting point for conversations with the administering agencies — not as a determination of what the family will receive. Nothing here means the family is or is not eligible; only Washington's Health Care Authority, DSHS, or Medicare can make that call.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family reports already being covered by Apple Health (Medicaid), and the described needs — help with dressing, mobility, and toileting — match three of the ADL categories CFC uses to determine ongoing eligibility. Because the rules engine found no missing information for this program, it appears to be a strong fit to explore.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and mobility, along with related tasks like medication management and some housework support.

It also covers relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training, and can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services.

CFC is a Medicaid state plan benefit (not capped by waiver slots), and people already active on Apple Health generally don't need a new financial application — only a functional (CARE) assessment showing nursing-facility level of care with at least three ADL needs.

nextSteps: Since Apple Health coverage is already in place, contact regional HCS central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask about combining CFC with COPES if additional in-home services beyond personal care are needed.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family reports Medicare coverage and needs help with mobility, dressing, and toileting while living in a family member's home. This matches the basic shape of the Medicare home health benefit, which covers part-time skilled care and aide services for people who have trouble leaving home. No information gaps were flagged for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, and grooming) — but aide care is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.
To qualify, a provider must certify the person is homebound (trouble leaving home without help, or leaving isn't medically advised) and needs skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the primary doctor about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Skagit County from the doctor or hospital.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 84 with help needed for dressing, mobility, and toileting, plus a dementia diagnosis, the profile lines up with the kind of functional needs (multiple ADL needs and cognitive impairment) that COPES uses to screen people in. The family also already has Medicare and Apple Health, which is often the starting point for this waiver. However, COPES has its own income and resource tests that are separate from general Apple Health eligibility, and those specific numbers aren't yet confirmed for this family.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services on top of basic Medicaid personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, nursing services, and help transitioning to a new home setting.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility-level care — this can include having unmet needs in several daily activities (like eating, toileting, bathing, or moving around) or cognitive impairment such as memory loss or disorientation that requires supervision, alongside an unmet ADL need.

Financially, COPES generally requires income at or below $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, though there are special rules for married couples and a medically-needy pathway for those with slightly higher income.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility, including nursing care, therapy, meals, and medical social services.
This benefit requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not counting observation status), followed by SNF admission generally within about 30 days.
