report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs for a 66-year-old in Clallam County who lives at home, has Medicare, needs help with toileting after a stroke, and has income and assets in the ranges described. Based on what's known so far, a Medicare-related benefit (home health) looks worth a closer look right away, and several Washington state programs may also be worth investigating once a few more details are confirmed — such as Medicaid enrollment status, how many daily activities need help, any recent hospital stays, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved. None of this is an eligibility decision; only the administering agency (Medicare, DSHS/HCS, or the local Area Agency on Aging) can confirm eligibility and benefit amounts. Because several key facts are still unknown, the follow-up questions below are especially important for getting a clearer picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you have a stroke diagnosis with an identified care need, and you live at home rather than in a facility — these are the three factors this benefit checks, and all three appear to match your situation as described.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology delivered at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and walking is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy — it does not cover 24-hour care or personal care alone.

To use this benefit, a health care provider must certify that the person is homebound and needs skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment, and the care must be ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor or nurse practitioner about whether a face-to-face homebound assessment and home health referral would be appropriate given the stroke and toileting needs.
Ask your provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County.
Gather documents the agency may ask for, such as your POLST, any power-of-attorney or health care directive, and information on any other insurance you have.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 66, live in your own home in Clallam County, need help with toileting following a stroke, and have income and assets in ranges that could fit within Medicaid limits — all factors this program considers. However, whether you're already enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, and whether your overall daily-activity needs reach the level required for ongoing coverage, are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Washington Medicaid program that pays for personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, including help with toilet use and other activities of daily living.
To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living.

Financially, a person generally must qualify for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage) — not the stricter institutional rules — and if already on qualifying Apple Health coverage, only the functional assessment is needed, not a new financial application.

Covered services can include personal care, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, provided in the person's own home or other approved settings.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around) you need help with
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or DSHS whether you already qualify for or are enrolled in community Medicaid, or need to apply.
Discuss with a caseworker whether your overall care needs meet the three-ADL threshold this program looks for.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and it's not yet known whether you've had such a hospital stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Covered services include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in the facility.

There's no charge for days 1–20 of a benefit period; days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026); after day 100, the person pays all costs. This is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has determined daily skilled care is needed as a result
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred recently, ask hospital discharge staff or the doctor whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 66, your age fits this program's 55+ requirement, and your reported income and assets appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person as of 1/1/2026). Whether your functional needs meet the required nursing-facility level of care is not yet known — and importantly, this program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with financial limits set well above regular Medicaid, and it also serves unpaid family caregivers of such individuals.

Eligibility requires nursing-facility level of care under state rules, income at or below $3,976/month, and resources at or below $84,354 for a single applicant (limits effective 1/1/2026); people already on categorically needy or alternative benefit plan Medicaid are not eligible for TSOA.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessment happens only when a person is called from the waitlist — there is no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your care needs meet the nursing-facility level of care standard this program requires
nextSteps: If interested, submit a TSOA application (online at washingtonconnection.org or paper form HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist, since intake is currently waitlist-only.
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a Family Caregiver specialist and about non-Medicaid respite options while waiting.
Ask HCS whether a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment can be scheduled to clarify functional eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You appear to need ongoing care or supervision related to the stroke and toileting needs, which is one factor these respite programs consider. However, it's not yet known whether you have an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) providing regular care, which is central to eligibility for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, awarded based on available funding and priority need rather than guaranteed for everyone who applies.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies, with service levels tailored through a caregiver assessment; amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging.

These programs generally require an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — for Lifespan Respite vouchers specifically, 40+ hours per week of care or supervision — and generally don't stack with other publicly funded respite programs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (family member or friend) providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options in Clallam County.
If there is an unpaid caregiver, ask about a TCARE caregiver assessment to determine what level of respite support might be available.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or would you like help finding out or applying?
Besides help with toileting, do you need help with other daily activities — such as bathing, dressing, eating, moving around, or transferring in and out of bed or chairs?

That combination lines up most clearly with the Medicare home health benefit, and it's worth a look at several Washington Medicaid long-term-care pathways and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) — though TSOA currently has a paused enrollment process. Because age-related disability status, exact income and asset figures, current Medicaid enrollment, caregiver status, and recent hospital history are still unknown, several programs can't be assessed with confidence yet. programs.whyThisMayApply: You reported being covered by Medicare, needing help with mobility and toileting, and living at home rather than in a nursing facility — this combination matches the core requirements for Medicare's home health benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and home health aide help (such as help with walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a doctor or other provider must certify homebound status and a need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment, and order care delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with the person's doctor about arranging a face-to-face assessment for homebound status and a home health order.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned needing help with mobility and toileting, which is the kind of care need this program's assessment looks at, and you're married, which matters because COPES has special asset rules for couples. whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care — such as adult day care, environmental modifications, home-delivered meals, specialized medical equipment, and transportation — for people in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.
It is generally for people age 65 or older, or 18 and older with a qualifying SSI-criteria disability, who are assessed as needing a nursing-facility level of care.

Financial rules generally require income at or below about $2,982/month (2026) or a Medically Needy pathway for higher income, and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple — but spousal protections can let a married applicant's spouse keep a much larger amount, up to about $162,660 in 2026.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 83-year-old with Parkinson's living in a family member's home in Lewis County, needing help with bathing and dressing, covered by Medicare, single, with income between $1,000–$2,999/month and countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington programs look worth a closer look. The Medicare home health benefit and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) line up well with what's known so far, though TSOA currently has a statewide waitlist. The Medicaid-based programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) may also apply, but we need your exact monthly income and Medicaid enrollment status to say more. Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage and respite care programs depend on details we don't have yet — a recent hospital stay, and whether someone is providing unpaid caregiving. None of this is a decision about eligibility; only the administering agency can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live at home rather than in a nursing facility, and you need help with bathing and dressing — all consistent with what this benefit is meant to cover, though a doctor still needs to confirm homebound status and the need for skilled care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy, and home health aide help (like bathing, grooming, and other personal care) when ordered by a doctor and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a health care provider must do a face-to-face assessment and certify both homebound status and the need for skilled care.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about a face-to-face assessment and a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 83 (well above the age-55 threshold), with income and assets that appear to fall under TSOA's higher limits, and needing help with daily activities like bathing and dressing, several of TSOA's criteria appear to align with your situation. Note that this program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers if there is one — with income and resource limits higher than regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person as of 1/1/2026).
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than served right away, and there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or contact your local Area Agency on Aging to be placed on the statewide waitlist, and ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds in the meantime.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 83, with a Parkinson's diagnosis and needing help with bathing and dressing, and with countable assets under $2,000, some of COPES' criteria (age, functional need, and resource limit) line up with what you've shared. Your monthly income falls in a range ($1,000–$2,999) that could be above or below the program's $2,982 income cutoff, so that piece is still unclear.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home and community-based services on top of personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, and nursing services.
It is meant for people age 65 or older (or meeting SSI-related disability criteria) whose care needs, assessed through the CARE tool, meet a nursing-facility level of care.
Financially, it generally requires monthly income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982 as of 1/1/2026) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for help with bathing and dressing suggests a personal-care need that CFC is designed to address, but we don't yet know if you're enrolled in community Medicaid or how many daily activities you need help with overall — both are needed to see if this fits.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for help with personal care tasks like bathing, dressing, and other daily activities, along with supports such as personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training.
It's available to people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and are enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid coverage such as CN or ABP, including SSI-related coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 83 with Parkinson's, needing help with bathing and dressing, and with assets under $2,000, several pieces of this Medicaid long-term care pathway line up. report.overallSummary: This screening looked at eight Washington-state programs based on what you shared: you're 64, married, living in a family member's home in Skagit County, dealing with heart disease, needing help with bathing and dressing, with monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999, countable assets under $2,000, and already covered by both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid). Several programs look worth a closer look, especially the Medicare home health benefit, since your Medicare enrollment and personal-care needs line up with what that program covers. Other programs — including Medicaid COPES, Apple Health long-term care, Community First Choice, TSOA, Medicaid Alternative Care, and respite care options — could be relevant too, but the screening is missing some key details (such as whether you have an SSI-related disability determination, your exact monthly income, how many daily activities you need help with, whether you have an unpaid family caregiver, and the exact category of your Medicaid coverage). Because of these gaps, this report leans heavily on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. Nothing here is a decision about whether you qualify — only the administering agency can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already covered by Apple Health, and Apple Health long-term care is the financial gateway to many of Washington's Medicaid long-term-care programs. Your reported ADL needs and asset level under $2,000 fit parts of this program's rules, but confirming whether you meet the age/disability criteria and income limits is still needed.

whatItCovers.text: This program is the financial pathway for nursing facility care and community long-term-care programs like COPES, requiring a nursing-facility level of care assessment and either SSI-related or MAGI-based financial eligibility.

Income eligibility generally uses the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or a Medically Needy pathway; resource limits are $2,000 for a single person, with special protections for a spouse still living in the community.

Applying involves a financial application (online, paper, phone, or in person) plus a functional (CARE) assessment requested through the regional HCS intake line — for Skagit County, that's Region 2 at 800-780-7094.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're 65+ or under 65 with an SSI-criteria disability determination
Your exact monthly income compared to program limits
nextSteps: Contact HCS Region 2 intake (800-780-7094) to ask about starting a financial application and CARE assessment.
Have income documentation ready to compare against the Special Income Level.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, you live at home (not a nursing facility), and you described needing help with bathing and dressing along with a heart disease diagnosis — all consistent with the basic requirements for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide care (bathing, grooming, and similar help) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Eligibility requires a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care, homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
The process starts with a provider's face-to-face assessment and order, followed by choosing from a list of Medicare-certified agencies that coordinate the care plan with your doctor.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a referral for a face-to-face assessment and home health order makes sense given your heart disease and help needed with bathing and dressing.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Skagit County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You reported needing help with bathing and dressing, assets under $2,000, and Skagit County residency — these line up with parts of COPES' functional and resource rules. However, COPES generally requires being 65 or older, or 18–64 with an SSI-criteria disability, and your income needs to be checked against the program's income limit — both of these are currently unknown for you.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds waiver services on top of Community First Choice personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To qualify, a person generally must be 65+ (or 18–64 with an SSI-criteria disability), meet a nursing-facility level of care assessment, and have income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or qualify through the Medically Needy pathway; countable resources must be at or below $2,000 for a single person.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a request for a CARE assessment through HCS, with ongoing case management through the local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're 65 or older, or under 65 with an SSI-criteria disability determination
Your exact monthly income compared to the $2,982 Special Income Level
nextSteps: Contact your regional HCS intake office (Region 2 covers Skagit County) to ask about a CARE assessment.
Gather documentation of your age/disability status and exact income to discuss with an HCS financial specialist.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid), which matches CFC's core financial requirement. You also described needing help with bathing and dressing. CFC generally requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living to continue services, and it's not yet clear how many ADLs you need help with beyond the two you mentioned.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, in settings like your own home or an adult family home.

Financially, CFC generally requires being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community; people already on qualifying Apple Health coverage typically only need the functional (CARE) assessment, not a new financial application.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (like eating, toileting, transferring, or bathing) you need help with, since continuing CFC generally requires needs in three or more
nextSteps: Ask HCS about scheduling a CARE assessment to document your care needs in detail.
If you're already on Apple Health, mention that you may only need the functional assessment step, not a new financial application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement here, but this benefit is specifically for skilled nursing facility care following a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay. Nothing in your profile indicates a recent hospitalization, so it's unclear whether this currently applies.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Costs include no daily charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100 ($217/day in 2026), and full costs beyond day 100.

nextSteps: If you are hospitalized in the future, ask the hospital discharge team whether the stay would qualify you for SNF coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your age (64), reported ADL needs, and assets under $2,000 fit some of TSOA's basic thresholds. TSOA involves no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure, with 12-month certification periods.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports unpaid family caregivers of people 55+ who are on Apple Health CN/ABP coverage, meet nursing-facility level of care, and choose not to use other Medicaid long-term-care programs, with benefit steps of $250 one-time, up to $500/year, and a later step averaging $928/month (up to $5,568 over six months, current through mid-2026).

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite, adult day services, or other options.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 89 (meeting the 65+ age rule), needs help with bathing, mobility, and transferring (which relates to the nursing-facility level of care assessment), and has monthly income under $1,000 and assets under $2,000 — both within COPES' income and resource limits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's main home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, serving people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living.

It adds services on top of personal care, such as adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.

To qualify functionally, an HCS social worker must find through a CARE assessment that the person needs nursing-facility level of care, based on ADL needs like bathing, transferring, and mobility, or certain nursing or cognitive-supervision needs.

nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 89 years old with help needed for bathing, mobility, and transferring, and income and assets within the program's limits (under $1,000/month income and under $2,000 in assets), your family member's profile lines up with the age, functional, and financial building blocks of this Medicaid long-term care coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it is also the financial gateway to waivers like COPES, PACE, hospice in an institution, and community LTSS programs like CFC.
For the SSI-related pathway, income must generally be at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), and full Apple Health CN medical benefits come along with LTC coverage.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a CARE assessment by an HCS social worker, with eligibility determined within 45 days generally (60 if a disability decision is needed).

nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or an HCS office.
Complete the required financial interview with an HCS financial specialist.
Schedule the in-person CARE assessment through HCS central intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare and needs help with bathing, mobility, and transferring, which suggests a personal or skilled care need — matching the basic pattern for this benefit. Living in a family member's home also fits the 'lives at home' condition.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and durable medical equipment at home, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.
Home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, grooming) is covered only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — it is not covered as stand-alone personal care.
To qualify, a provider must certify homebound status (trouble leaving home without help, or leaving is not recommended) and order the care, delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your family member's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for homebound status and skilled care needs.
Get a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County from the doctor.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 89 (well above the 55+ age requirement), needs help with bathing, mobility, and transferring (relevant to the nursing-facility level of care assessment), and has income and assets below TSOA's financial limits. whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, with income limits up to $3,976/month and resource limits up to $84,354 (single) as of 1/1/2026 — well above regular Medicaid limits.
There is no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs, and eligibility is certified for 12 months at a time.

Program status: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025. New applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced date for the pause to end; people already enrolled or approved before that date keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status and how to be added.

Submit the TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the waitlist by request date, even though processing is currently paused.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related IADL help like meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
Services can be provided in the person's own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, and CFC can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services.

CFC is a Washington Medicaid state plan option (under federal law §1915(k)) administered through Home and Community Services, generally requiring the person to already qualify for community Medicaid coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Washington Apple Health Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).

Request a CARE assessment through HCS central intake to evaluate ADL needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, we don't know whether there has been a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay, which is required before Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay.

The qualifying stay must be at least 3 consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (time in observation status or the ER doesn't count), with admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options).

None of this is a decision about whether you'll get help; only the agency running each program can decide that. A few important pieces of information are still missing (especially how many daily activities you need help with beyond transferring, whether you're enrolled in Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether a family member is providing regular unpaid care), so please treat the notes below as a starting point for conversations with HCS, your Area Agency on Aging, or a benefits counselor rather than a final answer.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You told us you're covered by Medicare and live at home in Whitman County, and you need help with transferring, which is the kind of need this benefit is designed around when it's tied to a skilled care plan ordered by a health care provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and — only while also receiving one of those skilled services — home health aide help with things like bathing, walking, and grooming, plus durable medical equipment for home use, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.
To use this benefit, a provider must do a face-to-face assessment and certify that you're homebound and need skilled care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment for home health certification

You mentioned needing help with transferring, which is one of the daily activities the program's assessment looks at, and being married may matter because the program has special rules protecting a portion of a couple's resources. Whether this fits still depends on a functional assessment and a closer look at your assets against the program's resource rules.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, available in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To be considered, a CARE assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care, and income/resource rules apply — including special protections for a spouse's resources such as a community spouse resource allowance that can go up to $162,660 (as of 1/1/2026).
COPES is administered by DSHS Home and Community Services and works alongside Medicaid Community First Choice; it does not replace Medicare or other insurance benefits.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, with no daily cost for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026) for days 21–100.

This coverage requires a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not counting observation status), admission to a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that might be worth exploring for a 70-year-old in Lewis County, WA, who has dementia, needs help with mobility, lives at home, is covered by Medicare, and has income and assets in the ranges given. None of these results are eligibility decisions — only the Washington Apple Health, DSHS, Medicare, or Area Agency on Aging offices that run these programs can confirm actual eligibility. Several important details are still unknown, including whether your family member is enrolled in community Medicaid, how many daily activities they need help with, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, and the exact monthly income. Filling in those gaps will make this screening much more useful, so the follow-up questions below are a good next step alongside talking to the programs directly.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is already covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and needs help with mobility — these line up with the basic starting conditions for Medicare's home health benefit. A doctor would still need to certify homebound status and order skilled care for this to move forward.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit can help pay for part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language services, medical social services, and home health aide help (only while also receiving skilled nursing or therapy).
It requires a homebound status finding and a face-to-face assessment and order from a health care provider before services begin.
Covered home health services cost $0; Medicare-covered durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about scheduling a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and the need for skilled home care
Ask about a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 70, lives at home in Lewis County, has a dementia diagnosis, and needs help with mobility — all signs that some level of personal care support could be relevant, which is the kind of help CFC provides. However, we don't know whether they're already enrolled in Washington's community Medicaid (CN/ABP) or how many daily activities they need help with, both of which affect whether this program fits.

whatItCovers.text: CFC can help pay for personal care help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, and medication management, plus household tasks like meal prep and shopping when personal care is also needed.

It can also include relief care for caregivers, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and help transitioning from an institution back into the community.
CFC generally requires being eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) and, to continue receiving it, needing help with at least three daily activities based on an assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in (or able to enroll in) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many daily activities (ADLs) they need help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE functional assessment
Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status, or apply if not already enrolled

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this coverage, but we don't have any information about a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days, which is required before Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay. This is included in case such a hospital stay has happened or happens in the future.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility — including room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy — but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Coverage is $0 per day for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance (about $217/day in 2026) for days 21–100, and no Medicare coverage beyond day 100 in a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care is needed afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage before admission to a nursing facility
If there's no recent hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 70 (above TSOA's 55+ age requirement) and has assets that appear to fall under TSOA's resource limit, which are encouraging signs. However, we don't know the exact monthly income relative to the $3,976/month limit, or how many daily activities they need help with, both of which matter for this program. It's also worth noting that TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, with new applicants going onto a waitlist rather than receiving immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is meant for people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular (CN/ABP) Medicaid, with income and resource limits higher than standard Medicaid limits.
As of the most recent update, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than getting services right away.
For those enrolled, there's no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure like some other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the TSOA waitlist
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has dementia and needs help with mobility, which suggests an ongoing need for care or supervision — the kind of situation respite programs are designed to support caregivers through. whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for respite care, though funding is limited and awards depend on availability and priority need.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility — plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling; amounts vary by county Area Agency on Aging.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 79-year-old San Juan County resident living at home, needing help with eating, toileting, and dressing after a stroke, with countable assets under $2,000 and current Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage — several Washington programs look worth a closer look with the appropriate agency. A few important pieces of information are still missing (especially monthly income, whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, and details of any recent hospital stay), so this screening is a starting point, not a determination of eligibility. Only the administering agency (DSHS/HCS, HCA, Medicare, or the local Area Agency on Aging) can confirm what actually applies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid) and needing help with three ADLs — eating, toileting, and dressing — matches the substantial ADL need and community Medicaid enrollment criteria this program is built around.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (including eating, dressing, toileting, transfer, and medication management) and related IADL support, plus relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

CFC is Washington's main Medicaid state-plan personal care benefit for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, and it can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services.

nextSteps: Request a CARE assessment through the regional HCS central intake line to establish the functional (ADL) level of need.
If already active on Apple Health CN/ABP, only the assessment paperwork (care needs, diagnoses, medications, physician information) should be needed — confirm this with HCS.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Current Medicare enrollment plus stroke-related needs for help with eating, toileting, and dressing while living at home suggest this benefit may be worth discussing with a doctor, since it covers part-time skilled and aide care at home.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, feeding) when paired with skilled care — at no cost for covered services.

To qualify, a provider must certify a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care and confirm homebound status through a face-to-face assessment, with care delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

COPES is operated by DSHS Home and Community Services and serves people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living.

informationStillNeeded: Monthly income bracket, to check it against the Special Income Level used for this program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 79, needing help with eating, toileting, and dressing, and having countable assets under $2,000 fits the age, functional, and resource pathways for this program. The income piece needed to confirm the financial pathway is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
Eligibility requires homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, a physician's order, and care delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

Eligibility requires a CARE assessment finding nursing-facility level of care — which can include needing help with several daily activities like toileting, dressing, and mobility, or having a cognitive impairment such as memory loss combined with an unmet daily-living need — plus income at or below $2,982/month and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person (figures as of 1/1/2026).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 67-year-old widow in Whitman County living in a family member's home, needing help with bathing, toileting, and transferring, diagnosed with Parkinson's, covered by Medicare, with monthly income in the $3,000–$3,999 range and countable assets of $85,000–$160,000 — four programs came up as worth a closer look. Some key details are still missing (whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether there's been a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly helping you), so a few of these entries lean more on questions than firm next steps. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency for each program can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already covered by Medicare, and you've described needing help with bathing, toileting, and transferring along with a Parkinson's diagnosis — needs that often come with a skilled care component (like therapy or nursing) that this benefit is built around. You're also described as living in a family member's home rather than a facility, which fits the 'lives at home' condition.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time skilled care at home — including skilled nursing, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, and medical social services — delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency at no cost for the covered services.
A home health aide (help with walking, bathing, grooming, feeding) is covered, but only while you are also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — not for personal care alone.
Eligibility requires homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and a plan of care ordered by a provider and delivered through a certified agency.
Costs for covered home health services are $0; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether your care needs (including any therapy or skilled nursing related to Parkinson's) and homebound status would support a home health referral.
Ask your doctor for a face-to-face assessment and to identify Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Whitman County.
If home health starts, ask the agency to explain in writing what will and won't be covered before services begin.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with bathing, toileting, and transferring — three ADLs, which matches the level of need CFC looks for when continuing services at reassessment (chunk wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility). CFC is available statewide through Home and Community Services, which would include Whitman County. However, CFC also requires eligibility for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), and it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in or eligible for that coverage.

whatItCovers.text: CFC requires a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and continuing CFC services requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Financially, CFC requires eligibility for Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) community Medicaid — not the stricter institutional financial rules.

CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, toileting, and transferring, along with IADL help, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings including a family member's own home.

Applying involves the same LTSS pathway as other HCS programs: a financial application (unless already on qualifying Apple Health) plus a CARE assessment requested through regional HCS central intake.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or would be eligible for, Washington Apple Health (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) community Medicaid.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional central intake to ask about a CARE assessment.
Ask HCS or DSHS whether your income and assets would put you in range for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), since this is separate from Medicare.
If not yet on Apple Health, consider starting a Medicaid application to see how CFC eligibility would be assessed.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a starting requirement for this benefit, but nothing in your profile mentions a recent hospital stay, and this benefit specifically requires a qualifying 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay before it applies. Without that piece, it's unclear whether this program is currently relevant to your situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not counting observation status) and admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, medications and supplies used at the facility, and some transportation and dietary counseling.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has determined a need for daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
If there has been a recent hospitalization not yet mentioned, share those dates and details with your care team to check this program's relevance.
If no qualifying hospital stay applies, ask about alternatives like Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're living in a family member's home and need ongoing help with bathing, toileting, and transferring, which suggests there may be a family caregiver providing regular support — a key condition for respite programs. It isn't yet confirmed whether that caregiver is unpaid and how many hours of care they provide, which affects which respite option (if any) could apply.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options).

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers free or low-cost planned short-term relief care for unpaid caregivers of adults 18+, with service levels tailored through a caregiver specialist's assessment; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.
Applying for FCSP happens through the local Area Agency on Aging, reachable via Community Living Connections; Lifespan Respite vouchers are applied for online, by phone, or by email.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as the family member whose home you live in) is providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program available through the local Area Agency on Aging serving Whitman County.
If a family member is providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, consider looking into a Lifespan Respite Washington voucher application.
Ask about a TCARE caregiver assessment to see what level of respite support might be available locally.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it recently?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days in the recent past (not counting time under 'observation' status)?

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared, this 90-year-old Jefferson County resident who needs help with dressing, mobility, and toileting after a stroke may want to look into a few Washington and Medicare programs that help pay for personal care, short-term skilled care, or caregiver relief. Because some key facts — like whether this person is enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), whether there was a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved — are not yet known, this screening is only a starting point. programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with dressing, mobility, and toileting following a stroke) that suggest a possible need for skilled or personal care support — all factors this program's screening looks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.
Covered home health services generally cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible ($283/year in 2026).

To qualify, a person must need part-time skilled care, be certified as homebound by a provider, have a face-to-face assessment, and have care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, homemaker services unrelated to the care plan, or personal/custodial care when that is the only kind of care needed — a skilled care need (such as nursing or therapy) generally has to be part of the picture.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or health care provider for a face-to-face assessment to determine homebound status and the need for skilled care (such as therapy related to the stroke).
If a skilled need is identified, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person needs help with dressing, mobility, and toileting — three ADLs — which lines up with CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue receiving services. However, it is not yet known whether this person is enrolled in or could qualify for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), which is a required piece of CFC eligibility, so this cannot be confirmed from the information given.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with daily activities like dressing, toileting, mobility/transfers, bathing, and eating, plus related help with things like meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also includes relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and training on how to manage personal care providers.
To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, which matches the dressing, mobility, and toileting needs described here.
Financial eligibility for CFC requires qualifying for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or an Apple Health specialist whether income and assets in this range could qualify for community Medicaid coverage, since eligibility can only be confirmed by the agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, it is not known whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, which is required for this coverage, so it's unclear whether this program is currently relevant.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and medical supplies during a covered skilled nursing facility (SNF) stay.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, admission to a Medicare-certified SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

In 2026, costs are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and the full cost after day 100 within a benefit period; coverage is capped at 100 days per benefit period and is not meant for long-term custodial care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has said daily skilled nursing or therapy care is needed afterward.

If there has been no recent hospital stay, ask a doctor about other options such as the Medicare home health benefit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person's stroke-related needs with dressing, mobility, and toileting suggest an ongoing care or supervision need, which is one factor these programs look at. It is not known whether an unpaid caregiver (family member, friend, or other unpaid helper) is regularly providing care, which is central to whether respite programs would apply, so this can't be confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40 or more hours per week of care, used through contracted respite providers statewide (subject to funding availability).

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care setting — plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies; amounts and rules vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are set through a caregiver assessment.
These respite programs are for unpaid caregivers, not for the care receiver directly, and generally don't stack with other publicly funded respite unless waitlisted.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver is providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week that care is provided.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program for Jefferson County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a nursing facility, and has a care need (help with transferring after a stroke) that could point to a need for skilled or personal care — all factors this program's screening rules look for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language therapy delivered at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy — it does not cover ongoing custodial or personal care alone.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and bed mobility, plus related support such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, delivered in a person's own home or other community settings.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared, we found four programs in Washington that may be worth a closer look for a 70-year-old living in Garfield County who has Medicare, long-term care insurance, a stroke diagnosis, and help needs with bathing, toileting, and mobility. Two programs (Medicare's home health benefit and skilled nursing facility coverage) are tied to Medicare, which you already have, so they may be more directly relevant. Two others (Medicaid Community First Choice and Washington's respite care programs) depend on information we don't have yet — such as whether there is Medicaid enrollment, a recent hospital stay, or an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care. None of this is a decision about eligibility; only the agency that runs each program can determine that. Please treat this as a starting point for conversations with those agencies, not a final answer.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has Medicare coverage and has help needs tied to a stroke, including mobility and bathing — the kinds of needs Medicare's home health benefit is built around, and this person lives at home rather than in a facility, which fits the setting for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language services delivered at home, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.
Home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and walking can also be covered, but only while skilled nursing or therapy services are also being provided.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor whether a face-to-face homebound assessment and home health order could be arranged.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has Medicare coverage, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care is currently unknown — and that is a required trigger for this coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, including skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, meals, and medical supplies, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

This coverage requires a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor determined daily skilled nursing or therapy care was needed afterward.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, lives at home in Snohomish County (not a nursing facility), and has care needs involving mobility, eating, and bathing that suggest a skilled or personal care need — all factors that line up with this benefit's basic criteria.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment for home use, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, speech-language pathology, or occupational therapy.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 79 (meeting PACE's age-55-or-older baseline), lives in Snohomish County — one of the four Washington counties with PACE coverage — and has needs with mobility, eating, and bathing that suggest a level of care PACE is designed to serve for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates and covers all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team, including home care, personal care, primary and specialty medical care, therapy, prescription drugs, transportation, and more.

Snohomish County has PACE coverage across 21 ZIP codes, but coverage within the county is not complete, so a specific address needs to be checked against the coverage list or with the organization's intake line.

Eligibility requires the state to certify a nursing-facility level of care, living in a PACE service area, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help; financial eligibility is separately determined by HCS.
With Medicaid there is no monthly premium; with Medicare only (no Medicaid), a monthly premium applies for the long-term-care portion plus a Part D drug premium, quoted at intake.

nextSteps: Contact a Washington PACE organization serving Snohomish County — Providence PACE ElderPlace, (206) 320-5325 — to confirm the specific ZIP code is in the service area.
Ask HCS about the required nursing-facility-level-of-care functional assessment and financial eligibility determination.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs help with mobility, eating, and bathing — three ADLs — which matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue services. whatItCovers.text: CFC provides personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related help with meals, housework, and shopping when ADL help is also needed.
It also can include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for managing personal care providers.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, the person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility requires being eligible for community (not institutional) Medicaid — Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage.
Services can be provided in the person's own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, among other settings.

Ask HCS how income and asset levels compare to community Medicaid limits, since eligibility can only be confirmed by the agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, which is a base requirement for this benefit. However, it's unknown whether there has been a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay with a doctor's order for daily skilled care afterward, and this benefit does not cover ongoing custodial help with ADLs like the mobility, eating, and bathing needs described.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status does not count) and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Costs in 2026 include no daily charge for days 1–20, then $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, with the person paying all costs after day 100.
This is a short-term skilled care benefit, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has ordered daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
If there is no qualifying hospital stay, ask about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or PACE for ongoing needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's ongoing needs with mobility, eating, and bathing suggest a need for regular care or supervision, which is a starting point for respite programs. However, it's unknown whether an unpaid family caregiver is currently providing that regular care, which several respite pathways require.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite, adult day services, or other options.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies, with amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging; in Snohomish County this has ranged roughly $1,000–$4,362 per year on a sliding fee scale, based on unpublished, manually gathered information that should be confirmed with the local AAA.
These respite programs generally require an unpaid caregiver (someone not paid to provide the care) and generally don't stack with other publicly funded respite the household is already receiving.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is a family member or friend providing regular, unpaid care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org to connect with the local Area Agency on Aging about FCSP.
If applicable, look into Lifespan Respite vouchers at wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has a financial application ever been submitted for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan)?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay (not observation status) of 3 or more consecutive days recently, followed by a doctor's order for daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is there a family member or friend providing regular, unpaid care or supervision, and if so, how many hours per week?
Has a doctor certified the person as 'homebound' under Medicare's definition (needs help or has trouble leaving home)?
What is the specific home ZIP code, to confirm it falls inside a PACE organization's service area within Snohomish County?
