report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look for this 90-year-old Snohomish County resident who lives at home, has Parkinson's disease, needs help with bathing and toileting, is married, is covered by Medicare, and has countable assets under $2,000. A few key pieces of information are still missing — most importantly the exact monthly income (you gave a range of $1,000–$2,999, which straddles some program thresholds), whether Medicaid coverage already exists, the full count of daily activities needing help, whether an unpaid caregiver provides regular care, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay. Filling in these gaps will make it much easier to know which programs are worth pursuing first. Nothing here is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency can decide that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is confirmed, the person lives at home rather than in a facility, and the described needs (help with bathing and toileting, plus a Parkinson's diagnosis) suggest there could be a skilled or personal care need worth discussing with a doctor.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide help (when paired with skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy) for people who are homebound and need a doctor-ordered care plan.
Covered home health services cost $0, though there is normally a cost share for durable medical equipment.

To start, a health care provider must do a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with the primary doctor about whether a face-to-face homebound and skilled-care assessment could be arranged.
Ask the doctor for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Snohomish County.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 90 and needing help with bathing and toileting are consistent with needing nursing-facility level of care, and Snohomish County is one of the four counties with PACE coverage — though whether the specific ZIP code is served still needs to be checked.

whatItCovers.text: PACE combines Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, coordinated by a single care team, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

Washington's PACE coverage area includes 21 ZIP codes in Snohomish County, with Providence PACE operating an Everett South center; whether a specific ZIP is covered still needs to be checked against the DSHS coverage list or by calling the intake line.

informationStillNeeded: The specific home ZIP code, to confirm it falls within a PACE service area.
Financial eligibility details (this would be assessed by HCS financial workers).
nextSteps: Call Providence PACE intake at (206) 320-5325 to check ZIP code coverage in Snohomish County.
Ask about the state's nursing-facility level of care certification process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 90, needing help with daily activities, and being married align with parts of this program's focus on older adults and their caregivers who don't rely on regular Medicaid. However, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, so new applicants currently go on a waitlist rather than starting services right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility level care but are not on regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above standard Medicaid limits, and no cost-sharing or estate recovery of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.
As of December 1, 2025, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced date for the waitlist to reopen.

nextSteps: Submit a TSOA application through washingtonconnection.org to establish a place on the statewide waitlist, even though services won't start immediately.
Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is for people 65+ who need nursing-facility level care and have countable resources at or below $2,000 — both of which match what you've shared (age 90, resources under $2,000, and needing help with bathing and toileting). However, whether the monthly income (given only as a $1,000–$2,999 range) falls within the program's income rules hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that adds services on top of personal care for people assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing support.

To be considered, a person generally must be 65 or older (or younger with a qualifying disability), be assessed by a CARE assessment as needing nursing-facility level of care, and have countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.
Financial eligibility depends on whether available monthly income is at or below the Special Income Level of $2,982 (as of January 2026), with an alternate path for people whose income is higher.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the $2,982 Special Income Level.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office.
Call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Gather documentation on income and any asset transfers or gifts made in the last 5 years.

programs.whyThisMayApply: CFC helps pay for help with daily activities like bathing and toileting, which matches the needs described. However, it's not yet known whether the household is enrolled in a qualifying community Medicaid program, or whether help is needed with three or more daily activities (the profile mentions two so far — bathing and toileting).

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for help with activities of daily living such as bathing, toileting, dressing, and mobility, plus related services like personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training, for people who meet nursing-facility level of care.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and must be eligible for a qualifying community Medicaid program.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the household is currently enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
The total number of daily activities (ADLs) needing hands-on help.
nextSteps: Confirm Medicaid enrollment status or apply if not already enrolled.
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS to document ADL needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the Medicaid pathway that opens the door to other long-term-care programs like COPES and PACE. Age 90, resources under $2,000, and the described care needs align with parts of this program's requirements, but the exact monthly income hasn't been confirmed against the income limits.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it serves as the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and nursing facility care.
Income eligibility generally depends on staying at or below the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of January 2026), with a Medically Needy pathway available for people above that amount.
Resource limits for this pathway are $2,000 for a single person, with special protections for a spouse still living in the community.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the Special Income Level.
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online, by mail, or in person at an HCS office.
Call the Region 2 HCS intake line (covering Snohomish County) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Prepare documentation on income, resources, and any recent asset transfers.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is confirmed, but this benefit only applies after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, and it's not known whether such a stay has occurred recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a short-term stay in a skilled nursing facility — including nursing care, therapy, and related services — but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and when daily skilled care is needed.
Coverage is limited to up to 100 days per benefit period, with no charge for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance from day 21 through day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
Ask the doctor whether skilled nursing facility care might be appropriate given the Parkinson's diagnosis.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being married suggests a spouse could be providing informal care, but it isn't yet known whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which is central to these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Programs like Lifespan Respite vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offer short breaks for unpaid family caregivers, including in-home respite, adult day services, support groups, counseling, and caregiver training.

In Snohomish County, FCSP respite funding through the county and Homage Senior Services has ranged roughly $1,000–$4,362 per year on a sliding fee scale, though this is unpublished local information that should be confirmed directly with the Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) provides regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options in Snohomish County.
If a spouse or family member is providing regular care, ask about a TCARE caregiver screening to see what level of respite support might be available.

followUpQuestions: What is the exact monthly income for the household (rather than just the $1,000–$2,999 range)?
Is this person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, or has an application been submitted?
Besides bathing and toileting, is help needed with any other daily activities (eating, dressing, transferring, mobility, medication management)?
Does the spouse or another family member provide regular unpaid caregiving, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more days in the recent past?
What is the specific home ZIP code in Snohomish County, to check PACE service-area coverage?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — you're 90, live in a family member's home in Grant County, WA, have Medicare and long-term care insurance, are managing heart disease, and need help with transferring and dressing — a few programs look worth a closer look. However, several details that matter for accurate screening (like whether you're on Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, your full list of daily-activity needs, any recent hospital stays, and whether someone is providing unpaid caregiving) are still unknown. Because of that, this screening should be treated as a starting point for conversation rather than a final answer, and the follow-up questions below matter as much as the program list itself. No agency here can confirm eligibility — only the program administrators can do that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, you have care needs (help with transferring and dressing), and you live in a home setting rather than a nursing facility — all of which line up with what this benefit is designed for. This program is Medicare-based, so it doesn't depend on income or assets the way Medicaid programs do.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help with bathing, grooming, and walking) — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.
Covered services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To use this benefit, a health care provider must certify homebound status and a need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment, and a Medicare-certified home health agency must deliver the ordered care.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or nurse practitioner about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grant County from your provider

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've mentioned needing help with transferring and dressing, which are activities of daily living that this program is built around, and you live in Washington. However, whether you're enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, and whether your total ADL needs reach the three-or-more threshold this program looks for at reassessment, are both unknown from what you've shared so far.

whatItCovers.text: This program can cover personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, and to continue receiving services at reassessment, the person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living.
Financially, this program requires eligibility for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) — not the higher income/asset limits used for institutional Medicaid.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or could enroll in, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many total activities of daily living you need help with (this program generally looks for three or more for ongoing services)
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about a CARE assessment
Ask HCS or a benefits counselor whether your income and assets could fit community Medicaid rules, given your reported income and asset levels
Gather details on all daily activities you need help with, not just transferring and dressing

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is required for this benefit, but whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor's order for daily skilled care — the key trigger for this coverage — is not yet known from your profile.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and equipment used in the facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation time doesn't count), admission to a skilled nursing facility generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, with a doctor recommending ongoing daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage before choosing a facility
Keep records of any hospital admissions, including whether you were listed as 'inpatient' rather than 'observation'

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have ongoing care needs (help with transferring and dressing) and live in a family member's home, which suggests there may be a caregiver involved, but it's not yet confirmed whether that caregiver is unpaid and how many hours of care they provide — both of which matter for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only if the care receiver has a developmental disability that began before age 18).
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for contracted in-home respite, adult day services, or similar options, though funding is limited and not guaranteed.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are not capped at a statewide published level.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing unpaid regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide it
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to be connected with the local Area Agency on Aging for Grant County
Ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers by phone (253-442-3214) or through wapave.org
If applicable, ask whether a TCARE caregiver assessment through FCSP might apply

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or would you be open to applying for it?
Besides transferring and dressing, do you need regular help with other daily activities such as bathing, eating, toileting, or moving around the home?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more days in recent weeks or months, with a doctor recommending ongoing skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving for you, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a doctor indicated you are 'homebound' — meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended because of your health?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — you're 67, live in your own home in Kittitas County, need help with dressing, eating, and toileting, have heart disease, are a Vietnam-era veteran, are covered by Medicare, and have countable assets of $160,000 or more — several programs look worth a closer look, and one (Medicare's home health benefit) lines up especially well with what you've described. Others, like VA Aid & Attendance, VA Housebound, Washington's Community First Choice Medicaid program, Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit, and respite care programs, may also be worth investigating, but key pieces of information are still missing — things like your exact net worth, whether you're enrolled in Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether a family member provides unpaid caregiving. None of the programs below have been determined to apply to you; only the agency that runs each program can make that call. Answering the follow-up questions would sharpen this screening.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, live at home in Kittitas County, and need help with dressing, eating, and toileting — needs that point toward the kind of skilled or personal care need this Medicare benefit is built to address.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech-language therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with walking, bathing, and grooming, when also receiving skilled care.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a person must need part-time skilled care, be certified as homebound by a provider, and have care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation from a health care provider through a face-to-face assessment that you meet Medicare's homebound definition and need part-time skilled care.
nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health certification and request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67 and living in your own home in Kittitas County, and you need help with dressing, eating, and toileting — three ADLs, which is the kind of substantial need this program is designed around. Whether it applies further depends on your Medicaid enrollment status, which wasn't part of your profile.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with daily activities such as dressing, eating, toileting, bathing, and medication management, plus related support like meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.

It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, an assistive technology benefit, skills training, community transition services, and training on managing personal care providers.

To qualify, a person generally needs to be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage) and have a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care need, including an unmet need in at least three ADLs at reassessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or could enroll in, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN or ABP coverage).
nextSteps: Contact Washington Home and Community Services (HCS) regional intake to ask about applying for Apple Health CN/ABP coverage and to request a CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran with a discharge that was not dishonorable, and with needs for help with dressing, eating, and toileting, you meet several of the basic building blocks this benefit looks at. Whether the net worth limit is met is unclear, since your reported asset bracket ($160,000 or more) doesn't pin down where you fall relative to the VA's limit.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's VA pension that the household can use for care costs at its own discretion.
As of December 2025, the maximum annual amount for a veteran with no dependents receiving Aid & Attendance is $29,093 per year, compared with $17,441 without it.

Qualifying for the underlying pension depends on net worth staying within a limit set by Congress — $163,699 effective December 2025 — along with income limits, discharge status, and wartime service requirements.

informationStillNeeded: Your more exact countable net worth (assets and certain income), so it can be compared to the VA's $163,699 pension limit.
nextSteps: Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to review your net worth and income against current pension limits and to discuss filing VA Form 21P-527EZ.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're a Vietnam-era veteran with a discharge that was not dishonorable, so the wartime-service and discharge pieces of this benefit line up. However, it's unknown whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability, and this benefit cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — only one applies.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
As of December 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving the Housebound benefit could receive up to $21,313 per year, compared with $17,441 without it.
It cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one benefit or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability.
Your more exact countable net worth, to compare against the VA's $163,699 pension limit.
nextSteps: Discuss housebound status with your doctor, and talk with a Veterans Service Officer about whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance would be the better benefit to pursue, since only one can be received.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which covers the basic insurance requirement for this benefit, but there's no information yet about a recent 3-day-or-longer hospital inpatient stay, which is a required trigger for this coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and physical, occupational, and speech therapy.
For 2026, days 1–20 cost $0 per day, days 21–100 cost $217 per day in coinsurance, and days 101 and beyond are the patient's full responsibility.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor determined you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward.
nextSteps: If you have an upcoming or recent hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay that could lead to Medicare-covered skilled nursing facility care.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported care needs — help with dressing, eating, and toileting — suggest an ongoing need for care or supervision, which is one basis for respite programs. It's not yet known whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing this regular support.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers providing regular care to an adult.

Lifespan vouchers generally require the caregiver to provide 40 or more hours per week of unpaid care, that both caregiver and care receiver live in Washington, and that they aren't already receiving respite through another publicly funded program.
FCSP services are tailored through a caregiver specialist's assessment and are free or low cost, with details varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week they provide.
nextSteps: If a family member or friend provides regular unpaid care, contact PAVE at 253-442-3214 about Lifespan Respite vouchers, or your local Area Agency on Aging at 1-855-567-0252 about the Family Caregiver Support Program.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or would you be open to applying for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage?
What is your more exact countable net worth (savings, investments, and other assets), so it can be compared to the VA's $163,699 pension limit?
Do you consider yourself substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability, or are you generally able to get out and about?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and if so, did a doctor recommend ongoing daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Does a family member, friend, or other unpaid caregiver regularly provide you with care or supervision, and if so, about how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at four programs based on what you shared: your relative is 78, lives in Skagit County in a family member's home, has dementia, needs help with dressing, transferring, and mobility, is covered by Medicare, and has income and assets on the higher end. Two programs (Medicare home health and Medicaid Community First Choice) look worth a closer look because they focus on personal care and daily living support. Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage is less likely to be a fit right now because it depends on a recent hospital stay, which we don't have information about. Respite care programs could help whoever is providing day-to-day care, but we don't yet know if there's an unpaid caregiver in the picture. None of this means anyone is approved or denied — only the actual agency running each program can make that determination. A few missing pieces of information (noted below) would help sharpen this picture, so please treat this as a starting point for conversations with the agencies, not a final answer.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is enrolled in Medicare and needs help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, which fits within the kind of in-home skilled and aide support this benefit is designed to cover, and they live at home with a family member rather than in a nursing facility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency.

Home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding can also be covered, but only while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided — it isn't available as personal care alone.
Covered home health services generally cost $0 out of pocket; durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To qualify, a doctor or nurse practitioner must certify a homebound status — meaning leaving home is difficult without help or is not recommended — along with a face-to-face assessment and a written care plan.

nextSteps: Talk with your relative's doctor about scheduling the required face-to-face assessment and discussing whether their condition and mobility difficulties would support a homebound-status certification.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Skagit County.
Have the care plan (CMS-485) and any supplemental coverage documents, like Medigap, ready when the agency reaches out.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is 78, lives in Skagit County, has a dementia diagnosis, and needs help with dressing, transferring, and mobility — this matches the kind of substantial, ongoing help with daily activities this program is built around. However, this program requires being enrolled in (or eligible for) regular community Medicaid coverage, and we don't yet know that status for your relative, so this remains an open question rather than a settled fact.

whatItCovers.text: This program can help pay for personal care support with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, and mobility, plus related help with things like meal prep, housework, and medication management when ADL help is also needed.
It also can include relief (respite) care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for family members managing caregivers.
Services can be provided in a person's own home, an adult family home, or assisted living settings, and can be combined with other waiver services (CFC+COPES) for additional support.

To continue receiving this kind of care over time, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, which lines up with the mobility, transferring, and dressing needs described.

Eligibility also requires meeting the financial rules for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), which is separate from — and generally stricter than — simply having Medicare.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your relative is enrolled in, or would need to apply for, Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through the regional intake line to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or a Washington Apple Health caseworker whether your relative's income and assets fit within the community Medicaid rules, since program financial rules can differ from Medicare eligibility.
Gather care needs, diagnoses, medication list, and physician information in case an assessment is scheduled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but this program is specifically for short-term rehabilitation after a recent hospital stay of at least 3 days, and we don't have information indicating a recent hospitalization — so it's unclear whether this situation currently applies.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying hospital stay.
It is not designed for ongoing custodial or long-term care needs like daily dressing or mobility help outside of a facility rehab stay.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient responsibility beyond day 100 in a benefit period.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative lives in a family member's home and needs help with dressing, transferring, and mobility due to dementia, which suggests ongoing care or supervision is likely being provided by someone — but we don't yet know if there's a specific unpaid family caregiver and how many hours of care they provide, which several respite programs require information about.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies including in-home respite and adult day services, though funding is limited and not guaranteed.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), run through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers short-term planned care from a few hours to a few days, plus caregiver training, support groups, counseling, and supplies, with details and amounts varying by county.
These programs generally serve unpaid caregivers providing regular care to someone in Washington, and typically don't stack with each other or with other publicly funded respite.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week they provide it.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org to find the local Area Agency on Aging serving Skagit County and ask about FCSP options.
If a caregiver is providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, consider applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher online at wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or by calling 253-442-3214.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 87, live in Klickitat County in a family member's home, need help with transferring and dressing after a stroke, are married, have Medicare, and have moderate income and assets — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look with an official agency. Programs tied to Medicaid long-term care (Apple Health LTC, COPES, Community First Choice) and Medicare home health look like the strongest starting points, but final answers depend on details we don't have yet, like your exact income, assets, current Medicaid status, and whether you've had a recent hospital stay. Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) may also fit but currently has a waitlist. Respite programs may be relevant if a family member is providing unpaid care. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency (HCS, HCA, or Medicare) can determine that after a full application and assessment. Please treat this as a starting map, not a verdict.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare and, after a stroke, need help with transferring and dressing while living at home with family — needs that Medicare's home health benefit is designed to help with when a doctor certifies part-time skilled care and homebound status.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only alongside skilled care), plus durable medical equipment, generally at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a health care provider must certify homebound status and the need for part-time or intermittent skilled services after a face-to-face assessment, and care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor for a face-to-face assessment and a referral for home health services.
Contact a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Klickitat County to discuss your needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 87 and covered by Medicare, live in Klickitat County, and need help with transferring and dressing following a stroke — the kind of daily-activity need COPES is meant to support for people who might otherwise need nursing-facility care. You're also married, which changes how Medicaid counts income and resources for a program like this.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver that adds extra home and community-based services on top of Community First Choice personal care, for people assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care but who live at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.
Covered services can include adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, specialized medical equipment and supplies, and transportation, among others.
To move forward, a CARE assessment would need to find that you need nursing-facility level of care, which looks at things like help needed with transferring, dressing, and other daily activities.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income, to compare against Medicaid's special income limit
Your countable assets, to compare against Medicaid's resource limit
Confirmation of your marital status for Medicaid purposes
Whether you are currently enrolled in Medicaid
nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit the HCS financial application (HCA 18-005) so a worker can review how your income and assets are counted.
Ask an HCS or Area Agency on Aging representative how spousal income and resource rules would apply given your marriage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 87, with a stroke and help needed with transferring and dressing, your daily care needs may overlap with what Community First Choice covers. Your Medicare coverage doesn't rule out Medicaid personal care, but we don't yet know if you're enrolled in community Medicaid or exactly how many daily activities you need help with.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice (CFC) is a Washington Medicaid state plan benefit for personal care for people who meet an institutional (nursing-facility) level of care but live in the community.

It covers help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, bed mobility, and toileting, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid
The number of daily living activities you need regular help with, since needing help with three or more may matter for this program
nextSteps: Ask HCS or an Apple Health representative to check your Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE assessment so your daily-activity needs are documented.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health LTC is the financial gateway to programs like COPES, and your age (87), stroke-related care needs (help with transferring and dressing), and Medicare coverage line up with the kind of situation this program is built for. Whether you qualify financially depends on income and asset details not yet confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care, and it's the financial gateway to HCS waivers such as COPES.
If income is above the $2,982/month special income level (2026), a person may still qualify through the Medically Needy pathway if allowed deductions bring net income down to $994/month or below.

Applying involves a financial application plus an in-person CARE functional assessment, with financial and functional eligibility determined together; standard processing is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income, to compare against Medicaid's special income limit
Your countable assets, to compare against Medicaid's resource limit
Confirmation of your marital status for Medicaid purposes
Whether you are currently enrolled in Medicaid
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit the paper HCA 18-005 form.
Call your regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Ask about spousal impoverishment protections given your marriage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. Whether it applies depends on whether you've had a recent hospital inpatient stay of three or more nights followed by a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy — information we don't yet have.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and dietary counseling in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.

This coverage requires a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more nights recently, and whether a doctor has said you need daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If you have had a recent hospital stay, ask the hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 87, married, with countable assets between $85,000 and $160,000 — within TSOA's resource limits for a married applicant — and needing help with transferring and dressing, your situation has some features TSOA is designed around. However, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, and we don't yet know if your monthly income is at or below the program's limit.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic (categorically needy/alternative benefit plan) Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using resource limits set well above regular Medicaid amounts.

As of the program status noted here, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving immediate services, though people already enrolled keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your monthly income is at or below TSOA's $3,976/month limit
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/your local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about joining the TSOA waitlist.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need help with transferring and dressing after a stroke — a situation that often involves an unpaid family caregiver, which is exactly who respite programs are designed to support. whatItCovers.text: Washington offers respite through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for care receivers with a developmental disability originating before age 18); Lifespan vouchers require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care.
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP respite amounts vary by county Area Agency on Aging with no published statewide cap.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program in Klickitat County.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, several programs look worth a closer look, but a few key facts are still missing that would sharpen this screening. Your Vietnam-era veteran status, need for help with bathing and eating, Medicare coverage, and reported net worth line up well with VA pension-based benefits, and your Medicare enrollment plus care needs suggest Medicare's home health benefit is worth asking about. Other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, VA Housebound, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and Washington respite programs) may also apply, but the screening couldn't fully assess them because information about your Medicaid status, total number of ADLs needing help, any recent hospital stay, whether someone provides you unpaid caregiving, and your home-confinement status wasn't provided. None of this means you do or don't qualify for anything — only the administering agency (VA, Medicaid/HCS, Medicare, or the local Area Agency on Aging) can make that determination. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are a Vietnam-era veteran with a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, you need another person's help with daily activities (bathing and eating), and your reported net worth falls within the VA's pension limit — all factors this program's screening looks at. This suggests it's worth investigating further with the VA directly.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.
The wartime service window includes the Vietnam era (8/5/1964–5/7/1975, earlier for in-country service), which matches your reported service era.

The payment amount is based on the difference between countable income and a Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR); for a veteran with no dependents, the MAPR with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year, effective December 1, 2025. The money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.

nextSteps: Gather VA-related documents: Social Security number, VA file number, service history, income and asset details, and unreimbursed medical/care expenses.
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680.
Contact a VA regional office or accredited veterans service officer to start VA Form 21P-527EZ.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, you have care needs (help with bathing and eating), and you live at home rather than in a nursing facility — the three factors this screening checks for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, feeding) — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT is also being provided.

To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended) and need part-time/intermittent skilled care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or another health care provider for a face-to-face assessment to determine homebound status and order home health services.
Ask your provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grant County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing help with bathing and eating, which are ADLs that Community First Choice is built around, and you live in Washington (Grant County) where Home and Community Services administers this program. However, the screening doesn't yet know whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, or how many total ADLs you need help with — both are required pieces for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, eating, dressing, toileting, mobility, etc.) and related IADLs, plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

To qualify, a person generally must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage) and, at reassessment, have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
CFC services are meant to supplement, not replace, Medicare, other insurance, or informal support — relevant since you're covered by Medicare and long-term care insurance.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or able to enroll in) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid
How many activities of daily living you need help with in total
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake line to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or DSHS about your current Medicaid enrollment status or how to apply.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime service era, discharge status, and net worth appear to align with the underlying VA pension requirements shared with Aid & Attendance. However, the screening doesn't yet know whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability, which is the specific clinical test for this particular benefit. Note that a person can receive either Housebound or Aid & Attendance, but not both.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
For a veteran with no dependents, the MAPR with Housebound status is $21,313/year, effective December 1, 2025 — lower than the Aid & Attendance MAPR.
Housebound and Aid & Attendance cannot both be received at the same time.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with the VA or a veterans service officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits your situation, since only one can be received.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, the screening doesn't know whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — a required trigger for this coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and only for up to 100 days per benefit period.
Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility.
Costs in 2026: $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and the patient pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a follow-up skilled nursing facility stay.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need help with bathing and eating, which suggests ongoing care needs that could involve a family caregiver. The screening doesn't yet know whether someone is providing you unpaid care on a regular basis, which is central to these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care to someone living in Washington.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers free or low-cost short-term respite care and caregiver support services for unpaid caregivers of adults 18+, with service levels tailored through a local assessment; amounts vary by county.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend provides you with regular unpaid caregiving, and how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about respite options in Grant County.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — an 80-year-old with Parkinson's living in Cowlitz County who needs help with toileting, eating, and mobility, has Medicare coverage, and has income and assets above typical program limits — four programs came up as worth a closer look. Two (Medicare home health and Medicaid Community First Choice) look like they may fit fairly well given the level of daily-living help needed, while the other two (Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage and respite care programs) depend on details we don't have yet, like a recent hospital stay or whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. None of this means the family qualifies for any of these programs — each agency makes its own eligibility decision. A few more answers would make this screening more useful, so please see the follow-up questions below.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with mobility, eating, and toileting related to Parkinson's) that suggest a possible skilled or personal care need — all factors this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help walking, bathing, and feeding) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.
It also covers durable medical equipment and medical supplies for home use, with no cap on the number of visits while the person qualifies.
To qualify, the person generally needs a homebound status determination and a face-to-face provider assessment, plus a provider order for services from a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered services cost $0; for covered durable medical equipment, the person pays 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Talk with the person's doctor about whether a homebound status and skilled-care need assessment would be appropriate.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Cowlitz County.
Discuss with the agency what will and won't be covered before care starts.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid option for funding personal care for adults who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, and it requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living to continue at reassessment.
Financially, it requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community (not institutional Medicaid rules).

It covers help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, and medication management, plus things like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, and can be provided in a family member's home.
Applying involves a financial application (unless already on qualifying Medicaid) plus a CARE functional assessment requested through regional Home and Community Services intake.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive inpatient days.
Covered services include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility.
Costs in 2026 include $0 for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100 within a benefit period.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — you're 76, live in your own home in Douglas County, need help with eating, dressing, and toileting after a stroke, are married, and have Medicare — the screening flagged six programs worth learning more about. None of this means you already qualify for anything; each agency makes its own eligibility decision. A few programs (the Medicaid-related ones) still need details about your income, assets, and Medicaid enrollment status before it's clear whether they're a good fit, and two others need information about a recent hospital stay and about whether a family member is providing unpaid care. The Medicare home health benefit looks like a strong match to look into now, since you're enrolled in Medicare and have care needs at home. Talking with the listed agencies directly is the best next step to sort out what actually applies to your situation.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already enrolled in Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and need help with eating, dressing, and toileting following a stroke — all of which line up with what this benefit is designed to help with, assuming a doctor confirms the specific homebound and skilled-care requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled care delivered at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a person generally needs part-time or intermittent skilled care (not more than that), must meet a homebound definition, and must have a face-to-face assessment and certification by a health care provider before services start.

Covered services can include skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and feeding — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.
The benefit does not cover 24-hour care at home, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that is the only care needed.
Before care starts, a doctor performs the face-to-face assessment, orders the care, and the agency must explain in writing what Medicare will and won't pay for.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility and homebound status
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Douglas County
Confirm with the agency in writing what services Medicare will and won't cover for your situation

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 76 and need help with eating, dressing, and toileting after a stroke, which may point to the kind of nursing-facility-level need this waiver looks for, and COPES generally serves people age 65 and older. Because you're married, the program also has special rules meant to protect a spouse's income and resources. We don't yet have your specific income and asset details or your Medicaid enrollment status, so it isn't clear yet whether the financial rules would line up for your household.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's main Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds extra services on top of personal care for people who choose to stay in the community instead of a nursing facility.

To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care — for example, having an unmet or partially met need in several activities of daily living such as eating, toileting, or bathing, or needing skilled nursing supervision.

Financially, the program generally looks at income at or below about $2,982/month and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, but married couples may have added protections that raise the resource allowance (state amount $72,529, federal maximum $162,660 as of the dates listed).

Covered services can include adult day care or adult day health, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing, transportation, environmental modifications, and other supports in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice (CFC) is a Washington Medicaid state plan benefit that pays for personal care for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, administered by Home and Community Services.
Financial eligibility requires being eligible for Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) Medicaid coverage in the community, or already being on SSI-related CN coverage.

Covered services include help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and transferring, plus IADLs such as meal preparation and housework, relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
Because CFC is a state plan option rather than a waiver, it isn't limited by a set number of program slots — it's available to anyone who meets the eligibility rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or would be eligible for, Washington Apple Health Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage
nextSteps: Contact HCS regional intake to request a CARE functional assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 76 with help needed for eating, dressing, and toileting after a stroke, you may fit the age and functional profile this program's financial gateway is built around. As a married couple, there are special income and resource rules that could apply to your household, but we don't yet have your specific income, asset, or Medicaid enrollment details to know how those rules would play out.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's also the financial gateway into HCS waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care and other LTSS programs.
Functional eligibility for HCS clients is based on a CARE assessment finding a nursing facility level of care.

Income eligibility generally looks at income at or below the Special Income Level (about $2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), with a Medically Needy pathway for people above that; resource limits are generally $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with spousal protections that can allow a community spouse to keep more (up to a federal maximum of $162,660 as of 1/1/2026).

To apply, you submit a financial application (such as HCA 18-005) and request a CARE assessment through HCS regional intake; the process includes a financial interview and typically takes up to 45 days to decide.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

To qualify, a person generally needs a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Covered services include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility.
Costs for 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and the full cost after day 100 within a benefit period.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability).

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington, and inability to afford respite independently; FCSP serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ with eligibility and services tailored locally through an assessment.

Lifespan Respite vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging with no statewide cap — for example, some counties allow several thousand dollars over a rolling period depending on local funding and caregiver priority level.
To apply, Lifespan Respite vouchers are requested online, by phone, or by email, while FCSP is accessed through the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, you're a 78-year-old living in Franklin County, WA, in a family member's home, managing Parkinson's disease with help needed for dressing and mobility. You're enrolled in Medicare and have long-term care insurance, with monthly income in the $3,000–$3,999 range and countable assets of $160,000 or more. Four programs came up in this screening: Medicare's home health benefit looks like a strong fit to look into given your Medicare coverage and care needs. The other three — Medicaid Community First Choice, Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit, and Washington's respite care programs — could also be worth a look, but the screening is missing some details (like Medicaid status, the full list of daily activities you need help with, any recent hospital stay, and whether someone provides you unpaid care) that would help clarify whether they fit your situation. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the agencies that run these programs can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare, you live at home (not in a nursing facility) in Franklin County, and you have Parkinson's disease with needs around dressing and mobility — the type of skilled or personal care need this Medicare benefit is designed to support.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while you are also receiving skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy — it does not cover 24-hour care or personal care alone.

To qualify, a provider must certify you as homebound and in need of skilled care after a face-to-face assessment, and the care must be ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to establish homebound status and the need for skilled care
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Parkinson's disease and need help with dressing and mobility, and you live in Franklin County, Washington. However, the screening doesn't yet know whether you are enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid, or exactly how many daily activities you need help with — both of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program pays for personal care help such as bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, medication management, and other daily living tasks for people assessed as needing a nursing-facility level of care.
It can also cover relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and training for choosing and managing caregivers.

To keep receiving this benefit at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in a skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

To qualify, a person needs a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers three main respite channels for unpaid family caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability that generally began before age 18).

Eligibility for Lifespan vouchers generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40 or more hours per week of care, with both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington and unable to afford respite on their own.

The other three (Medicaid Community First Choice, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and Washington respite care programs) each depend on details we don't yet have — such as Medicaid enrollment status, the full list of daily activities needing help, any recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. whatItCovers.text: This is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need another person's help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

It requires meeting the underlying VA pension rules, including a net worth limit of $163,699 (effective December 1, 2025) and at least one day of service during a recognized wartime period, which includes the Vietnam era.
The maximum yearly pension amount with Aid & Attendance for a veteran with no dependents is $29,093, though the actual payment depends on countable income compared to that limit.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact countable net worth, to compare against the VA's $163,699 limit

whatItCovers.text: This is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
It uses the same underlying pension rules as Aid & Attendance, including the $163,699 net worth limit, and a person can only receive one of the two benefits, not both.
The maximum yearly pension amount with Housebound status for a veteran with no dependents is $21,313.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 67, live in your own home in Ferry County, WA, have Parkinson's, need help with dressing, are married, are on Medicare, and have monthly income of $4,000+ and countable assets between $85,000 and $160,000 — one program (Medicare home health) looks like it may fit well with what you've already told us. Several Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) could be worth exploring, but key details are still unknown — especially how many daily activities you need help with, your exact income/asset numbers for Medicaid purposes, and whether you're already enrolled in Medicaid — so it's too early to tell how well those fit. A skilled nursing facility stay under Medicare depends on a recent 3-day hospital stay, which hasn't been mentioned, so that program looks less likely to apply right now. Respite care for a caregiver could be relevant if a family member (such as your spouse) provides regular unpaid care, but that hasn't been confirmed either. Nothing here is an eligibility decision — only the agency that runs each program can determine that. Because several important facts are still unknown, please use the follow-up questions below to gather more detail before treating this as a final picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and need help with a daily activity (dressing) related to your Parkinson's diagnosis — this matches the type of situation this benefit is designed to help with, and the rules engine found all three of its basic checks (Medicare enrollment, an indicated care need, and living at home) to be a match.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, and home health aide help at no cost for the covered services themselves, once a doctor certifies you are homebound and need skilled care.

Home health aide help with things like bathing, dressing, and walking is covered, but only while you're also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy — not for personal care needs alone.

To use this benefit, a health care provider must complete a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and order services from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether your Parkinson's-related needs meet Medicare's 'homebound' and 'skilled care' requirements.
Ask your doctor for a face-to-face assessment and a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Ferry County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67, which meets COPES's age requirement, and you have Parkinson's with a need for help dressing, which is the kind of care need this program looks at. However, we don't yet know how many daily activities you need help with, your exact income and asset split as a married couple, or whether you're on Medicaid — all of which determine whether COPES could apply.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver for people who qualify for nursing-facility level of care but choose to stay in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living; it adds extra services on top of Medicaid's Community First Choice personal care benefit.

To qualify, you generally need to be 65 or older (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), and an assessment (CARE) must show a need for nursing-facility level of care — for example, help with two or more daily activities at certain levels, skilled nursing needs, or cognitive supervision needs.

Financially, income is compared to a Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026), and countable resources are compared to $2,000 for a single person — but married couples have spousal protections that can shelter a much larger amount for the non-applying spouse, up to $162,660 federally as of 1/1/2026.

If eligible, COPES can help pay for services like adult day care/day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, transportation, nursing services, and help moving into a new living situation.

Whether you currently have any Medicaid coverage

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice (CFC) is Washington's main Medicaid program for in-home personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care.

It covers help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and medication management, plus related help like meal preparation, housework, and transportation to medical appointments when ADL help is also needed.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities is required, and eligibility generally requires already qualifying for regular community Medicaid rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're currently enrolled in Medicaid
How many daily activities you need help with, beyond dressing
nextSteps: Contact HCS to request a CARE assessment to check functional eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 67, you meet the age criterion this program looks at, and your Parkinson's-related need for dressing help is the kind of care factor considered. Since this program is the financial gateway to Medicaid long-term-care services like COPES, the same open questions apply — your exact income/asset numbers as a married couple, your living situation, ADL needs, and current Medicaid status all still need to be confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid program that serves as the financial gateway to waiver programs like COPES, as well as nursing facility care.

Eligibility generally requires being 65 or older (or having a qualifying disability), needing nursing-facility level of care per a CARE assessment, and meeting either the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) or a Medically Needy income pathway.

Countable resources are compared to $2,000 for a single person, but spousal impoverishment protections for married couples can shelter significantly more — a state amount of $72,529 and a federal maximum of $162,660 under the dates cited in the program materials.

Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online) plus a required interview and an in-person CARE assessment; the standard processing time is 45 days, or 60 days if a disability decision is needed.

Please treat this as a starting point for a conversation with the actual agencies, not a determination of benefits. The Medicare home health benefit looks like the strongest match right now because your basic Medicare enrollment and at-home living situation are already known. The Medicaid-related programs (COPES, Community First Choice, Apple Health LTC) and respite care could also be worth exploring, but they depend heavily on information we don't have yet. whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology services at home.
It also covers part-time home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming, but only while skilled nursing or therapy services are also being provided.
Covered home health services generally cost $0, though durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To use this benefit, a doctor or qualified provider must first do a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.

whatItCovers.text: This program can add services on top of basic Medicaid personal care, including adult day care, adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.

To be considered, an assessment must show a need at a nursing-facility level of care, which can include needing help with transferring along with other ADLs.
For married applicants, there are special rules that protect some income and resources for the spouse who isn't applying.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at four programs that may be worth exploring for an 82-year-old living alone in Clark County who needs help with dressing, transferring, and toileting and who has Medicare coverage. Two programs (Medicaid Community First Choice and respite care programs) look potentially relevant but some key details — like Medicaid enrollment status and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved — are still unknown and would need to be confirmed. Medicare's home health benefit looks like a strong match based on the information given. Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit is less clearly relevant right now because it depends on a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay, which hasn't been reported.
