programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, live in your own home in Clark County, and have reported needing help with dressing, transferring, and toileting — all factors the rules engine matched against this benefit's basic requirements (enrolled in Medicare, indication of skilled or personal care need, living at home).

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Part-time home health aide help — such as help walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding — can also be covered, but only while you are also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language therapy.

To qualify, a doctor or other provider must certify a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care, confirm homebound status through a face-to-face assessment, and order the care from a Medicare-certified home health agency.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, home meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only kind of care needed.

informationStillNeeded: No specific missing items were flagged, but the agency will still need to confirm homebound status and a doctor's certification of a skilled care need before services can start.
nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment for home health services.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clark County.

whatItCovers.text: This program can help pay for personal care support with activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and medication management, plus related help such as meal prep and shopping when paired with ADL help.
It also can include relief care for caregivers, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and help finding and managing care providers.
Services can be provided in your own home, among other settings, and are administered through Home and Community Services.

To keep receiving this program at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility requires qualifying for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is one requirement for this benefit. whatItCovers.text: When it applies, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical, occupational, and speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit — it is meant for short-term skilled rehabilitation and nursing needs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite for people with a developmental disability that began before age 18.

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for a caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite, adult day services, or other options.

FCSP offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility — plus caregiver support groups, training, and consultation, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.
These programs generally do not stack — Lifespan vouchers exclude people already receiving respite through another publicly funded program, with a limited waitlist exception.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment for home use.

Home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy is also being provided — it is not covered as stand-alone personal care.

To use this benefit, a doctor or other provider must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care after a face-to-face visit, and services must be ordered and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered services cost $0; for covered durable medical equipment, a 20% coinsurance applies after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the treating doctor whether a face-to-face homebound assessment and home health order can be arranged.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Pacific County.
Discuss with the agency what will and won't be covered before care starts, including any equipment costs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program provides Medicaid-funded personal care for adults living in the community who need help with daily activities like bathing and toilet use — needs you described. Because the profile doesn't yet show whether Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage is in place, or the full count of daily activities needing help (three or more are required to continue this benefit), this program is only possibly relevant at this stage.

whatItCovers.text: Covers personal care help with activities of daily living such as bathing, toilet use, dressing, eating, and transferring, plus related supports like relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

To qualify, a CARE assessment must show a nursing-facility level of care, and at reassessment the person must have unmet or partially met needs in at least three ADLs; financial eligibility requires being on community Medicaid (CN or ABP), not institutional Medicaid rules.
Services can be delivered at home or in settings like adult family homes and assisted living, and CFC cannot replace Medicare, other insurance, or informal supports already in place.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
Whether help is needed with three or more daily activities on an ongoing basis, not just bathing and toileting.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask whether a Medicaid financial application is needed, or whether existing Apple Health coverage already qualifies.
Bring information about care needs, diagnoses (including the dementia diagnosis), and current supports to the assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage appears to be in place, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, it's not yet known whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — a required condition for this coverage — so this program is only possibly relevant right now.

whatItCovers.text: Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications/supplies used during a covered stay in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility.

Requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation time doesn't count), admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period: days 1–20 have no daily cost, days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026), and the person pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient, with an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
Ask the hospital or doctor to check Medicare coverage before any SNF admission.
If there was no qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or other long-term care options.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The dementia diagnosis and need for help with bathing and toileting suggest ongoing care or supervision is needed, which is one condition these respite programs look at. It isn't yet known whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing regular care, which is a key eligibility factor for respite support, so this is only possibly relevant at this stage.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers respite support mainly through three channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability that began before age 18).
Lifespan Respite vouchers are generally for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, worth up to $1,000 per household per year, subject to available funding.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver counseling, training, and equipment, with service level set through a TCARE assessment; amounts vary by county Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (family member, friend, or other unpaid person) providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program services in Pacific County.
If there is an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours a week of care, ask about applying for a Lifespan Respite Washington voucher through PAVE.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about a TCARE caregiver assessment to see what respite options and amounts may be available locally.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application been submitted?
Besides bathing and toileting, is help also needed with other daily activities such as dressing, eating, walking, or transferring?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (as an admitted inpatient, not just observation) in the recent past?
Is a family member, friend, or other unpaid person providing regular care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?

It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 74, living in your own home in Franklin County, need help with bathing, have a dementia diagnosis, are a Vietnam-era veteran, are divorced, have monthly income of $4,000 or more, countable assets of $160,000 or more, and are covered by Medicare — six programs came up as worth a closer look. Two (Medicare home health and VA Aid & Attendance) line up fairly well with what you've told us, but several important details are still missing for every program, especially around Medicaid enrollment, exact asset value, how many daily activities you need help with, any recent hospital stay, and whether someone provides you with unpaid care. This screening is educational only — it cannot determine eligibility. Please use the follow-up questions below and take the next steps listed for each program to get a real answer from the agency that runs it.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have an indication of a care need (help with bathing, along with a dementia diagnosis), which matches the basic requirements this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment for people who are homebound and need skilled care, at $0 for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with bathing, grooming, and similar personal care is covered only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language therapy — it is not covered as a stand-alone personal care benefit.
A doctor or qualifying provider must do a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status, and order the care, which is then delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether you meet Medicare's homebound criteria and whether a skilled care need (such as therapy or skilled nursing) exists alongside the need for bathing help.
If so, request a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Franklin County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with bathing, which is one of the personal-care needs this Medicaid program can cover, and you live in Franklin County, Washington. However, the program generally requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities to continue, and it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in (or would qualify financially for) community Medicaid given your reported income and assets.

whatItCovers.text: Covers personal care help with activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, mobility) and, when ADL help is also needed, related household tasks (meal prep, housework, shopping), plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, community transition help, and caregiver management training.

To keep receiving these services at reassessment, a person generally must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and must be financially eligible for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid in the community
How many daily activities you need help with, since three or more may be required to keep this benefit
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake office to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Ask whether your income and assets would fit community Medicaid rules, or whether a different Medicaid pathway (like one with spend-down) might apply.
Bring a list of all the daily activities you need help with to any assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran with a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, who needs help with a daily activity (bathing), you meet several of the underlying pension and clinical requirements this benefit looks at. The main open question is your net worth: the VA's limit is $163,699, and your reported countable assets of $160,000 or more are close to that line, so the exact figure matters a great deal here.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans (or survivors) who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing; the money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.

Underlying pension eligibility requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, at least one day of service during a recognized wartime period (Vietnam era: 8/5/1964–5/7/1975, or earlier for in-country service), and net worth within the VA's limit of $163,699 (effective December 1, 2025).

For 2025–2026, a veteran with no dependents receiving Aid & Attendance could have a Maximum Annual Pension Rate of $29,093/year, with the VA paying the difference between countable income and that rate.

informationStillNeeded: The exact current value of your countable assets, to compare against the VA's $163,699 net worth limit
nextSteps: Gather documentation of your exact asset values and unreimbursed medical/care expenses.
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting your need for help with daily activities.
Consider contacting a Veterans Service Officer (VSO) for free help filing VA Form 21P-527EZ.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime-era service, and discharge history line up with the underlying pension rules this benefit shares with Aid & Attendance. However, it's not yet known whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability (the specific clinical test for this benefit), and your exact net worth relative to the $163,699 limit is also unknown. Note that a person can only receive Housebound or Aid & Attendance, not both — since you seem to meet the Aid & Attendance clinical criterion already, that program may be the more relevant one to explore first.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.
For 2025–2026, a veteran with no dependents receiving the Housebound benefit could have a Maximum Annual Pension Rate of $21,313/year.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability
The exact current value of your countable assets
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits your situation, since only one can be received.
If pursuing this path, have a medical examiner document housebound status on VA Form 21-2680.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a qualifying hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, and it isn't yet known whether you've had such a stay recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

It covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapies, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain ambulance transportation; days 1–20 have no daily charge, days 21–100 carry a coinsurance ($217/day in 2026), and it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days as an inpatient recently
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask the hospital discharge team whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay before assuming SNF coverage applies.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask your doctor about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits for ongoing care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have an ongoing care need related to your dementia diagnosis and need for help with bathing, which fits the general idea behind these programs. It isn't yet known whether you have an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care, which is a key eligibility factor for most of these respite options.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for in-home respite, adult day services, or similar short-term relief, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care who live in Washington and cannot afford respite on their own.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), available through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers free or low-cost short-term care (a few hours to a few days), support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment for unpaid caregivers of adults who need care; amounts vary by county.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family member or friend providing you with regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: If someone provides you with unpaid care, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program through your local Area Agency on Aging.
Ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers if the caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care and needs financial help affording respite.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
How many daily activities — such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around — do you need help with?
What is the current total value of your countable assets (bank accounts, investments, and property other than your home)? A program limit is close to your reported range, so the exact figure matters.
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights in a row recently?
Does a family member or friend provide you with regular unpaid care? If so, about how many hours per week?
Are you substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability, or are you generally able to get out with some help?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 86-year-old widow(er) in Grays Harbor County living in a family member's home, covered by Medicare, diagnosed with dementia, needing help with transferring and eating, and with countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington programs are worth a closer look. However, some important pieces of information are still missing (especially monthly income, current Medicaid enrollment, how many daily activities need help, whether someone is providing unpaid care, and any recent hospital stay). Because these gaps affect several of the programs below, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a final answer — filling in the missing details with an agency worker will make the picture much clearer.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with eating and transferring, plus dementia) that could indicate a need for skilled home care — all of which match this benefit's basic requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time skilled nursing, physical/occupational therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as bathing, walking, and feeding assistance) at no cost for the covered services, as long as the person also needs skilled care and meets the homebound definition.
A health care provider must do a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and order services delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about arranging a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and order home health services.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grays Harbor County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 86 (meeting the 65+ age rule), has countable assets under $2,000 (matching the Medicaid resource limit), and has care needs — help with transferring and eating, plus dementia — that align with the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard this program uses. Monthly income wasn't provided, so the income part of eligibility isn't yet clear.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community services on top of personal care — such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing support — for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.

To meet the functional requirement, an assessment must find a need for nursing-facility-level care, which can include an unmet or partially met need in daily activities like eating or transferring, or cognitive impairment (such as dementia) that requires supervision.

informationStillNeeded: The person's monthly income, to check it against Medicaid's income limits for this program.
nextSteps: Start the two-part application process (financial application plus a CARE functional assessment) through DSHS Home and Community Services.
Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's needs (help with transferring and eating) may relate to CFC's personal care services, but it isn't yet known whether the person is enrolled in community Medicaid or how many daily activities require help, both of which this program depends on.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help such as bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and toileting, plus related supports like caregiver management training and personal emergency response systems, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care.

To keep receiving CFC over time, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and must qualify for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health Medicaid.
How many daily activities, such as bathing, dressing, or transferring, the person needs help with.
nextSteps: Check current Medicaid enrollment status with the Health Care Authority or DSHS.
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to document the person's daily-activity needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 86 years old with care needs suggesting nursing-facility-level care, and countable assets under $2,000, the person's profile lines up with several parts of this program's functional and resource rules. Monthly income wasn't provided, so the income-based eligibility pathway isn't yet clear.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's also the financial gateway to home-based waiver programs such as COPES for people who meet functional and financial rules.

Financial eligibility usually depends on income being at or below the Special Income Level, or qualifying through the Medically Needy pathway if income is higher, along with countable resources generally at or below $2,000 for a single applicant.

informationStillNeeded: The person's monthly income, to see which financial pathway may apply.
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by submitting form HCA 18-005.
Call the regional HCS intake line (numbers vary by county) to request a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit, but there is no information yet about a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay, which this benefit requires before it applies.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period — including skilled nursing, therapy, and related services — but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (not observation status), with an ongoing need for daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay before admission.
Keep records of any hospital admission dates and whether the stay was classified as inpatient or observation.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's age (86) and resource level appear to fit TSOA's rules, and the care needs described suggest a nursing-facility level of care, but TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, and income information hasn't been provided.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits higher than regular Medicaid and no cost-sharing for services.

As of the latest information available, new TSOA applications only join a statewide waitlist rather than being processed for services right away, because enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, with no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: The person's monthly income, to check it against TSOA's income limit.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current statewide waitlist process.
Ask a local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid respite or support options while TSOA enrollment is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person lives in a family member's home and has dementia along with help needs for transferring and eating, which suggests a family member may be providing regular unpaid care — a key factor for respite programs — though this hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Programs such as Lifespan Respite vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offer short-term relief for unpaid caregivers, including in-home respite, adult day care, or short residential stays.
Lifespan Respite vouchers are generally intended for caregivers providing 40 or more hours a week of unpaid care, while FCSP eligibility and services vary by county Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family caregiver is providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite options available in Grays Harbor County.

followUpQuestions: What is the person's approximate monthly income (from Social Security, pensions, etc.)?
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), including SSI-related or Medically Needy coverage?
Besides eating and transferring, does the person need help with other daily activities such as bathing, dressing, toileting, or getting around?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more days as an admitted inpatient recently, with an ongoing need for daily skilled care afterward?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that might be worth exploring for a 70-year-old living alone in Douglas County, WA, who needs help with dressing, has income under $1,000/month, has assets between $2,000 and $85,000, and is covered by Medicare. Because several key details are still missing — such as how many daily activities need help, whether the person is enrolled in community Medicaid, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved — this screening is only a starting point. More information is needed before any of these results can be treated as a solid recommendation. Please review the follow-up questions below and consider connecting with a Home and Community Services (HCS) or Area Agency on Aging (AAA) specialist to fill in the gaps. Nothing here determines eligibility — only the administering agency can do that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, lives at home in Douglas County, and needs help with dressing, which is a personal-care need the home health benefit can address — but only when it's paired with a skilled nursing or therapy need, and only if the person also meets Medicare's homebound criteria.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care and home health aide help (including help with bathing and grooming) at no cost for covered services, but aide help is covered only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language therapy — custodial or personal care alone, with no skilled need, is not covered.

To qualify, a person needs a face-to-face assessment and a doctor's certification of both a skilled-care need and homebound status — meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended because of a health condition.
The process starts with a provider ordering care after a face-to-face visit, then a Medicare-certified agency schedules an appointment and coordinates the care plan with the doctor.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has a skilled nursing or therapy need in addition to help with dressing
Whether the person meets Medicare's homebound criteria (trouble leaving home, or leaving not recommended)
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment for home health services
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Douglas County from the doctor's office

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is over 18, lives in Douglas County, WA, has income under $1,000/month and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — figures that fall within the range CFC discusses for SSI-related coverage — and needs help with dressing, which is one of the ADLs CFC assessments look at. However, it's not yet known whether the person is enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, or whether the total number of ADLs needing help reaches the 3-ADL threshold CFC uses for ongoing eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs including dressing, bathing, eating, and toileting, along with IADLs like meal prep and housework, plus health-related tasks, relief care, nurse delegation, PERS, and caregiver management training.

To qualify, a person generally must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, and must have a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care need; to continue receiving CFC over time, the person must have unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Applying involves a financial application (unless already on qualifying Medicaid) plus a CARE assessment requested through regional HCS intake.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in or can enroll in community Medicaid (CN/ABP coverage)
How many ADLs the person needs help with, to see if it reaches the level CFC assessments consider
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask HCS or a DSHS financial worker about current Medicaid enrollment status and community Medicaid options

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit, but there's no information yet about a recent hospital stay, which is the key trigger for skilled nursing facility coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Days 1–20 have no daily charge, days 21–100 carry a coinsurance ($217/day in 2026), and the person pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, followed by a doctor's order for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage before any SNF admission

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 70 (over the 55+ age requirement), has monthly income under $1,000 (well within the $3,976 limit) and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 (within the $84,354 single resource limit), and based on the profile does not appear to be on Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid — all factors this program's financial rules describe. However, whether the person's care needs meet the nursing-facility level of care standard TSOA requires is not yet known, and the program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person as of 1/1/2026), and normally has no cost-sharing or estate recovery.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments only happen once someone is called from the waitlist.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person's care needs meet the nursing-facility level of care standard TSOA requires
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist and non-Medicaid respite options in the meantime
If interested, submit a TSOA application through Washington Connection to establish a place on the waitlist

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year, but require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, with both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care and caregiver support services, tailored through a TCARE screening with a caregiver specialist, with no statewide dollar cap but amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging.

programs.whyThisMayApply: All we currently know is that you are an adult living in Washington state. COPES is a Washington Medicaid program for people who need nursing-home-level care at home, so it was flagged as worth checking into, but we don't yet know your age, care needs, income, assets, or Medicaid status, which are all needed to see whether this program is a fit.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that helps people who need nursing-facility-level care live at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living instead of a nursing home.
Generally, it is meant for people who are 65 or older, or who are 18+ and meet Social Security's disability criteria, and who need help with daily activities at a level similar to nursing home care.

It can help pay for things like adult day care, home-delivered meals, home safety modifications, transportation, skilled nursing, and help moving into community housing, on top of personal care services.

There are income and asset limits (for example, a $2,982/month income guideline and a $2,000 asset limit for a single person, as of 1/1/2026), and applying involves both a financial application and a care needs assessment through DSHS Home and Community Services.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
How much help you need with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or moving around

Your approximate countable assets/savings
Whether you are married

nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) or apply through washingtonconnection.org to start a conversation about your situation.
Ask about requesting a CARE functional assessment to see what level of care support might be appropriate.
Gather basic financial information (income, assets) in case a financial application is needed.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Based only on knowing you're an adult in Washington, this program was flagged as a general possibility because it's a common Medicaid personal-care benefit in the state. However, we don't know whether you have Medicaid coverage or how much help you need with daily activities, so we can't say more right now.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice (CFC) is Washington's main Medicaid-funded personal care benefit for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, run through Home and Community Services and DDA.
It covers help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, and medication management, plus things like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and help managing caregivers.
To keep receiving it long-term, a person generally needs help with at least three daily activities, and must be eligible for regular community Medicaid coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you currently have Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage
How much help you need with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or eating
nextSteps: Check with Washington Apple Health or HCS about your current Medicaid status.
Ask about a CARE assessment to document daily activity needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is Washington's overall long-term-care Medicaid program and a gateway to several others, so it was flagged as generally relevant to explore. But with only 'adult in Washington state' known, we can't say whether the age, income, asset, or care-need rules would apply to you.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial doorway to programs like COPES, PACE, and community-based Medicaid services.
It covers nursing facility care for people who need that level of care, and opens access to home-and-community-based waivers, PACE, and other community long-term-care options.
Applying involves both a financial application (for example through washingtonconnection.org) and a functional (CARE) assessment arranged through regional HCS intake lines.

How much daily help you need

programs.whyThisMayApply: WA Cares is a state program available to many Washington workers, so it was flagged for review. We don't yet know whether you've contributed to WA Cares through work or how much daily-activity help you need, so we can't say whether this applies to you.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares is Washington's public long-term care insurance program, funded through a payroll premium, offering a lifetime benefit of $36,500 (2026) per person once someone qualifies.
It can pay approved providers directly for things like in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, respite care, and residential care.
To use it, a person generally must have contributed through work for a required period and need help with three or more daily activities, verified through an application and care-needs discussion.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have contributed to WA Cares through payroll deductions, or have an exemption
How much help you need with daily activities
nextSteps: Check your WA Cares contribution status through account.wacaresfund.wa.gov or by calling 844-CARE4WA.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This VA benefit is specifically for veterans, and we don't yet know whether you are a veteran, when you served, your discharge status, your care needs, or your net worth. It's listed here only because the screening tool checks for it generally; nothing in your profile currently points to eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: VA Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly pension payment for veterans (and some survivors) who need help with daily activities, on top of the regular VA pension.

It requires meeting the underlying VA pension rules — including wartime service, discharge status, and net worth limits (currently $163,699) — plus at least one clinical criterion such as needing help with daily activities.
The benefit is a cash payment the household can use for care costs at its discretion; for 2025-2026 rates, a veteran with no dependents could receive up to $29,093/year with Aid & Attendance.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran
When you served (to check wartime service requirements)
Your discharge status
How much help you need with daily activities
Your approximate net worth
nextSteps: If you are a veteran, contact a VA-accredited representative or Veterans Service Officer to discuss pension and Aid & Attendance eligibility.
Gather military service records and financial information if you decide to pursue this.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Like Aid & Attendance, this VA benefit is for veterans, and veteran status, service era, discharge status, and disability details are all unknown. It's included here for completeness, not because anything in your profile suggests it applies.

whatItCovers.text: The VA Housebound benefit is an increased monthly pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.

It requires meeting the same underlying VA pension rules as Aid & Attendance (discharge status, wartime service, net worth limits) plus a specific disability-related housebound criterion, and cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.
For 2025-2026, a veteran with no dependents receiving this benefit could get up to $21,313/year, compared to $17,441 standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran
Your service era
Your discharge status
Whether a permanent disability substantially confines you to your home
Your approximate net worth
nextSteps: If you are a veteran, speak with a Veterans Service Officer about pension and Housebound eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is a Medicare benefit, and we don't yet know whether you have Medicare, what care needs you have, or your living situation. It's listed generally because it's a common resource for people needing skilled care at home, but nothing specific in your profile confirms it applies.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and home health aide help (when combined with skilled care) delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost for the covered services.

To qualify, a person must be homebound, need only part-time or intermittent skilled care, have a face-to-face provider assessment, and have care ordered by a provider and delivered by a certified agency.
It does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only care needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in Medicare
What kind of care or skilled nursing needs you may have
Your current living situation
nextSteps: Check your Medicare enrollment status.
If skilled care needs arise, ask your doctor about a home health referral and face-to-face assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This Medicare benefit depends on Medicare enrollment and a recent qualifying hospital stay, neither of which is known. It's listed as a general possibility, not a specific match, since nothing in your profile points to it yet.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, after a qualifying hospital stay.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medications and supplies used in the facility, with no cost for days 1-20 and a daily coinsurance from day 21-100 ($217/day in 2026).
It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether you've had a recent hospital stay of 3 or more days requiring skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay before admission.
Confirm your current Medicare Part A enrollment status.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: PACE serves older adults who need nursing-home-level care and live in specific Washington counties, but your age, county, and care needs are all unknown, so we can't tell whether this applies.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, coordinated by a care team, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.
It is only available in specific Washington service areas, currently limited to parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.
Covered services include adult day primary care, dental, hospital care, home care, prescription drugs, therapy, transportation, and more, coordinated entirely through the PACE provider network.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your county of residence
How much help you need with daily activities
nextSteps: If you live in King, Pierce, Spokane, or Snohomish County, contact a local PACE organization to ask about your ZIP code's service area.
Ask about a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment if interested in PACE.

programs.whyThisMayApply: TSOA supports older adults with care needs who aren't on regular Medicaid, but your age, income, assets, and care needs are all unknown. Also, this program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a waitlist in place, which is important context regardless of eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is meant for people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid CN/ABP coverage, along with their unpaid family caregivers.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go onto a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed immediately, though current participants keep their services.
Financial limits are higher than regular Medicaid — for example, income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 single (as of 1/1/2026).

Your monthly income

programs.whyThisMayApply: MAC supports unpaid caregivers of people 55+ already on Medicaid, but your age, Medicaid status, living situation, and caregiving situation are all unknown. This program's enrollment is also currently paused statewide with a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports the unpaid family caregivers of people age 55+ who are already on Apple Health Medicaid, meet nursing-facility level of care, and have chosen not to use other Medicaid long-term-care programs.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.
Benefits include caregiver assistance services like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and specialized medical equipment, provided in steps with increasing dollar amounts.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Respite programs support unpaid caregivers of people needing ongoing care, but we don't know whether you need ongoing care or supervision, or whether you have an unpaid caregiver. This is listed as a general resource to consider, not a specific match.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers respite care mainly through Lifespan Respite vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which give unpaid caregivers short-term relief.
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP respite amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, with no statewide cap.
Eligibility generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing regular care (Lifespan Respite typically requires 40+ hours/week) and involves a screening or assessment process.

Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP respite options.

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared, this is an 87-year-old individual in Clallam County, Washington, currently living in a nursing facility, diagnosed with dementia, single, with monthly income under $1,000, countable assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage. These facts line up with several income and asset rules used by Washington's long-term care programs. However, some important details are still missing — especially how much daily help is needed with things like eating, bathing, or moving around, whether there was a recent 3-day or longer hospital stay before the nursing facility admission, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Because those pieces are unknown, this screening should be treated as a starting point rather than a final answer — the follow-up questions below matter a lot for narrowing things down, and none of this determines actual eligibility. Only the administering agency can make that determination.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is 87 (meeting the age requirement), has income under $1,000/month (within the program's income limits), and countable assets under $2,000 (matching the single-person resource limit). The dementia diagnosis could relate to the 'cognitive impairment requiring supervision' path to meeting nursing facility level of care. However, COPES is designed to support people choosing to live in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility instead of a nursing facility, and this person currently lives in a nursing facility — so it's unclear whether this waiver is the right fit without more information about daily care needs and living plans.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care — such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, and skilled nursing — for people living in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility rather than a nursing facility.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find that the person needs nursing facility level of care — which can include needing help with several daily activities, or having cognitive impairment such as memory problems, disorientation, or wandering that requires supervision, plus at least one unmet daily-activity need.
Financially, the program allows income up to $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026) and a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person, matching the SSI standard.

informationStillNeeded: How much help is needed with daily activities like eating, bathing, or moving around (this affects whether the 'nursing facility level of care' requirement is met)
Details about the current or planned living situation (private home, adult family home, assisted living, or continuing in the nursing facility)
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services to request a CARE assessment through the regional intake line for Clallam County (Region 3: 800-786-3799)
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office
Ask a caseworker or elder-law advisor whether COPES or nursing-facility Medicaid coverage better fits the current living situation

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is 87, currently living in a nursing facility in Clallam County, WA, with monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000. Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid program specifically for paying for nursing facility stays, and the reported income and assets appear to fall within the program's financial limits, which makes this worth a closer look given the current living situation.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care for people who meet nursing facility level of care, including 24-hour supervised nursing care, personal care, therapy, nutrition management, activities, social services, room, board, and laundry.
It also serves as the financial gateway to other services, including HCS home-and-community-based waivers (like COPES), PACE, hospice in a medical institution, and community LTSS.

Financially, the SSI-related pathway allows income up to the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (1/1/2026), with a lower Medically Needy Income Level pathway ($994/month) for people with higher income after deductions; there is also a resource test tied to SSI standards.

informationStillNeeded: How much help is needed with daily activities like eating, bathing, or moving around, to help confirm nursing facility level of care
Confirmation of the current living arrangement and whether the nursing facility stay is expected to continue long-term
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or submit the paper HCA 18-005 application by mail, fax, or in person at an HCS office
Call HCS central intake for Clallam County (Region 3: 800-786-3799) to schedule the required CARE assessment
Be ready for a financial interview with an HCS financial specialist as part of the process

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, Medicare SNF coverage is a short-term benefit (up to 100 days per benefit period) that requires a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days plus a doctor's order for daily skilled care — and it's unknown whether that applies here. Since dementia care needs are often long-term and custodial rather than short-term skilled rehabilitation, this program may not be a strong match, but it's worth checking given the Medicare coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days; it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Covered services can include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and medical supplies used in the facility, with cost-sharing that increases after day 20 and ends Medicare coverage after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there was a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient right before the nursing facility stay
Whether a doctor ordered daily skilled nursing or therapy care for an ongoing or new condition
nextSteps: Ask the hospital or nursing facility's admissions/business office whether the stay followed a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital admission
Ask the treating doctor whether daily skilled care was formally ordered
If this benefit doesn't apply, ask about Medicaid (Apple Health LTC) or other long-term care coverage options instead

programs.whyThisMayApply: Respite programs are designed to support unpaid family caregivers of someone who needs regular care. This profile doesn't indicate whether an unpaid caregiver is currently providing care, and since the person is described as single and currently living in a nursing facility, it's unclear whether there is an active family caregiver who would benefit from respite support right now.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through channels such as Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability).
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year toward services like in-home respite or adult day services for caregivers providing 40 or more hours per week of unpaid care.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility, plus support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with amounts varying by county and Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family member or friend is currently providing regular caregiving support, and if so, how many hours per week
How much daily help this person needs, since that affects whether a caregiver is providing the type of care these programs support
nextSteps: If there is a family caregiver involved, contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org to ask about local Area Agency on Aging respite options
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) and its TCARE screening process

followUpQuestions: How much help does this person need with daily activities such as eating, bathing, toileting, transferring, or moving around, and is there any confusion, memory loss, or wandering that requires supervision?

Was there a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (not just observation) right before the nursing facility stay, and did a doctor order daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward?
Is the current nursing facility stay meant to be short-term rehabilitation or a longer-term stay?
Is any family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving support, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: Thanks for sharing these details about your mom. Based on what you've told us — she's 83, lives in Clallam County in a family member's home, has Parkinson's, needs help with toileting and mobility, is covered by Medicare, and has income and assets in the ranges you gave — this screening identified five programs worth learning more about. Please note: this tool cannot confirm that your mom 'qualifies' for any program. Only the agency that runs each program can make an eligibility determination, and several key facts (like whether she's enrolled in Washington Medicaid, how many daily activities she needs help with, whether she's had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone is providing unpaid caregiving) are still unknown. Because those pieces are missing, treat this as a starting point for conversations with the agencies below, not a confirmation of benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is covered by Medicare, and her documented needs — help with toileting and mobility, along with a Parkinson's diagnosis — suggest she may benefit from skilled or personal care support at home, which is what this Medicare benefit is designed to cover.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled care — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To use this benefit, a person must need part-time/intermittent skilled care, be considered homebound, have a face-to-face provider assessment, and receive care ordered by a provider through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask her doctor whether she meets Medicare's 'homebound' definition and whether a face-to-face assessment and home health order could be arranged.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County from her provider.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is 83 (over the program's age-55 minimum), her monthly income and assets fall within the program's current limits, and her needs with toileting and mobility point toward the nursing-facility level of care this program is designed for. Importantly, this program is currently on a statewide waitlist, so even strong-seeming matches go through a first-come, first-served process rather than immediate enrollment.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using income and resource limits that are higher than regular Medicaid.

As of the most recent update, income limits are $3,976/month and resource limits are $84,354 (single) or $156,883 (married with a community spouse); functional eligibility requires a nursing-facility level of care determination.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional assessments happen only when called from the waitlist.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by submitting form HCA 18-008 to establish a waitlist request date as soon as possible, since the program is paused and positions are ordered by request date.

While waiting, ask the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is 83 and living in Clallam County, needs help with toileting and mobility, and has Parkinson's — these are the kinds of personal-care needs this program is built around. However, whether she currently has community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage and whether she needs help with 3 or more daily activities (ADLs) are both unknown, and both are required to continue this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: CFC provides personal care help with activities like toilet use, bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility/transfers, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, delivered in a home or community setting.

To qualify, a person generally must be found to need nursing-facility level of care and be eligible for community Medicaid (CN or ABP) rather than institutional Medicaid rules; to keep receiving CFC at reassessment, the person must have unmet or partially met needs in at least three ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your mom is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage.
How many daily activities (ADLs) she needs help with, since ongoing CFC generally requires help with at least three.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment and find out her current Medicaid status.
Ask HCS whether a financial application for community Medicaid is needed, or whether she may already qualify through existing coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay followed by a need for daily skilled care, and it's currently unknown whether she has had such a stay recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, medications, and supplies during a covered skilled nursing facility stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to a Medicare-certified SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether she has had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has determined she needs daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF purposes.
If there's no recent hospital stay, this program likely isn't the right avenue right now — consider asking about Medicare home health or Medicaid options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom's ongoing care needs — help with toileting and mobility due to Parkinson's — suggest she may need regular supervision or care, which is the kind of situation these respite programs are meant to support. However, it's currently unknown whether an unpaid family caregiver is providing her regular care, which determines which respite option might fit.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, through contracted in-home respite, adult day services, or similar options, for unpaid caregivers providing significant regular care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility — plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling, with service levels tailored through a caregiver assessment; amounts vary by county/Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing regular care or supervision for her, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging serving Clallam County to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options and to arrange a caregiver assessment.

followUpQuestions: Is your mom currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), either Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) coverage?
Besides toileting and mobility, does she need help with any other daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, eating, or transferring)? Roughly how many total?
Has she had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days in the recent past, followed by a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is there a family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving for her, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Is she homebound, meaning she has trouble leaving home without help or leaving is not medically recommended?

Several key facts are still missing — especially how many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, or eating) require help, whether Apple Health/Medicaid coverage is already in place, whether a recent hospital stay occurred, and whether a spouse or other family member is providing regular unpaid care. Because these functional and coverage details are unknown for most programs, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a conclusion — a benefits specialist or the administering agency (such as DSHS Home and Community Services) can confirm what actually applies. programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has a care need (help with toileting, plus a dementia diagnosis) that could involve skilled or personal care. These match the basic Medicare home health criteria checked by the rules engine, though a doctor still needs to confirm homebound status and a skilled care need before services can start.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and home health aide care (such as help with bathing and walking) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended) and have a face-to-face assessment and provider order confirming the need for skilled care from a Medicare-certified agency.
This benefit does not cover ongoing custodial or personal care alone, 24-hour care, or homemaker services unrelated to the care plan.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 74 (meets the 65+ age rule), has a dementia diagnosis, needs help with toileting, and reports monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000 — figures that fall within COPES' income and resource rules. whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, specialized equipment, transportation, and help transitioning to a new living situation.

To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find nursing-facility-level care needs — for example, cognitive impairment causing disorientation, memory problems, or wandering, combined with at least one unmet or partially met ADL need (such as toileting), or unmet needs in multiple ADLs.

Financially, COPES generally requires income at or below $2,982/month and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person (or $3,000 for a couple), which appears consistent with the income and assets reported here.

Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient responsibility beyond day 100 in a benefit period.

report.overallSummary: Right now we only know a few basic facts about this situation: the person lives in their own home in Okanogan County, Washington, and has countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000. We don't yet know the person's age, whether they need help with daily activities like bathing or dressing, their monthly income, whether they're enrolled in Medicare or Medicaid, their marital status, or whether a family member helps care for them without pay. Because so much key information is missing, this screening can only point to programs that *might* be worth a closer look — it cannot say whether this person actually qualifies for any of them. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening much more useful. An eligibility determination can only come from the agency that runs each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person lives in their own home in Washington State (Okanogan County) with countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000, which is in the general range this Medicaid waiver considers, but we don't yet know age, whether daily-activity help is needed, monthly income, or the exact asset amount — all of which determine whether this waiver could apply.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that serves people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living facilities.

On top of personal care, it can add services such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and help transitioning into a new home setting.

To be considered, a person generally must be 65 or older (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care, and meet income and resource limits — for example, resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with some exceptions for spouses.

Complete the Apple Health long-term care financial application (HCA 18-005) if not already on file.
Confirm current income and asset amounts against the program's published limits with an HCS financial specialist.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person lives in Washington State and is in their own home, which is one of the settings this program serves, but whether they are already enrolled in community Medicaid and whether they need help with three or more daily activities — both required pieces — are unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice provides personal care help with daily activities (bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, medication management) and related tasks like meal prep and light housework, plus services such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

It's a Medicaid State Plan benefit for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, and it generally requires being eligible for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in or eligible for Apple Health (Medicaid) community coverage

nextSteps: Ask HCS or the local Area Agency on Aging about requesting a CARE functional assessment.
Check current Medicaid enrollment status through Washington Connection or HCA.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the financial gateway program behind several Medicaid long-term-care waivers, and the household's countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall in a range that could matter for it, but age, income, care needs, and marital status are all unknown, so it's unclear whether the person would meet the underlying rules.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for nursing facility care and the financial gateway to home-and-community waivers like COPES, run jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

It generally requires meeting income limits (such as the Special Income Level, $2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and resource limits ($2,000 for a single person, with higher amounts protected for a spouse), along with a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment.

Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview, and a separate in-person CARE assessment of care needs; the standard processing time is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports the unpaid family caregivers of people age 55+ who are already on Apple Health CN/ABP Medicaid, meet nursing-facility level of care, and live in their own or another person's home rather than a licensed facility.

Benefits are delivered in steps — a one-time $250 amount, then up to $500/year, then a higher recurring amount — and can pay for things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, home-delivered meals, training, and counseling for the caregiver.
As with TSOA, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date; current participants keep their services.
