report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared — an 80-year-old in Snohomish County who needs help with mobility, has heart disease, is married, has Medicare, income between $1,000–$2,999/month, and countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington programs could be worth a closer look. However, some key details are still missing, like exactly how much help is needed with daily activities (bathing, dressing, transferring, etc.), whether a formal care-level assessment has happened, the specific monthly income figure, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver involved. This screening is educational only — it cannot determine eligibility for any program. The agencies that run these programs make that determination. Filling in the missing details below would make this screening more useful, and a conversation with Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) or Community Living Connections would help sort out real options.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare, needs help with mobility, has a heart disease diagnosis, and lives at home rather than in a nursing facility — all of which align with what this Medicare benefit is designed to address.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical and occupational therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy) at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To use this benefit, a doctor must certify — after a face-to-face visit — that the person is homebound and needs part-time skilled care, and the care must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor whether a home health referral and homebound certification would be appropriate given the mobility and heart-related needs
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Snohomish County

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 80, which meets the age requirement, and reported countable assets under $2,000 match the resource limit this program uses. Whether the mobility-help needs described add up to the required 'nursing facility level of care,' and whether the specific income amount fits within program rules, isn't yet known from what's been shared.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services — like adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation — on top of basic personal care, for people assessed as needing that level of care who choose to stay in the community instead of a nursing facility.
It's Washington's largest program of this kind, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and can be used in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To be considered, an assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care, and financial rules apply, including a $2,000 individual asset limit and an income test tied to a $2,982/month threshold (2026 figure).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a Washington adult who has had a stroke, is covered by Medicare, and has countable assets under $2,000. Those facts line up with the resource limits used by several Medicaid long-term-care programs and confirm Medicare enrollment for Medicare-based programs. However, many important details are still unknown — age, how many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, or eating) require help, current living situation, monthly income, county of residence, whether Medicaid is already in place, work/payroll history for WA Cares, whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved, and whether there was a recent 3+ day hospital stay. Because so many of the underlying rule questions are unanswered, this report should be treated as a starting point for gathering more information rather than a set of recommendations. Please review the follow-up questions below with the family, then consider speaking with the agencies noted for each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family reports countable assets under $2,000, which matches this program's SSI-standard resource limit for a single person. The stroke diagnosis suggests the person may need help with daily activities, which is one of the areas this program looks at — but the screening does not yet know the person's age, how many daily activities they need help with, their income, or their living situation, all of which affect whether the other requirements might apply.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds services on top of personal care for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.

Covered services can include adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, transportation, and help transitioning into the community.

To be considered, an applicant generally needs to be 65 or older, or 18+ with a qualifying disability, and a CARE assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care need; financial rules include an income test (Special Income Level) and the same $2,000 single resource limit noted above.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
How many daily activities the person needs help with
The person's current living situation (own home, adult family home, assisted living, etc.)
The person's approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Ask HCS or an Area Agency on Aging to walk through the income and functional-need questions this program uses.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The stroke diagnosis and Medicare coverage are noted, but this program's main requirements — being enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid and needing help with three or more daily activities — are both unknown for this family right now.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and medication management, plus related supports such as relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

It is a Medicaid state plan option (not a capped waiver) run through Home and Community Services, generally requiring enrollment in community Medicaid (CN/ABP) and a CARE assessment finding a nursing-facility level of care need.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE assessment through HCS central intake if not already scheduled.
Ask HCS whether a financial application is needed or whether only the functional assessment applies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported countable assets under $2,000 match this program's SSI-related resource limit, and this program serves as the financial gateway to several other services mentioned in this report. Age, functional need, living situation, and income are still unknown, so it isn't yet clear whether the other eligibility pieces line up.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial pathway into HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, and other community LTSS.
Eligibility involves both a financial application and interview and a functional CARE assessment; both are determined together, with services starting once both are established.
Financial rules include an income test (Special Income Level, with a Medically Needy option above that) and the $2,000 single resource limit noted above, plus a 60-month look-back on asset transfers.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
How many daily activities the person needs help with
The person's current living situation
The person's approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit paper form HCA 18-005.
Complete the required phone or in-person interview with an HCS financial specialist.
Request the CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: placeholder

The stroke diagnosis could relate to needing help with daily activities, which is part of this program's care-need trigger, but it isn't known whether the person has paid into WA Cares through work, and the number of daily activities needing help with is also unknown.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares provides a lifetime benefit (up to $36,500 as of 2026) that pays approved providers directly for services such as in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, and respite care.

To draw on the benefit, a worker generally needs to meet a contribution (vesting) requirement and have a care-need trigger of needing help with 3 or more daily activities expected to last at least 90 days.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has contributed to WA Cares through work (and has no exemption)
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Create a SecureAccess Washington account and check WA Cares contribution status, or call 844-CARE4WA.
If contribution history is unclear, gather work history records for verification.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is confirmed, which meets one requirement outright. A stroke can sometimes lead to a need for skilled nursing or therapy at home, but the screening does not yet know whether a provider has certified a skilled or personal care need, or whether the person lives at home versus a facility.

whatItCovers.text: This Medicare benefit covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (when paired with skilled care), plus related durable medical equipment, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.
It requires homebound status and a face-to-face provider assessment before services can be certified.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a provider has identified a skilled or personal care need
Whether the person currently lives at home (rather than in a nursing facility)
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment for home health certification.
Get a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving the area from the provider.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is confirmed, but this benefit depends on a recent qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, which is not something this screening has information about.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

Covered items include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in the facility, with cost-sharing that increases after day 20.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there was a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, with a doctor's order for daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: Check hospital discharge paperwork for a documented 3-day+ inpatient (not observation) stay.
Ask the hospital discharge team whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay in this case.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The stroke diagnosis suggests a possible need for coordinated nursing-home-level care in the community, but PACE is only available in certain Washington counties, and the person's age, county, and daily-activity needs are all currently unknown.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, plus additional services the care team decides are needed, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community, including home care, personal care, therapy, transportation, and prescription drugs.
Washington PACE eligibility requires being 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, needing a nursing-home level of care, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE support.
PACE coverage in Washington is limited to specific ZIP codes within King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
The county (and ZIP code) where the person lives
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Check whether the home ZIP code is within a PACE service area by contacting Providence PACE, ICHS PACE, or PNW PACE Partners directly.
If in a covered area, ask about the nursing-facility-level-of-care certification process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported assets are well under this program's resource limit, which is a good sign for that specific piece, but age, income, and daily-activity needs are unknown, and — importantly — TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist rather than being processed immediately.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, and also supports their unpaid family caregivers.

As of the most recent information, enrollment is paused statewide; new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional review happens once called from that list.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
The person's approximate monthly income
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: If interested, submit an application (HCA 18-008 or via washingtonconnection.org) to establish a place on the waitlist.
While waiting, ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: A stroke diagnosis can sometimes mean a family member is providing regular unpaid care, but this screening does not yet know whether there is an unpaid caregiver involved or how many daily activities the person needs help with, both of which these programs use to determine fit.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs in Washington include Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which offers planned short-term care along with caregiver training, support groups, and counseling; a separate DDA respite track applies only when the care receiver has a developmental disability.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington, inability to afford respite independently, and not already receiving respite through another public program.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss FCSP options.
If a family caregiver is providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers via wapave.org or 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: What is the person's age?

How many daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or moving around) does the person need help with, and how much help (supervision, some assistance, or full assistance)?
Where does the person currently live — their own home, an adult family home, assisted living, or a nursing facility?
What is the person's approximate monthly income?
What county in Washington does the person live in?
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, which program?
Has the person worked in Washington and paid into the WA Cares payroll program, or do they have an approved exemption?
Is there an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has the person had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an inpatient recently, and did a doctor order daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far — an adult in Kittitas County, Washington, living in a family member's home, needing help with toileting and eating, and covered by Medicare — several Washington and federal programs are worth a closer look. However, many of the details these programs use to sort people (age, monthly income, countable assets, marital status, current Medicaid enrollment, and veteran status) are still unknown, so this screening is only a starting point. Because so much information is missing, please treat every program below as something to explore with the administering agency, not as a program you're confirmed for. The follow-up questions below would help sharpen this picture considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile confirms Medicare coverage and a need for help with daily activities (toileting and eating), and the person lives at home rather than in a facility — all three things this benefit's screening checks passed.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, at $0 for beneficiaries for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with activities like walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language pathology — custodial or personal care alone is not covered.

Eligibility requires a face-to-face assessment and provider certification of homebound status and a skilled-care need, plus care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor or health care provider about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned the person needs help with toileting and eating — two of the activities of daily living Washington's CARE assessment considers when deciding whether someone needs nursing-facility level of care, and the screening's ADL-based check already came back as a possible match. COPES serves people living in a private residence (which could include a family member's home), an adult family home, or assisted living. What's still unclear is the person's age, income, assets, marital status, and whether they're already enrolled in Medicaid — all of which determine whether COPES fits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services such as adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation on top of personal care, for people living in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

Eligibility generally requires being 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability or age 65+, and a CARE assessment finding nursing-facility level of care, which can be met by having an unmet or partially met need in multiple activities of daily living such as toileting and eating.

Financially, COPES generally requires income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with spousal protections if married.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Monthly income bracket
Countable assets bracket
Marital status
Whether the person is already enrolled in Medicaid
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office to start the two-track review.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for help with toileting and eating fits the kind of personal-care assistance CFC covers, but continuing CFC requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs — right now we only know of two. We also don't know if the person is enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid, which is required for CFC.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs — including toilet use and eating — as well as IADLs, relief care, personal emergency response systems, and other supports, in settings including someone's own home.
CFC requires being 18+, meeting a nursing-facility level of care assessment, and being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
The total number of activities of daily living the person needs help with
nextSteps: Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake to evaluate ADL needs.
Check current Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment status, or apply if not yet enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES. The person's need for help with toileting and eating aligns with the kind of ADL-based functional test this program uses, and that check already came back as a possible match in the screening. Key financial details — age, income, assets, marital status, and current Medicaid status — are still unknown, and those determine which specific pathway (if any) would apply.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (including COPES), PACE, and other community LTSS, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Financial eligibility generally looks at income against the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or a Medically Needy pathway, and resources against a $2,000 single-person limit, with spousal impoverishment protections for married applicants.

Application involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online) plus a CARE functional assessment arranged through regional HCS intake; processing standard is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Monthly income bracket
Countable assets bracket
Marital status
Current Medicaid enrollment status
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit form HCA 18-005.
Call regional HCS central intake to request the required CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: WA Cares pays for care when someone needs help with 3 or more ADLs; the profile currently shows two (toileting and eating). We also don't know whether the person contributed to WA Cares through work, which is required to receive a benefit. Both of these gaps make it hard to say how relevant this program is right now.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares provides a lifetime benefit (currently $36,500, indexed annually) covering services such as in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, adaptive equipment, home modifications, transportation to medical appointments, and residential care, paid directly to approved providers.
A family member can potentially become a paid caregiver under the program, and respite is available to give a paid or unpaid family caregiver a break.

To receive benefits, a worker generally needs to meet a contribution (vesting) requirement and have a care-need trigger of needing help with 3 or more of seven counted ADLs, expected to last at least 90 days.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person contributed to WA Cares through work (or has an exemption)
How many total activities of daily living the person needs help with
nextSteps: Contact WA Cares at 844-CARE4WA or create an account at account.wacaresfund.wa.gov to check contribution status.
Ask about a care-needs discussion/assessment to review the full list of ADLs needing help.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for help with daily activities like toileting and eating matches the kind of clinical need Aid & Attendance looks for, and that part of the screening already passed. However, we don't yet know if the person is a veteran, when they served, their discharge type, or their net worth — all of which are required for the underlying VA pension this benefit is added to.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly pension amount for veterans (or some survivors) who need help from another person with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

It pays the difference between countable income and an annual Maximum Annual Pension Rate set by Congress, delivered as a monthly cash payment that can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.
The underlying pension requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, income and net worth within limits ($163,699 as of 12/1/2025), and specific wartime service requirements.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a veteran
Dates and era of military service
Discharge type
Countable assets/net worth bracket
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status, service dates, and discharge type.
If the person is a veteran, contact a Veterans Service Officer or the VA to discuss the pension and Aid & Attendance.

programs.whyThisMayApply: We don't have enough information to say whether this benefit fits — veteran status is unknown, and the profile doesn't indicate whether the person is substantially confined to their home, which is the specific clinical test this benefit uses (living in a family member's home does not by itself establish that).

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly pension amount for veterans substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, paid as a monthly cash addition to the VA pension.

It relies on the same underlying VA pension eligibility as Aid & Attendance — discharge status, income and net worth limits, and wartime service requirements — plus a clinical finding of being substantially confined to the home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a veteran
Dates and era of military service
Discharge type
Whether the person is substantially confined to home
Countable assets/net worth bracket
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status, service dates, and discharge type.
Discuss with a doctor or Veterans Service Officer whether the person's daily activity level would meet the 'substantially confined to home' standard.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is on Medicare Part A, but this benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — we don't know whether such a hospital stay has happened or is planned.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, including room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and medical social services, following a qualifying hospital stay.

It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has had (or is expected to have) a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with a daily skilled-care need
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it meets the 3-day qualifying stay requirement for SNF coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for help with toileting and eating fits the kind of nursing-facility-level-of-care standard this program checks, and the screening's related checks (ADL proxy, and not currently on CN/ABP Medicaid) already came back as possible matches. However, TSOA's income, age, and asset tests are unknown for this person, and — importantly — the program is currently on a statewide waitlist rather than open enrollment.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic CN/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits set higher than regular Medicaid, and also serves eligible individuals with no unpaid caregiver.

Financial standards (as of 1/1/2026) allow gross income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 for a single person or $156,883 for a married person with a community spouse, with no client cost-sharing and no estate recovery.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments happen only once someone is called from the waitlist; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Monthly income bracket
Countable assets bracket
Marital status
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by mailing form HCA 18-008 to be placed on the statewide waitlist, understanding there is no announced reopening date for new services.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The care receiver's ongoing need for help with toileting and eating fits the general idea behind respite programs, which passed in the screening. But these programs are built around supporting an unpaid family caregiver, and we don't yet know whether someone in the family member's home is providing that unpaid care on a regular basis.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for respite delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options), available to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) plus caregiver counseling, training, and support groups for unpaid caregivers of adults 18+, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite voucher eligibility.

followUpQuestions: What is the person's age?
What is the person's approximate monthly income?
What is the approximate value of countable assets (savings, investments, etc.), not counting the primary home?
Is the person married, and if so, does a spouse live in the home?
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) in any category?
Has the person ever served in the U.S. military? If so, when, and what was the discharge type?
Besides toileting and eating, does the person need help with other daily activities (bathing, dressing, transferring, walking, medication management, bed mobility)?
Is there a family member or friend who provides unpaid care on a regular basis, and if so, roughly how many hours a week?
Has the person had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more days recently, or is one expected soon?
Is the care need expected to continue for at least 90 days?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a 71-year-old Washington resident who needs help with bathing, transferring, and mobility, has a dementia diagnosis, and has Medicare coverage. Those facts line up with the functional (care-need) requirements for several programs — but many of the financial, veteran-status, county, and living-situation details needed to narrow the list are still missing. Because so many rule checks came back 'unknown,' this report leans heavily on the follow-up questions below. Nothing here is an eligibility decision — each agency listed would need to review the actual application to confirm eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes a 71-year-old needing help with bathing, transferring, and mobility, plus a dementia diagnosis — these are exactly the kinds of needs COPES' functional test looks at, including its specific allowance for cognitive impairment paired with an unmet ADL need [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. The rules engine found the age and functional checks pass, but income and asset information wasn't provided, so the financial side of eligibility could not be checked.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care for people who qualify for nursing-facility-level care but want to stay in the community.

It can cover things like adult day health, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

One path to the required 'nursing facility level of care' finding is cognitive impairment (such as dementia-related disorientation or memory impairment) combined with an unmet or partially met ADL need — relevant given the dementia diagnosis and reported ADL needs.

Financially, one path requires income at or below $2,982/month (2026 figure) or, if higher, meeting a medically-needy spend-down; countable assets generally must be at or below $2,000 for a single person, with higher protections for a spouse.

informationStillNeeded: Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets
Marital status
Whether Medicaid (Apple Health) is already in place
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask a benefits counselor to review income and asset details against COPES' financial rules

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's reported needs — help with bathing, transferring, and mobility — map onto the ADL categories CFC is built around, and the rules engine confirmed a substantial ADL need. Whether the person is already enrolled in, or can enroll in, community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is unknown and is central to this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is Washington's main Medicaid personal-care benefit for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, covering help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, transfer, mobility, medication management, etc.) and related IADLs.

It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, in settings like one's own home, adult family home, or assisted living.
Financial eligibility requires qualifying for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community (not institutional financial rules), or SSI/SSI-related coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in, or would qualify for, community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
nextSteps: If not already on Apple Health, submit a Medicaid application; if already enrolled, request a CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake
Ask HCS whether CFC alone or CFC combined with COPES fits the described needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the financial gateway program behind COPES, CFC, and PACE. Given the person's age (71) and reported care needs, the functional side looks promising, but income, assets, marital status, and current Medicaid status were not provided, so the financial gateway itself can't be assessed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, covering nursing facility care and serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (COPES, New Freedom, Residential Support), PACE, hospice in an institution, and community LTSS like CFC.

Eligibility requires meeting nursing-facility-level-of-care through a CARE assessment, plus either SSI-related income limits (at or below $2,982/month in 2026) or a medically-needy spend-down pathway.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online) plus a phone/in-person interview and an in-person CARE assessment; processing standard is 45–60 days.

informationStillNeeded: Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets
Marital status
Whether Medicaid (Apple Health) is already in place
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Request a CARE assessment through the regional HCS intake number for the applicant's area

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported need for help with bathing, transferring, and mobility matches the A&A clinical criterion of needing another person's help with daily activities. However, whether the person is a veteran, served during a recognized wartime period, had a qualifying discharge, and is within the net worth limit are all unknown — and these are foundational to this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans (or some survivors) who need help with daily activities, and it can be used at the household's discretion for care costs.

To qualify, a person must first meet the underlying VA pension rules — non-dishonorable discharge, service during a recognized wartime period, and income/net-worth limits (net worth limit $163,699 as of December 1, 2025) — and then meet at least one A&A clinical criterion, such as needing help with bathing, feeding, or dressing.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a veteran
Service era/dates
Type of discharge
Approximate net worth (assets)
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and discharge details, ideally with military service records (DD-214 if available)
If a veteran, contact a Veterans Service Officer or the VA to discuss a pension/A&A application
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 if pursuing this benefit

programs.whyThisMayApply: The rules engine found that the clinical 'substantially confined to home' criterion passed based on the profile, but veteran status, service era, discharge type, and net worth — all required for the underlying VA pension — are unknown.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability.

It requires first qualifying for the underlying VA pension (discharge status, wartime service, and income/net worth limits, with the net worth limit at $163,699 as of December 1, 2025), the same as Aid & Attendance, plus meeting the housebound clinical or regulatory definition.
A person can receive either Aid & Attendance or Housebound, not both at the same time.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a veteran
Service era/dates
Type of discharge
Approximate net worth (assets)
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and discharge details
If a veteran, discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance fits better
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is covered by Medicare and has care needs consistent with skilled or personal-care services, both of which passed the rules engine's check. Whether the person currently lives at home (as opposed to a nursing facility) — a requirement for this benefit — was not provided.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only alongside skilled nursing or therapy) delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost for the covered services.

To qualify, the person must be homebound (leaving home is difficult or not recommended) and need part-time/intermittent skilled services certified after a face-to-face provider assessment; it does not cover 24-hour care or custodial personal care alone.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person currently lives at home, in an adult family home, or elsewhere
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor whether a face-to-face homebound assessment and home health order would be appropriate
If eligible, the doctor can provide a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving the area

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A, which passed the rules engine's check, but there is no information about a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay, which is a required trigger for this specific benefit. Without that hospital stay, this program is unlikely to be currently relevant.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.
Covered costs include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and supplies, for up to 100 days per benefit period, with coinsurance starting on day 21.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospitalization occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether the stay qualifies for SNF coverage before any nursing facility admission
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, consider other programs on this list instead

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's age (71) and reported functional needs align with PACE's age and nursing-facility-level-of-care requirements, both of which passed the rules engine's check. However, PACE is only available in specific Washington counties, and the person's county was not provided.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community, with services provided exclusively through the PACE provider's network.

In Washington, PACE is only available in parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, through three contracted organizations (Providence ElderPlase, ICHS PACE, and PNW PACE Partners); coverage within a county is not complete, so a specific ZIP code needs to be checked.

informationStillNeeded: County (and ideally ZIP code) of residence, to check whether it falls in a PACE service area
nextSteps: Check the person's ZIP code against the DSHS PACE coverage area list or call a PACE organization's intake line directly
If in a covered area, contact the local PACE organization to begin the functional and financial eligibility process

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's age and described care needs align with TSOA's functional requirements, which passed the rules engine's check, and TSOA does not require being on classic Medicaid. However, income and asset information were not provided, and — importantly — TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic (CN/ABP) Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid.

As of the program's current status, enrollment is paused; new applications only place a person on a statewide, first-come-first-served waitlist rather than starting services immediately, though current participants keep their benefits.
Financial limits (2026 figures) are income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 single / $156,883 married with a community spouse.

informationStillNeeded: Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets
Marital status
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds in the meantime, since TSOA intake is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's dementia diagnosis and need for help with bathing, transferring, and mobility suggest ongoing supervision needs, which passed the rules engine's check. Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — the key requirement for these programs — was not stated.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), among other channels.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver to live in Washington, and the household to be unable to afford respite on its own; FCSP eligibility and services vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are assessed through a caregiver specialist.

nextSteps: If there is a family or friend caregiver, contact the local Area Agency on Aging (via Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite vouchers

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a Washington adult who needs help with toileting, dressing, and eating, has been diagnosed with heart disease, and has countable assets under $2,000. Because several key facts are still unknown — including age, monthly income, Medicare/Medicaid enrollment, county of residence, living situation, whether there's a family caregiver, and WA Cares payroll contribution history — this screening cannot yet tell which programs are most likely to fit. The notes below explain what each program generally covers and what would need to be confirmed with the family and the administering agency before going further. More information is needed before this screening is fully useful — please see the follow-up questions.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs help with toileting, dressing, and eating, and has countable assets under $2,000 — both facts line up with two of COPES's screening factors (nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment and the $2,000 single resource limit). Age and monthly income are not yet known, and both are required pieces for this program.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care (CFC), including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and specialized medical equipment, in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To move forward, an assessment looks at whether the person has an unmet or partially met need in 3 or more ADLs — including eating and toileting — at certain assistance levels, among other ways to show a nursing-facility level of care.
COPES also has a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person, matching the SSI standard.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age (COPES generally requires age 65+, or 18-64 with a disability determination under SSI criteria)
The person's approximate monthly income, to check it against the program's income standards
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment
Discuss age and income details with an HCS financial specialist to see how they compare to program standards

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for help with toileting, dressing, and eating matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs. Whether the person is already enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is not yet known, and that is central to this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as dressing, eating, personal hygiene, and toilet use, along with things like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
CFC financial eligibility is based on being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, not institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health enrollment status
If not enrolled, ask about applying for community Medicaid alongside a CARE functional assessment
Contact regional HCS intake to request the CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Countable assets under $2,000 match the SSI-related resource limit used for this program, and the described care needs (toileting, dressing, eating) line up with the nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment used for functional eligibility. Age and income are not yet known, and this program's financial pathway depends heavily on both.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is the Medicaid coverage for nursing facility care and the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, as well as PACE, hospice in an institution, and other community LTSS.

Financial eligibility uses either the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or a Medically Needy pathway for people with higher income, alongside a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person.
Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview, and a separate in-person CARE functional assessment by an HCS social worker.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
The person's approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or by paper form HCA 18-005
Schedule the required financial interview with an HCS financial specialist
Request the CARE functional assessment through regional HCS intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: duplicate-placeholder

Needing help with toileting, dressing, and eating (three activities of daily living) matches the WA Cares care-need trigger. This is a Washington-based program, which fits the person's state of residence. Whether the person has contributed to WA Cares through work, or holds an exemption, is not yet known and is required to establish any benefit.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares offers a lifetime benefit of $36,500 (2026), paid directly to approved providers, covering services like in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, professional nursing, and respite care.
The care-need trigger is needing help with 3 or more activities of daily living — including bathing, eating, toileting, and transferring — expected to last at least 90 days.
Family members can potentially become paid caregivers under the program.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has contributed to WA Cares through work (and for how long), or has an approved exemption
nextSteps: Create a SecureAccess Washington account and check contribution status at account.wacaresfund.wa.gov
Call 844-CARE4WA to check vesting status and start a care-needs discussion

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described need for help with toileting, dressing, and eating suggests a personal-care need, but this Medicare benefit is built around a skilled-care need (such as nursing or therapy) plus homebound status, not personal care alone. Medicare enrollment and current living situation are both unknown, so it's unclear whether this program's specific requirements are relevant here.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide care — but aide care is only covered while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT.
The benefit does not cover custodial or personal care when that is the only care needed, and requires homebound status plus a face-to-face provider assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare
Current living situation (own home vs. facility) and whether a skilled care need (not just personal care) has been identified by a provider
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare enrollment status
Ask a physician or nurse practitioner whether a skilled home health need exists alongside the personal care needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: The heart disease diagnosis could relate to hospital care in some circumstances, but nothing in the profile confirms a recent qualifying hospital stay, and this benefit is for short-term skilled rehabilitation after such a stay rather than ongoing help with daily activities. Medicare Part A enrollment and any recent hospitalization are both unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services, with cost-sharing that increases after day 20 and ends after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare Part A enrollment
If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The described care needs (toileting, dressing, eating) match the kind of assessment PACE uses to identify a nursing-home level of care. Age and county of residence are both unknown, and PACE only operates in specific Washington counties.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community, through an interdisciplinary team.
PACE is only available in certain zip codes across four Washington counties: King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish.
Financial eligibility can run through either home/community waiver rules or institutional Medicaid rules, and the state must certify a nursing-facility level of care.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Countable assets under $2,000 fall well within TSOA's higher resource limits, and the described ADL needs match the kind of nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment TSOA uses. However, TSOA's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist, and age, income, and current Medicaid enrollment are all unknown.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Categorically Needy/ABP Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, though people already enrolled or approved before that date keep their services.
Financial standards allow income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 for a single person (as of 1/1/2026).

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described ADL needs match the kind of nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment MAC uses, but MAC requires the care receiver already be enrolled in CN/ABP Medicaid, living at home, and to have an identified unpaid caregiver age 18+ — none of which are yet known. MAC enrollment is also paused statewide with a waitlist in effect.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports the unpaid family caregivers of people 55+ who are already on Apple Health Medicaid, meet nursing-facility level of care, and have chosen not to use other Medicaid LTSS.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.
Covered categories include caregiver assistance services like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals, at benefit levels that grow across three steps.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Whether the person is currently enrolled in CN/ABP Medicaid
Current living situation (own home vs. licensed facility)
Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver age 18 or older providing regular care
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or HCS office to ask about the MAC waitlist
Confirm current Apple Health enrollment status

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described ongoing need for help with toileting, dressing, and eating matches the general idea of needing regular care or supervision that respite programs are meant to support around. Whether there is currently an unpaid caregiver providing that regular care is not yet known, and most respite pathways are built around supporting such a caregiver.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers respite through Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (planned short-term care from hours to days, plus counseling and training), and DDA waiver respite for people with a developmental disability.
Lifespan Respite vouchers require the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40+ hours per week of care or supervision and not already receiving respite from another publicly funded program.
The Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with service levels tailored through a TCARE screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite options
If there is a caregiver providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, consider applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher

report.overallSummary: This screening only has a few facts to work with so far: the person lives in Washington State, has a Parkinson's diagnosis, is single, and has countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000. That's a good start, but almost every program below also depends on things this screening doesn't yet know — things like age, county, veteran status, how much help is needed with daily activities, current Medicaid or Medicare enrollment, income, living situation, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Because so much is still unknown, this report leans heavily on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. None of the programs listed here have been checked against actual eligibility rules by an agency — they are only starting points worth a closer look once more details are gathered.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This screening knows the person lives in Washington and has a Parkinson's diagnosis, which can involve needing help with daily activities — the kind of need this program addresses. However, whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, and how many daily activities they need help with, are both unknown right now, so it isn't yet clear whether this program fits.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, moving around, and medication management, plus related help like meal preparation or housework when personal care is also needed.
It also covers things like relief care (short breaks for caregivers), personal emergency response systems, and training for choosing and managing personal care providers.

To use this program, a person generally needs to already qualify for regular community Medicaid coverage, and an assessment must show a nursing-facility level of care need, including an ongoing need for help in at least three daily activities to continue receiving services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in, or able to enroll in, community Medicaid coverage.
How many daily activities the person needs help with.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to ask about requesting a CARE functional assessment.
Ask a Medicaid caseworker whether the person currently qualifies for or is enrolled in community Medicaid (Apple Health).

programs.whyThisMayApply: The rules engine found that this person's reported assets fall within the VA pension net worth limit ($163,699). However, this screening does not yet know whether the person served in the U.S. military, during what period, under what type of discharge, or how much help they need with daily activities — all of which determine whether this program could apply.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans (and some survivors) who need help with daily activities — it is not a service program, and the money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.

To qualify for the underlying pension, a veteran generally needs a discharge that was not dishonorable, income and net worth within set limits, and a minimum period of service including at least one day during a recognized wartime period.

On top of the pension, at least one Aid & Attendance clinical criterion must apply, such as needing help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing, or being largely confined to bed due to illness.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a veteran of the U.S. military.
Their period of military service (to check against recognized wartime periods).
Their discharge type.
How much help they need with daily activities.
nextSteps: Check military discharge papers (DD-214) to confirm service dates and discharge type.
Contact the VA, an accredited Veterans Service Organization, or a VA-accredited claims agent to discuss the underlying pension and Aid & Attendance criteria.
Have a doctor familiar with the person's Parkinson's-related care needs available to complete VA Form 21-2680 if an application moves forward.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The rules engine found that this person's reported assets fall within the VA pension net worth limit ($163,699). However, veteran status, period of service, discharge type, and whether the person is substantially confined to home by a permanent disability are all unknown, so it isn't yet clear whether this benefit could apply.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.

The underlying pension eligibility — discharge status, income and net worth limits, wartime service requirements, and age/disability criteria — is the same as for VA Aid & Attendance, and a person cannot receive both Housebound and Aid & Attendance at the same time.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a veteran of the U.S. military.
Their period of military service.
Their discharge type.
Whether the person is substantially confined to home by a permanent disability.
nextSteps: Check military discharge papers (DD-214) to confirm service dates and discharge type.
Contact the VA or an accredited Veterans Service Organization to discuss pension eligibility and whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance criteria might fit better.

programs.whyThisMayApply: A Parkinson's diagnosis can sometimes involve a need for skilled nursing or therapy care at home, which is what this benefit covers. However, this screening does not yet know whether the person is enrolled in Medicare, what their care needs look like, or their living situation, so it's unclear yet whether this fits.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology services at home, plus medical social services and durable medical equipment.
Home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, speech, or occupational therapy.
To qualify, a person generally needs Medicare, a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care (not full-time care), and homebound status certified by a health care provider.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare.
Whether there is an indication of a skilled or personal care need.
Where the person currently lives.
nextSteps: Check current Medicare enrollment status (Part A and/or Part B).
Ask a doctor whether a face-to-face assessment for home health services would be appropriate given Parkinson's-related care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program covers short-term skilled nursing facility care after a hospital stay. This screening doesn't yet know whether the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A or has had a recent qualifying hospital stay, so it's unclear whether this fits the current situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in a skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

To qualify, a person generally needs Medicare Part A with days remaining in the benefit period, a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A.
Whether there has been a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay with a daily skilled-care need.
nextSteps: Check Medicare Part A enrollment status.
If there is an upcoming or recent hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify as a 3-day inpatient stay for skilled nursing facility coverage.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: PACE serves people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community, which could be relevant given a Parkinson's diagnosis. However, this screening doesn't yet know the person's age, county of residence, or level of care need, all of which determine whether a PACE center actually serves the area and whether the level-of-care requirement would be met.

whatItCovers.text: PACE covers all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care plus anything the interdisciplinary care team determines is needed, including home care, personal care services, therapy, prescription drugs, nursing home care if needed, and transportation to the PACE center and medical appointments.

Eligibility generally requires being age 55 or older, living in the service area of a Washington PACE organization, needing a nursing-home level of care as certified by the state, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help.
In Washington, PACE service areas as of a 2026 review covered parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties only, through three contracted organizations.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age.
The county (and ZIP code) where they live.
How much help they need with daily activities or their overall level of care need.
nextSteps: Check whether the home address falls within a PACE service area (King, Pierce, Spokane, or Snohomish County) using the DSHS PACE ZIP code list.
Contact a Washington PACE organization intake line (Providence PACE, ICHS PACE, or PNW PACE Partners) to ask about the assessment process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The rules engine found that this person's reported assets fall within TSOA's resource limit for a single person ($84,354). However, this screening doesn't yet know the person's age, monthly income, current Medicaid enrollment, or level of care need, so it isn't clear yet whether the other eligibility pieces line up. It's also important to know that TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 or older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above standard Medicaid amounts (income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person, as of the dates cited).
People already receiving Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Apple Health coverage are not eligible for TSOA under WAC 182-513-1615.

Enrollment in TSOA has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served statewide waitlist rather than being processed immediately, and there is no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age.
Their approximate monthly income.
How much help they need with daily activities (to assess nursing-facility level of care).
Whether they are currently enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current TSOA waitlist process.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds, which don't depend on the paused TSOA waitlist, if that type of support is relevant.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program supports unpaid family caregivers of Medicaid enrollees who need nursing-facility level care. This screening doesn't yet know the person's age, Medicaid enrollment status, living situation, level of care need, or whether there is an unpaid family caregiver involved, so it's unclear whether this fits.

whatItCovers.text: MAC provides caregiver support benefits in steps: a one-time $250 payment, then up to $500/year, then a higher monthly benefit (roughly $928/month up to $5,568 over six months as of the 2025–2026 rate period), covering things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals for the caregiver's benefit.

To qualify, the care receiver generally must be age 55 or older, live in their own home or another person's home, already be enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Apple Health, meet nursing facility level of care, and have an unpaid caregiver age 18 or older.
MAC enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist, the same pause affecting TSOA.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age.
Whether they are currently enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.
Their living situation (own home, another person's home, or a licensed facility).
How much help they need with daily activities.
Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver age 18 or older providing regular care.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or HCS office to ask about the current MAC waitlist.
Confirm current Medicaid (Apple Health) enrollment status with a caseworker.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Respite programs support unpaid caregivers of people who need ongoing care or supervision. This screening doesn't yet know whether the person needs that level of ongoing care, or whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, so it's unclear whether this fits right now.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far — you are 74, live in Washington state, have a Parkinson's disease diagnosis, and are widowed — a rules engine flagged eight programs that might be worth a closer look. However, almost all of these programs need more details (like your income, assets, living situation, daily care needs, county, and whether you have Medicare or Medicaid) before anyone can say whether they're a good fit. This screening is a starting point, not a determination — only the agency running each program can decide if you qualify. Because so much information is still missing, please treat the follow-up questions below as the most useful next step, and use the notes on each program as things to ask a caseworker or benefits counselor about.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 74, you meet the age condition most people need for this program (65 or older) [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. Parkinson's disease can involve increasing day-to-day care needs over time, which is part of why this program showed up in the screening. We don't yet know how much help you need with daily activities, your income, your assets, or whether you're on Medicaid — all of which are needed before this program's fit can be checked.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of basic personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, transportation, environmental modifications, and help with moving into the community.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be 65 or older (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care, and meet income and asset limits (the 2026 special income level is $2,982/month and the asset limit is $2,000 for a single person).
Services can be provided in a private home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — needing help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, a dementia diagnosis, living in Washington state, and a monthly income under $1,000 — several state and federal programs may be worth a closer look. However, many of the details that actually determine eligibility (your age, veteran status, county, current Medicare/Medicaid enrollment, savings and other assets, marital status, living situation, and whether you have an unpaid caregiver) are still unknown. Because so much information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a conclusion — please answer the follow-up questions below or discuss them with a caseworker so the picture can be sharpened. None of the programs listed here have been determined to apply to you; only the administering agency can make that call.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, along with a dementia diagnosis — the kind of functional and cognitive needs COPES looks at when checking for nursing-facility level of care. Your reported income (under $1,000/month) is below the program's at-home income limit. But we don't yet know your age, whether you have a qualifying SSI-criteria disability determination, your countable assets, or your marital status, all of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds home-and-community-based waiver services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.

To qualify, an assessment must find a need for nursing-facility level of care — which can include unmet needs in daily activities like transferring or dressing, or cognitive impairment (such as memory problems) combined with an unmet care need.
For people receiving care at home, income up to $2,982/month is allowed, and countable resources generally must be at or below $2,000 for a single applicant.

informationStillNeeded: Your age

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, bed mobility, walking, and medication management, delivered in a private home or other community settings.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility requires Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers, PACE, and other community LTSS programs, and covers nursing facility care.

Income up to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month, 1/1/2026) is allowed under the SSI-related pathway, with a Medically Needy option for income above that; resources generally must be at or below $2,000 for a single applicant.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far — that you live in Washington state, are widowed, have a monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999, and have countable assets under $2,000 — a number of long-term care programs are worth a closer look. However, most of the details that actually determine whether these programs fit your situation are still unknown, including your age, whether you need help with daily activities like bathing or dressing (and how many), your current living situation, veteran status, and whether you have Medicare or Medicaid. Because so much key information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point rather than a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below — ideally with help from a Washington Area Agency on Aging or the Home and Community Services intake line — will make this screening much more useful. None of the programs listed here have been determined to apply to you; only the administering agency can make that determination.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've told us you live in Washington, are widowed, and have countable assets under $2,000 — which matches COPES's $2,000 single-person resource limit. Your monthly income ($1,000–$2,999) may also fall within the program's income rules, but we don't yet know your age, whether you need help with daily activities at a nursing-facility level, or your living situation, all of which this program depends on.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, and skilled nursing services, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.
It's run by DSHS Home and Community Services and serves people who need nursing-facility level care but choose to stay in the community.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be 65 or older or have a qualifying disability, need substantial help with daily activities at a nursing-facility level, and have income and assets within certain limits (the single asset limit is $2,000).
