report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — needing help with mobility, eating, and bathing, being divorced, and having countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington and federal programs could be worth a closer look. However, important details are still missing: age, monthly income, whether Medicaid or Medicare coverage is already in place, county of residence, veteran status, living situation, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver. Because so many of these key facts are unknown, this screening should be treated as a starting point rather than a conclusion. Answering the follow-up questions below — and speaking with the agencies that run these programs — will give a much clearer picture of what might actually be available. No program listed here has been confirmed as something the family qualifies for; eligibility can only be determined by the agency that runs each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for help with mobility, eating, and bathing lines up with the kind of assistance-with-daily-activities criteria this waiver looks at, and countable assets under $2,000 matches the resource limit for a single applicant. Age and monthly income are not yet known, and both are needed before this can be evaluated further.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers extra services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, nursing services, and specialized medical equipment and supplies, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.
It is meant for people whose assessed care needs reach a nursing-facility level of care but who choose to receive services in the community instead of a nursing facility.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or at an HCS office
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask DSHS Home and Community Services directly whether current age and income would fit within program limits

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described need for help with mobility, eating, and bathing suggests a substantial need across several activities of daily living, which is the kind of need this program looks at. Whether there is already Medicaid coverage in place — which this program's financial pathway depends on — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, along with related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, nurse delegation, and caregiver management training.
It's available to people whose assessed needs reach a nursing-facility level of care and who are enrolled in (or can enroll in) regular community Medicaid.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
nextSteps: If not already on Apple Health, submit a financial application; if already enrolled, only the functional CARE assessment is needed
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake
Ask HCS whether current Medicaid enrollment status supports this pathway

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described care needs and assets under $2,000 align with parts of this program's functional and resource criteria, which also serves as the financial gateway to other long-term care programs. Age and income are still unknown and are central to determining the applicable income pathway.

whatItCovers.text: This is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports — it serves as the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and institutional nursing facility care.
Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview and an in-person CARE functional assessment, with financial and functional eligibility reviewed together.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or by paper form HCA 18-005
Complete the required interview with an HCS financial specialist
Request the CARE assessment through the regional HCS intake line for your area

The described need for help with mobility, eating, and bathing suggests a need across multiple daily activities, which is the kind of care-need trigger this program looks at. Whether there has been qualifying payroll contribution (or an exemption) is not yet known, and that's a required piece for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares pays a lifetime benefit (currently $36,500, indexed annually) directly to approved providers for services such as in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, and respite care.

To draw on the benefit, a worker generally needs a qualifying history of payroll contributions and an assessed need for help with 3 or more activities of daily living expected to last at least 90 days.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have contributed to WA Cares through work, or have an approved exemption
nextSteps: Create a WA Cares Benefits Account online or call 844-CARE4WA to check contribution history
If contribution history qualifies, request the care-needs assessment for the 3+ ADL requirement
Contact a local Area Agency on Aging for in-person help with the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described need for help with daily activities like bathing and eating matches the kind of clinical need this benefit looks at, and assets under $2,000 would likely fall within the net worth limit. However, veteran status, service dates, and discharge type are not yet known, and these are foundational requirements for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to an underlying VA pension for veterans (or some survivors) who need another person's help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.
The underlying VA pension has its own requirements around discharge status, wartime service, and income/net worth limits.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran
What period you served in, if applicable
Your discharge status, if applicable
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and discharge paperwork (DD-214) if applicable
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to discuss the underlying pension and Aid & Attendance
Gather military service dates to check against recognized wartime periods

programs.whyThisMayApply: Assets under $2,000 would likely fall within the net worth limit for the underlying VA pension, but veteran status and the 'substantially confined to home' clinical criterion are not yet established from what's been shared, and this benefit cannot be paid alongside VA Aid & Attendance.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly cash payment to an underlying VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran
What period you served in, if applicable
Your discharge status, if applicable
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and discharge paperwork if applicable
Discuss with the VA whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance is the better fit
Contact a Veterans Service Officer for help with the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described need for help with bathing and eating could relate to home health aide services, but this benefit requires being homebound and needing skilled care alongside aide help — and whether there is Medicare coverage and what the current living situation is are both unknown.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy, and home health aide help (such as with bathing, grooming, and feeding) at no cost for covered services, but only for people who are homebound and need skilled care — not full-time or custodial-only care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in Medicare
Your current living situation
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare enrollment status
Ask a doctor or care provider whether a homebound and skilled-care assessment would apply
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies in the area if a referral is made

programs.whyThisMayApply: There is no information yet about Medicare enrollment or a recent qualifying hospital stay, both of which are required for this short-term skilled nursing facility benefit, so it's unclear whether this would apply to the described situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified facility after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare Part A enrollment
Check with a hospital discharge planner about whether a recent stay would qualify
Ask about alternatives like Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits if no qualifying stay exists

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The described care needs could relate to the nursing-facility level of care criterion this program looks at, but age and county of residence are unknown, and PACE is only available in a limited number of Washington counties.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.
Washington PACE organizations currently serve only King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, and not every ZIP code within those counties is covered.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
The Washington county you live in
nextSteps: Check whether your ZIP code falls within a PACE service area

whatItCovers.text: TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date; people already enrolled keep their services.

When open, it supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility level care but are not on regular categorically needy or alternative benefit plan Medicaid, using income and resource limits well above typical Medicaid levels.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Ongoing care needs like the ones described often go along with respite programs for caregivers, but whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care — which these programs are built around — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers, including Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to about $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program, which offers planned short-term care plus support groups, training, and equipment.
These programs are generally built around supporting an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, rather than paying for the care recipient's services directly.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's shared so far — a 91-year-old living at home in Washington who needs help with dressing, mobility, and eating, has heart disease, monthly income under $1,000, and countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look with an agency. However, a number of important details are still missing (exact asset amount, Medicaid and Medicare enrollment status, county of residence, marital status, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved), so this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a conclusion about what the family will receive. Nothing here is an eligibility determination — only the administering agencies (DSHS/HCS, HCA, Medicare, or Area Agencies on Aging) can decide that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 91 years old and needing help with dressing, mobility, and eating, this profile matches COPES's functional focus on people who need nursing-facility-level care. Monthly income under $1,000 is well within the program's income limit for this pathway. The countable asset bracket given ($2,000–$85,000) means it isn't yet clear whether assets fall within or above the program's resource limit.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that layers additional home and community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.

It's intended for people 65 or older, or with a qualifying disability, who need nursing-facility-level care (as measured by a CARE assessment) but choose to receive services in the community instead of a nursing facility.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount, to see if it's at or below the program's resource limit (generally $2,000 for a single person)
Marital status
Whether currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE (functional) assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Gather documentation on income, assets, and any transfers or gifts from the past 5 years

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with dressing, mobility, and eating suggests a substantial need across multiple daily activities, which lines up with CFC's focus on ongoing personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care. Whether this pathway applies depends on being enrolled in regular community Medicaid (CN or ABP), which hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, plus supports like personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

It's meant for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, and financial eligibility generally requires having regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether currently enrolled in Apple Health Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), or would need to apply
nextSteps: Apply for Apple Health Medicaid if not already enrolled
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to establish functional eligibility

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 91 with help needed for dressing, mobility, and eating, and income under $1,000/month, this profile fits the functional and income criteria this program looks at. It also serves as the financial gateway to several of the other programs on this list. The exact asset amount and Medicaid enrollment status still need to be confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care covers nursing facility care for people who meet that level of need, and also serves as the financial gateway to home-based options such as COPES, PACE, and community personal care programs.
To qualify financially, available income generally must be at or below $2,982 a month, with a Medically Needy pathway available for people with somewhat higher income.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount
Marital status
Whether currently enrolled in Apple Health Medicaid
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Request a CARE assessment through HCS regional intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with mobility, eating, and dressing while living at home, combined with a heart disease diagnosis, could point to a need for skilled or supportive care at home. This program requires Medicare enrollment and a doctor's certification of homebound status and skilled care need, neither of which is confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help — but aide help only comes alongside skilled nursing or therapy — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a doctor must certify through a face-to-face assessment that the person is homebound and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether enrolled in Medicare
Whether a doctor has assessed a need for skilled home health care and homebound status
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare enrollment status
Talk with the primary care provider about whether a face-to-face home health assessment makes sense

programs.whyThisMayApply: There isn't currently information about a recent hospital stay, which is a required starting point for this benefit. The heart disease diagnosis and care needs mentioned don't by themselves indicate this program applies — more information would be needed to know if it's relevant.

whatItCovers.text: Covers up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care — including nursing, therapy, room, board, and medications — but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

informationStillNeeded: Whether enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare Part A enrollment
If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 91 and needing help with dressing, mobility, and eating fit PACE's focus on people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community. Whether this program is available depends on living in one of the specific counties PACE currently serves, which hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: PACE combines all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through one coordinated team for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.
In Washington, PACE is only available in parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, and coverage isn't complete within each county.

informationStillNeeded: The county (and ideally ZIP code) of residence, to check whether it falls within a PACE service area
nextSteps: Contact a Washington PACE organization's intake line to check ZIP code coverage
If in a covered area, pursue the state functional and financial eligibility review

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 91 with help needed for dressing, mobility, and eating, income under $1,000/month, and assets in the $2,000–$85,000 range appear to fit within TSOA's higher income and resource limits. However, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, and it isn't yet known whether Medicaid enrollment status would rule this program out.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether currently enrolled in Apple Health Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid
Status of any waitlist placement, given the current enrollment pause
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the TSOA waitlist
Confirm current Medicaid enrollment status

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 91, living at home, and needing help with dressing, mobility, and eating fit MAC's focus on people who need nursing-facility-level care and support their unpaid caregiver. This program requires already being enrolled in Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid and having an identified unpaid caregiver, both of which are unknown, and enrollment is currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: MAC benefits support an unpaid family caregiver in steps — a one-time $250 amount, up to $500 a year at the next step, and roughly $928 a month (up to $5,568 over six months) at the highest step — plus services like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals.
MAC enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Whether currently enrolled in Apple Health Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid
Whether there is an unpaid caregiver age 18 or older providing regular care
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging/Community Living Connections to ask about the MAC waitlist
Confirm current Medicaid enrollment status

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing ongoing help with dressing, mobility, and eating suggests a need for regular care or supervision, which is the starting point for respite programs. Whether a specific respite option applies depends on whether there's an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers who provide 40 or more hours of care per week and can't afford respite on their own.

The Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term respite care and other caregiver supports (like counseling and training), with specific amounts and rules varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide it
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options

followUpQuestions: What is the exact countable asset amount? The bracket given ($2,000–$85,000) is wide and matters a lot, since some programs require assets at or below $2,000 while others allow much higher amounts.
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) — and if so, is it Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan coverage?
Is the person enrolled in Medicare Part A and/or Part B?
What county does the person live in? (This matters especially for PACE, which is only available in parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.)
Is there an unpaid family caregiver (age 18 or older) helping regularly, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
What is the person's marital status? (This affects resource limits for some Medicaid long-term care programs.)
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently?
